```
On Thu, 13 Sep 2007 12:16:21 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
>news:c8ZFi.377367$Bo7.200313@fe07.news.easynews.com...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> attain any credibility here :-)

There are words for that

malapert: One who likes to shock with saucy, brasen, impudent statements
ultracrepidarian: One who tries to work outside his field of knowledge,
    where he has no business being.

And there are words for the other side

catagelophobe: A thin-skinned person overly sensitive to criticism.
logomachist: One who constantly asks for definitions of words.
mome: One who loves to use others' petty mistakes as an excuse to put
    them down. A hair-splitter.

>> However, his "sweeping" statements (without qualification) are so common 
>> that they do remove most credibiltiy from his posts on most subjects (IN 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>think that's valuable.

You've put your finger on it. In the world of corporate IT, where most Cobolers live,
Standards are the Holy Writ. Criticism of those practices is an insult to the company's
wisdom. Standard is supposed to mean consensus. Believers don't see the irony in using the
word for beliefs that are different in every company, sometimes between departments in the
same company. It's like telling religious folks that 100 conflicting texts can't all be
right. They get angry because you even raised the question.

>> Whether it is poersonality or writing style - or something else - no 
>> attempst that I or anyone else that I remember could make to help him 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>then everybody wins.

If it turns into a flamefest, everyone loses.

>> All I think that is worth saying is that he is back and appears (to me) to 
>> be none the wiser on how to post.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>didn't.)

It's hard to believe I said that. If that had been my intention, I would have come on as a
troll, because that's how newsgroups are destroyed. If you don't believe me, ask the
wrecking crew in alt.2600.

>It never happened, and, along the way, Robert changed his mind about that. 
>For people in this group to change their minds about anything is pretty rare 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>the form, rather than the content that upsets most people.)

Bummer. I want them worked up about the content. Any fool can be personally offensive;
that's easy.

>On the negative side, he appears to have little or no respect for people 
>here (that may be because no one here has earned his respect; or it just may 
>be that he doesn't respect people in general, and this group isnt being 
>singled out for any particular treatment :-)), and he posts in a manner that 
>tends to alienate people rather than persuade them.

I have a lot of respect for SOME people I've worked with in real life. Unfortunately, none
of them have been mainframe applications programmers (except the shops I managed). The
team members I work with now, about 20 people, are excellent. Sadly, I'm the only
American, the rest are from Russia, India and <gulp> Arab countries. 

I sometimes ponder what would happen if the situation were reversed, if we lived in third
world countries, had to become fluent in another language, abandon our families and live
in an exotic culture. Most of us wouldn't be strong enough. There's some natural selection
going on here; we get the best .. at least the most adventuresome.

>He is not a stupid man and is capable of modifying his behaviour, just like 
>all of us.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>> many of his opinions on this topic are NOT generally accepted.

Thanks, Pete, for the defense. I was tempted to rebut that the ones who agree with me
abandoned Cobol in the '90s, and went on to fame and fortune with other languages. Like
the Chief Computer Scientist at a very well known search engine company who advised me to
completely delete Cobol from my resume else I'd never get a job in Silicon Valley. He said
Cobol programmers are in laughing stock in places that do real programming. 

With your erudite (as always) writing, I don't have to go down that road now. :)

>We all learned many years ago how pointless it is to have arguments about 
>coding style here, but we still do it :-)

How sad that style debates about programming style are futile here. I thought that was the
purpuse of programming forums. I'll do a little recon in the forums of other languages,
where I expect to find many code samples. 

>Although it is important to all of us, it is still only computer programming 

What do you mean ONLY?
```

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:rn4he3ptrgaaq3olgl1uvp4j1shi7b3aqa@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 13 Sep 2007 12:16:21 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> malapert: One who likes to shock with saucy, brasen, impudent statements

Never heard that one; like it :-)

> ultracrepidarian: One who tries to work outside his field of knowledge,
>    where he has no business being.

Never heard that one either... definitely a keeper... :-)
>
> And there are words for the other side
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    them down. A hair-splitter.

English is a wonderful language.

>
>>> However, his "sweeping" statements (without qualification) are so common
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> right. They get angry because you even raised the question.

I think it is not the fact that you raised the question, Robert. It is the 
MANNER in which you raised it; form rather than content.

Consider a case where you see something going on and you know it is stupid, 
but they've been doing it for years, and "everybody knows" it is "right"...

Before rocking the boat it is wise to consider WHY the boat needs rocking, 
and WHY you need to be the one to rock it.

Only if your motives are pure (no putting people down, or scoring off them, 
or showing how smart you are and how dumb THEY are... in other words, no ego 
involved) and you genuinely want to see improvement, should you even 
consider rocking the boat at all.

Given that you do, which of the following is most likely to achieve change?:

1. "What's the MATTER with you dummies?! Can't you see that..."
2."You guys are SO lucky to have an expert like me around to fix things for 
you. Here's what you need to do..."
3."I have an idea I'd like to open for discussion with you all. Everybody OK 
to have a look at this? Now, it seems to me..."

In all three scenarios, the solution will probably be the same one you 
realised in a heartbeat before you spoke, but it has much more chance of 
being adopted if the last approach is used.

I have had some experience at rocking corporate boats and have made serious 
money at it. I can think of at least three occasions where I simply planted 
ideas in someone's mind and let them take the credit for the solution. No 
resentment or ego-bruising on my part; I was laughing all the way to the 
Bank :-) (I might add that I've seen the converse happen too, where I 
learned from them.)

Having said all of that, nobody should be required to have a personality 
transplant just so their ideas become acceptable. Neither should people have 
to walk around on eggshells for fear of offending.

Grown-ups should be able to say what they think and be judged as much on 
their motivation as on their action (if you really have to judge people at 
all...I try not to.)

>
>>> Whether it is poersonality or writing style - or something else - no
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> If it turns into a flamefest, everyone loses.

Exactly.
>
>>> All I think that is worth saying is that he is back and appears (to me) 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's hard to believe I said that.

It was a long time ago. I only mentioned it because Bill felt that you 
hadn't changed, or learned anything.

>If that had been my intention, I would have come on as a
> troll, because that's how newsgroups are destroyed.

Well, actually, you did... for a while.:-) When you found your posts were 
not being responded to, you calmed down a bit.

>If you don't believe me, ask the wrecking crew in alt.2600.

Oh, I believe you... :-)


>
>>It never happened, and, along the way, Robert changed his mind about that.

>>For people in this group to change their minds about anything is pretty 
>>rare
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> that's easy.

Well, there's a challenge that may be worthy of your steel.:-)  I manage to 
upset a lot of people here a fair bit of the time but I do try not to be 
personal about it. (Don't always succeed, and when I don't I apologise...) I 
think they let me get way with it because they know there is no malice in me 
:-) As far as you're concerned, there may be some reservations about that... 
:-)

The point is that intelligent adults SHOULD be able to discuss things and 
disagree about them, and none of us should be getting worked up about what 
goes down here. ("it is only computer programming"). Nevertheless, the 
majority of the people here (self included) are pretty passionate about what 
they do and we do take it seriously (that's one reason why I love to see 
some of the wit that gets posted here; it is light relief from serious 
work...). I know you do too, so you have as much right here as anyone.
>
>>On the negative side, he appears to have little or no respect for people
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> American, the rest are from Russia, India and <gulp> Arab countries.

Robert, you have a perfect right to like and respect whoever you want to. To 
decide that a certain group of people, whether it is mainframe programmers, 
or bus conductors or even (as in my case, The French :-)) are contemptible, 
is to simply demonstrate a bias or prejudice that means that any exceptions 
are never given a chance to show why they are exceptional :-)

(I am gradually growing out of my French prejudice, but I'm not there 
yet..still, it's only been 45 years... give it time....:-))

>
> I sometimes ponder what would happen if the situation were reversed, if we 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Having done exactly what you describe, I can confirm that it isn't always 
easy. However, it is seldom boring :-)

>>He is not a stupid man and is capable of modifying his behaviour, just 
>>like
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks, Pete, for the defense.

It wasn't really a defense, Robert. Just making sure that everyone gets, as 
we say in NZ: "a fair go".

> I was tempted to rebut that the ones who agree with me
> abandoned Cobol in the '90s, and went on to fame and fortune with other 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming.

I would say that ANY professional programmer who scorns other programmers 
because of the language they write in, simply doesn't get it. (Unless of 
course, they write in RPG...:-))
>
> With your erudite (as always) writing, I don't have to go down that road 
> now. :)
:-)
>
>>We all learned many years ago how pointless it is to have arguments about
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> where I expect to find many code samples.

Programming forums can have many purposes. (This one caters to a myriad 
needs). We found through experience that heated, emphatic debates about a 
particular style of coding really didn't change anyone's mind. (However, 
pros and cons were thoroughly explored :-)). And the results are what's 
really important.

As a professional contract programmer, I'll maintain whatever anyone gives 
me; good, bad, or indifferent. I don't advocate inconsiderate coding, 
(convoluted, obtuse, or unstructured code is simply inconsiderate of people 
who must maintain it), but I don't think there's only one way to solve 
problems in computer programming, either.

>
>>Although it is important to all of us, it is still only computer 
>>programming
>
> What do you mean ONLY?

:-)

Pete.
```

```
them the same things I would have. If I said I used to be an XYZ administrator, it would

language. Cobol and C are comparable, in my opinion. C is not 'closer to machine
language', as often said. Why is there such a difference in image?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW and "good COBOL" (was: Cobol Myth Busters

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-14T00:02:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1189753332.054979.34550@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d34ke3h63e71pmrcqshrra42pnvorgtm7j@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4ml9e39bfkd30oa1e0o7tdhs6eef49kdtg@4ax.com> <1189405361.877179.168910@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <c8ZFi.377367$Bo7.200313@fe07.news.easynews.com> <5krdqnF5605lU1@mid.individual.net> <rn4he3ptrgaaq3olgl1uvp4j1shi7b3aqa@4ax.com> <5ksancF579lpU1@mid.individual.net> <d34ke3h63e71pmrcqshrra42pnvorgtm7j@4ax.com>`

```
On Sep 14, 5:14 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:

> C has been used since 1972. It is regarded as old, but is still a respected programming
> language. Cobol and C are comparable, in my opinion. C is not 'closer to machine
> language', as often said. Why is there such a difference in image?

C _is_ 'closer to machine language' than the standard Cobol language
in use, simply because C uses the machine's data types for each
different machine it is on while Cobol uses (mostly) its own types
regardless of the machine it is on.

That is, Cobol hides the underlying machine while C exposes it.

Granted various _extensions_ in Cobol compilers can fake C's usage for
some systems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW and "good COBOL" (was: Cobol Myth Busters

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-14T20:05:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<69bme397rfotfms74llfbtt6nkltb1i21o@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <c8ZFi.377367$Bo7.200313@fe07.news.easynews.com> <5krdqnF5605lU1@mid.individual.net> <rn4he3ptrgaaq3olgl1uvp4j1shi7b3aqa@4ax.com> <5ksancF579lpU1@mid.individual.net> <d34ke3h63e71pmrcqshrra42pnvorgtm7j@4ax.com> <1189753332.054979.34550@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 14 Sep 2007 00:02:51 -0700, Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Sep 14, 5:14 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>some systems.

Cobol's small endian Standard is a weak argument. IA-32 can flip the endianness of a
32-bit integer with a single instruction. And, as you say, major compilers offer a native
type. 

Cobol's insistance on decimal truncation is a valid complaint. Who thought THAT was a good
idea? Probably the company selling packed decimal.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW and "good COBOL" (was: Cobol Myth Busters

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message
news:69bme397rfotfms74llfbtt6nkltb1i21o@4ax.com...
[snip]
> Cobol's small endian Standard is a weak argument. IA-32 can flip the
endianness of a
> 32-bit integer with a single instruction.

The COBOL standard does not specify how binary
numbers are stored. It is up to the implementor. Some
chose to use the same arrangement as IBM, probably,
for compatibility.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<4ml9e39bfkd30oa1e0o7tdhs6eef49kdtg@4ax.com> <1189405361.877179.168910@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <c8ZFi.377367$Bo7.200313@fe07.news.easynews.com> <5krdqnF5605lU1@mid.individual.net> <rn4he3ptrgaaq3olgl1uvp4j1shi7b3aqa@4ax.com> <5ksancF579lpU1@mid.individual.net> <d34ke3h63e71pmrcqshrra42pnvorgtm7j@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:d34ke3h63e71pmrcqshrra42pnvorgtm7j@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 13 Sep 2007 20:29:31 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
> sound like 2.

Excellent!

>
>>I have had some experience at rocking corporate boats and have made 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

ROFL!!

It just so happens that a certain Editor who I sometimes write pieces for, 
sent me an e-mail about two weeks ago suggesting a certain airline, and that 
she could easily place good material there.  I didn't give it any more 
thought (being busy and sick) until I saw your post above. Now I'm extremely 
tempted :-)


>>>>When Robert first came here he was extremely hostile, belligerent, and
>>>>with
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> They raise the signal to noise ratio to make conversation impractical.

And Billy Goats simply ignore the Troll post altogether, making no response 
to the Troll or discussing the post amongst themselves.

Sometimes this is done deliberately by agreement amongst the goats... :-)

Usenet can be a tiresome place, but it can also be exhilarating. It's really 
our choice...
>
>>I would say that ANY professional programmer who scorns other programmers
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> You wouldn't say that if you'd worked in DIBOL or Basic.

I have. The DEC implementation I found bizarre; BASIC I have always had a 
liking for.

>
>>> How sad that style debates about programming style are futile here. I
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> .. Cobol is disrespected in other programming cultures

(Only 2 sweeping statements :-)? Your argument is rendered more powerful 
than if there were three...)

I think the first two are pretty fair; certainly they cannot be proven to be 
not true.

The third one simply doesn't matter.  COBOL belongs to a paradigm which the 
world has rejected, so it is quite likely that people working in other 
languages will look askance at it. Does it need approval? :-)

>
> C has been used since 1972. It is regarded as old, but is still a 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, actually, C is suffering from its own image problems at the moment, 
but, leaving that aside, there is a much wider world base for C than there 
is for COBOL. (In the absence of any solid, credible data, I base this on my 
experience, indicators that I note in various forums, available jobs on 
various job servers, and very quick "rules of thumb" like running searches 
in various engines... ["COBOL programming" returns just over 2 million hits; 
"C programming" returns just over 230 million hits... while this may prove 
nothing, a factor of 100 times cannot be ignored.]

If somethng is popular, in the sense that it has a huge user base, then its 
image is likely to be better than something that has a diminishing user 
base.

COBOL kind of missed the boat because it was not freely available (and never 
has been). It is ironic to me that the guys writing Open COBOL are doing so, 
NOW. Obviously, it is a labour of love. I really wish them well.

When the paradigm shift occurred, it was pretty easy for C programmers to 
move to C++ and embrace OO.  COBOL programmers were given the same 
opportunity, but it would have cost them seriously and there was no 
encouragement to do so. Those of us who DID make that move are now realizing 
that the paradigm is more important even than the language, and recognizing 
that COBOL is not great for web and network programming (I'm not saying you 
CAN'T use it, and I have done so, it just suffers by comparison with other 
things.)

Having said all of that, there are still many places using COBOL 
successfully, and we are still 8 years away from 2015 which is when I 
predicted the "Death of COBOL". It's too early to switch the lights off yet, 
but I wouldn't recommend young people to choose COBOL programming as a 
career right now...:-)

Pete.
```

```
On Sep 15, 3:39 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in messagenews:p%FGi.154721$1J4.119852@fe06.news.easynews.com...
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> that would be pedantic...:-)

No, what _would_ be pedantic is pointing out that internet 'trolls'
have nothing to do with bill goat gruff or living under bridges.

Trolling is, amongst other things, a fishing term used where a baited
hook is drawn through the water in front (hopefully) of the fish,
hoping they will take the bait and be caught on the hook to be played
until finally landed.

The troll is the baited line, or the message, not the fisherman or
message writer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW and "good COBOL" (was: Cobol Myth Busters

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-14T21:15:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l1dme39fe9r9m1vb8lgl72ae8th6t75cmt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1189405361.877179.168910@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <c8ZFi.377367$Bo7.200313@fe07.news.easynews.com> <5krdqnF5605lU1@mid.individual.net> <rn4he3ptrgaaq3olgl1uvp4j1shi7b3aqa@4ax.com> <5ksancF579lpU1@mid.individual.net> <d34ke3h63e71pmrcqshrra42pnvorgtm7j@4ax.com> <5kv9raF5ng8lU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Fri, 14 Sep 2007 23:29:03 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>COBOL kind of missed the boat because it was not freely available (and never 
>has been). It is ironic to me that the guys writing Open COBOL are doing so, 
>NOW. Obviously, it is a labour of love. I really wish them well.

C wasn't free in the old days, but it was affordable at less than $100. Turbo Pascal was
on top from '83 to '85 primarily because it had a better IDE than other compilers,
secondarily because it sold for $50. It wasn't because of the Pascal language. 

I believe the biggest nail in Cobol's coffin was the failure of negotiation between
Microsoft and Realia. If it had gone forward, Cobol would now be integrated with Dev
Studio, OLE and dot-Net. As a result, OO Cobol would have succeeded. 

>When the paradigm shift occurred, it was pretty easy for C programmers to 
>move to C++ and embrace OO.  COBOL programmers were given the same 
>opportunity, but it would have cost them seriously and there was no 
>encouragement to do so. 

There would have been encouragement outside the corporate world. IBM and its customers
would have followed, not led, as they did with Java and Linux. 

I find it hard to think of a major computer advance that originated in the corporate world
after 1990. Ok, Tivoli and EJB. 

>Those of us who DID make that move are now realizing 
>that the paradigm is more important even than the language, and recognizing 
>that COBOL is not great for web and network programming (I'm not saying you 
>CAN'T use it, and I have done so, it just suffers by comparison with other 
>things.)

Other languages get their power from libraries and integration, not the language per se.
If Cobol had the same support as say C#, it would be as useful.

>Having said all of that, there are still many places using COBOL 
>successfully, and we are still 8 years away from 2015 which is when I 
>predicted the "Death of COBOL". It's too early to switch the lights off yet, 
>but I wouldn't recommend young people to choose COBOL programming as a 
>career right now...:-)

Haven't you heard? There's a Critical Shortage of Cobol skill. Big companies can't find
enough Cobol programmers, even in India. (India was cranking out 30,000 per year before
Y2K. The bottom fell out after Y2K.)

Pay rate for Cobol contractors reveals the untruth of that assertion. It's been flat since
the '90s, except for the Y2K blip.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW and "good COBOL" (was: Cobol Myth Busters

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:l1dme39fe9r9m1vb8lgl72ae8th6t75cmt@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 14 Sep 2007 23:29:03 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Both MicroFocus and Fujitsu have COBOLs that meet all of the above criteria. 
I don't think the MicroSoft/Realia deal was pivotal, but it was probably 
important. Certainly, MicroSoft support for COBOL (And they partnered with 
Fujitsu for .Net COBOL for .NET) would not have hurt the perception of the 
language, but, at the end of the day, the procedural paradigm is just too 
expensive for it tio remain viable.


>>When the paradigm shift occurred, it was pretty easy for C programmers to
>>move to C++ and embrace OO.  COBOL programmers were given the same
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> If Cobol had the same support as say C#, it would be as useful.

It IS useful. It just costs too much to write and maintain millions of lines 
of code. Library support for Fujitsu OO COBOL is extensive and excellent. (I 
have never encountered a bug in their database or Java integration 
libraries).  Unfortunately, the libraries are not interchangeable and that 
is why DotNET and MONO will rule the world :-)
>
>>Having said all of that, there are still many places using COBOL
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
>On Thu, 13 Sep 2007 12:16:21 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"

[snip]

>>>>> CLC regulars are more interested in recreational pedantry than they are 
>>>>> in writing good
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>malapert: One who likes to shock with saucy, brasen, impudent statements

Do you have a cite for this definition, Mr Wagner?  Ones I can find (eg 
http://dictionary.reference.com/browse/malapert ) indicate merely the 
condition, not the desire.

>ultracrepidarian: One who tries to work outside his field of knowledge,
>    where he has no business being.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>catagelophobe: A thin-skinned person overly sensitive to criticism.

Do you have a cite for this definition, Mr Wagner?  Ones I can find (eg 
http://dictionary.reference.com/search?r=2&q=catagelophobia ) indicate 
something else.

>logomachist: One who constantly asks for definitions of words.

Do you have a cite for this definition, Mr Wagner?  Ones I can find (eg 
http://dictionary.reference.com/browse/logomachist ) indicate something 
else.

>mome: One who loves to use others' petty mistakes as an excuse to put
>    them down. A hair-splitter.

Do you have a cite for this definition, Mr Wagner?  Ones I can find (eg 
http://dictionary.reference.com/browse/mome ) indicate otherwise.

>
>>> However, his "sweeping" statements (without qualification) are so common 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Standards are the Holy Writ.

In the world of corporate IT where I have lived, Mr Wagner, Standards are 
not 'holy' any more than where one works is a church.  Write any code you 
want... but if it is put into Prod it must be done by the business' rules.

>Criticism of those practices is an insult
>to the company's
>wisdom.

Anywhere I have worked, Mr Wagner, folks have pointed to what they see as 
foolish or unnecessary parts of the site's Standards as objects of 
mirth... followed by a resigned 'What are ya gonna do, you want to get it 
into Prod, these are the rules.'

>Standard is supposed to mean consensus.

If one examines the code in Prod one may well see in it a consensus of 
statements in agreement with the Standards, sure.

[snip]

>>If, instead of the group reacting with ad hominem attacks, his arguments are 
>>examined and refuted (I think Richard and Doug Miller have done a good job 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>If it turns into a flamefest, everyone loses.

That might be argued against by those who enjoy flamefests.

DD
```

```
On Thu, 13 Sep 2007 13:35:15 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <rn4he3ptrgaaq3olgl1uvp4j1shi7b3aqa@4ax.com>,
>Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>condition, not the desire.

Definitions came from:
"Dimboxes, Epopts and other Quidams", David Grambs
"Mrs. Byrne's Dictionary", Josefa Heifetz Byrne

Here's another:
Floccinaucinihilipilification: the act of describing things as worthless

I got it from a beer bottle:
http://www.stonebrew.com/timeline/2004/8thanniv/ale/makingof/index.html

>Anywhere I have worked, Mr Wagner, folks have pointed to what they see as 
>foolish or unnecessary parts of the site's Standards as objects of 
>mirth... followed by a resigned 'What are ya gonna do, you want to get it 
>into Prod, these are the rules.'

At one place I convinced the programming manager that every paragraph should begin with
NOTE, followed by an explanation of what it did. He published it as a shop standard. 

In the Good Old Days, NOTE as the first word of a paragraph turned the whole paragraph
into comments.
```

```
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
>On Thu, 13 Sep 2007 13:35:15 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>"Mrs. Byrne's Dictionary", Josefa Heifetz Byrne

I see... certainly a different one than either the Random House 
Unabridged; further decisions may be left up to The Gentle Reader.

>
>Here's another:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.stonebrew.com/timeline/2004/8thanniv/ale/makingof/index.html

I recall it as an 'act of estimating' or 'evaluating'... but my memory, 
is, admittedly, porous.

(years ago I read a Story for Schoolchildren about a lad who was forced 
into babysitting a neighbor's child while his sister was out doing 
something else; he responded to this moderately embarassing bit of 
effiminisation by reading to the infant from his list of words for the 
next spelling test. a few days later there was an uproar at the neighbors' 
house; they thought the child was suffering from delusions or genius or... 
something weird because of the words coming out of its mouth.  the lad 
listened and said 'no, it is parrotting what I had for my spelling 
test...'

... and e'er since then I've remembered 'pusillanimous - devoid or 
destitute of manly strength')

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW and "good COBOL" (was: Cobol Myth Busters

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-09-14T13:16:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fce1iu$3vv$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<dldhd39vccjgdgs3g1572hbi2eq2suil7u@4ax.com> <29qje39ojajk40tp8frds01bu2ope4h87n@4ax.com> <fcdl9r$rhq$1@reader1.panix.com> <5kva3tF5jh8mU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5kva3tF5jh8mU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fcdl9r$rhq$1@reader1.panix.com...

[snip]

>> ... and e'er since then I've remembered 'pusillanimous - devoid or
>> destitute of manly strength')
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>milk occasionally...;-)

I'm not sure, Mr Dashwood... I recall translating, reading and discussing 
a play by Aristophanes ('The Acharnians') during my Kollidj Daze which 
contained a scene where a farmer brought his daughters to market so that 
he might sell them.  He disguised them as young pigs and there was much 
jolly bantering about selling such piggies in order to sacrifice them to 
Aphrodite - 'How!  One does not sacrifice piggies to Aphrodite!'  'And 
thus so, my good man, to what other goddess might such piggies be 
sacrificed?' - and it didn't make much sense...

... until more research was done.  Pigs were not sacrificed to Aphrodite, 
true, but we found out that in Ancient Greek the word for a piglet 
(translated as 'piggie') had the same position of linguistic ambiguity as 
'pussy' does in English.

DD
```

```
On Wed, 12 Sep 2007 22:55:04 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>with that :-)

I didn't say *all* CLC regulars are *always* pedantic. Isn't the omitted number and adverb
goot enough qualification?

>Sometimes, it certainly seems as you describe, and I have felt the same way. 
>But never would I suggest that all the people here are guilty, and even the 
>ones who specifically are sometimes, on other occasions can offer very 
>useful and informative information, and there is never any doubt as to their 
>ability. It shouldn't get personal, (even though it inevitably does...)

Personal? I didn't mention names because the guilty know who they are.

>The fact is that there is value here.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>are all better when we work together.

I concur.

>And most of the regulars (even me... who is no longer actively writing 
>COBOL) DO care about maintaining high standards of COBOL. In my case I base 
>it on the fact that if you're going to do anything, you should do it as well 
>as you possibly can.

Unless the person signing your time sheet or check wants you to write dreck, according to
Doc and others. 

>I think you have been out there in the real world, working on sites where 
>you have seen a lot of bad COBOL. 

Probably more than anyone else here. In the last 9 years, I've worked at 15 F-100
companies that are household names -- Sears, Merrill Lynch, Wal-Mart, Coca-Cola, Sprint,
etc.  And I deliberately avoid mainframe shops.

>This has engendered a lack of respect for 
>COBOL people in general (you KNOW you can do better and you KNOW you have 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>shop floor COBOL guys" here; we all have to learn...)

Cutting down average shop floor guys is like taking candy from a toddler. It's no
accomplishment; it makes you seem like a bully. 

There are two kinds of ignorance: simple and willful. Simple ignorance means you just
don't know; willful ignorance means you refuse to know, even when presented with
compelling evidence or logic. Some of the mainframers here are willfully ignorant. They
deserve no mercy.

>I therefore contend that your last statement above is wrong on two counts:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>alone.

That's been said many many times.

>2. As long as you believe you have the monopoly on writing "good" COBOL and 
>only what YOU consider "good" IS good, then your statement is suffering from 
>an inadequate definition of what is "good" COBOL.

There is no generally accepted definition of good Cobol. McCracken wrote some excellent
books on that in the '60s and '70s. The best we have now are Code Complete and Beautiful
Code. 

>Leaving aside the rhetoric and logic, as a statement, it doesn't help much, 
>does it?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>you are as much entitled to yours as anyone else here.

I must write like a lawyer? Well I did go to law school.
```

```
On Sep 13, 3:52 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Wed, 12 Sep 2007 22:55:04 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
> wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> goot enough qualification?

Yes, what you said did include 'all'. It is a common mistake for you
to make inclusive statements whether you mean to or not.

"CLC regulars" is a group that is all regular visitors to CLC. If you
omitted 'some' or 'blue ones' or 'those over 6 foot tall' then it
means 'all'.

But you'll probably just claim that as being pedantic.


> >Sometimes, it certainly seems as you describe, and I have felt the same way.
> >But never would I suggest that all the people here are guilty, and even the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Personal? I didn't mention names because the guilty know who they are.

You seem to think that having to retell you several times until you
understand is 'pedantry'.


> >The fact is that there is value here.
>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> deserve no mercy.

more group ad hominem.


> >I therefore contend that your last statement above is wrong on two counts:
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> I must write like a lawyer? Well I did go to law school.
```

```
You're falling into the same trap as Howard :-) (Maybe it's a military thing 
:-))

While I understand that you feel the "tone" is different, in fact it isn't. 
The two statements are identical. I notice that Skippy made the same 
observation as me (and he's ex VietNam, so I guess it isn't military.) Maybe 
it is something in the local culture (inflexion or stress; I really don't 
know...) but I hear the same thing when either form is used and they both 
mean exactly the same to me, without me needing to analyse it.

If a statement is otherwise unqualified, it implies "ALL".

However, maybe not in American vernacular :-)

Pete.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:5kvamaF5o8dtU1@mid.individual.net...
>
<snip>
> You're falling into the same trap as Howard :-) (Maybe it's a military thing 
> :-))
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snip>
> Pete.
> -- 
> "I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."
>

It may be an "Americanism" (or depending on Candada, etc, a US-ism), but I think 
that I actually agree that OFTEN (not always <G>) there is a difference, e.g.

Americans like fast food.  (If said around here, would be something that MOST 
Americans - that I know - would accept as true)
    while
All Americans like fast food. (Would be considered an "over-statement" and could 
be proven false my a single counter-example.)

Having said that, I would certianly think that the first sentence would be 
BETTER expressed as

Most Americans like fast fool.
   or
Most of the Americans that I know of like fast food.

  ***

My (personal) impression is that RW does tend to use suffiently many 
generalizations that I usually READ those as a statement applying to all - 
UNLESS he explicitly quaalifies them (which he rarely does).
```

```
On Sat, 15 Sep 2007 00:31:31 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
>news:5kvamaF5o8dtU1@mid.individual.net...
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I hate fast food because:

1. It's meat-centric. I don't eat meat.
2. It's patently unhealthy. 
3. It lays bare Americans' poor taste and lack of self-esteem.
4. It is a formula for exploiting no-talent, minimum wage workers.
5. It simply isn't any good, gastronomically. 

I've visited (and reviewed) small scale restaurants offering GOOD food, delivered quickly.
To America's shame, they're not commercially successful. 

Analogies could be drawn between American taste for food and programming. I mean lack of
taste.

>My (personal) impression is that RW does tend to use suffiently many 
>generalizations that I usually READ those as a statement applying to all - 
>UNLESS he explicitly qualifies them (which he rarely does).

I read somewhere that simple declarative sentences are most forceful and convincing.
Qualifiers make the reader use recursion, forward or backward, and make the writer look
hesitant.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Finally to the point (was: [OT] Pedantry (Was: Cobol Myth Busters)

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-17T01:37:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<STkHi.421052$sR4.417330@fe08.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1189405361.877179.168910@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <1189657537.360200.316670@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <8MOdnXKXN8tKnXfbnZ2dnUVZ_qainZ2d@comcast.com> <5kvamaF5o8dtU1@mid.individual.net> <DJFGi.154400$1J4.36335@fe06.news.easynews.com> <qrjre3hbc9ju481ku2fdlpplrni1vcebd8@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:qrjre3hbc9ju481ku2fdlpplrni1vcebd8@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 15 Sep 2007 00:31:31 GMT, "William M. Klein" 
> <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
<snip>

> I read somewhere that simple declarative sentences are most forceful and 
> convincing.
> Qualifiers make the reader use recursion, forward or backward, and make the 
> writer look
> hesitant.

I can't (and won't try to) speak for ALL (or even most) CLC readers (and 
particularly "regulars"), but this (to me) is the root of my (and possibly 
others) problems with many of your CLC posts.

To me (and I believe others) your (and most) posts would be read (better) for 
content than for "tone"

1) Use (or even OVER-use) qualification.
 - When you are expressing an opinion, be SPECIFIC that it is an opinion
 - When you are speaking from your experiences, be specific that it is your 
personal experience that something is true or common
 - When you are stating that it is your impression that something is a UNIVERSAL 
truth, say that
 - When you are stating a "fact", be clear as to why you think it is a fact

2) When you are "adding" information because someone has responded to something 
that you didn't "mean"
 - Be clear that you are adding clarification
 - If you are "changing" what you originally stated, make that clear

3) When you were wrong (or misunderstood) be "out front" that you made a mistake 
or that you need to clarify what you originally stated

  ***

Obviously, as Pete would remind me/us, this is an UNMEDIATED group.  However, in 
one of your posts you indicated that you WANTED your notes to be read (and 
considered) for their technical content and recommendations/options.  I would 
(personally) recommend that you change the "tone" of your posts to emphasis 
opinion and generalizations as such and distinguish them from "simple 
declarative sentences" that many of us (as I read
responses to your notes) read as "false statements of universal fact".

And I fully accept that my writing confirm your statement about qualification 
"Qualifiers make the reader use recursion, forward or backward, ..." but I think 
that they also make such statements more acceptable to some (possibly most) of 
this group.
```

```
In article <qrjre3hbc9ju481ku2fdlpplrni1vcebd8@4ax.com>,
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
>On Sat, 15 Sep 2007 00:31:31 GMT, "William M. Klein"

>>My (personal) impression is that RW does tend to use suffiently many 
>>generalizations that I usually READ those as a statement applying to all - 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

How interesting... I read somewhere that 'simple statements indicate a 
simple mind' (either in the author or in the intended audience) and that a 
lengthier, elegantly-crafted sentence, replete with Latinisims and 
complex, moderetly deep inner punctuation, might better attract the 
attentions of a mind which could - given time and inclination, of course - 
be able to take the thought expressed therein and create therefrom 
something of value.

(a lack of qualifiers, of course, make the writer seem hubris-filled and 
prone towards expressing opinion or anecdote as unqualified fact... 
including this sentence)

DD
```

- **References:** `<1189405361.877179.168910@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <1189657537.360200.316670@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <8MOdnXKXN8tKnXfbnZ2dnUVZ_qainZ2d@comcast.com> <5kvamaF5o8dtU1@mid.individual.net> <DJFGi.154400$1J4.36335@fe06.news.easynews.com> <qrjre3hbc9ju481ku2fdlpplrni1vcebd8@4ax.com> <pvuHi.52080$Y7.14874@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <1190312789.537198.83700@y42g2000hsy.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Robert" <n...@e.mail> wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> the first chips (non-potato) made?

The Internet grew from what was once "DARPANET," a creation of
the U.S. military for communications between scientists working on
defense contracts under DARPA (Defense Advanced Research
Planning Agency, IIRC). When it grew outside the bounds the U.S.
military was comfortable with, they left it to everybody else and built
another closed system for themselves. I believe that TCP/IP was
created specifically for DARPANET, though I believe the name was
a bit different in the early days.

Don't remember offhand who invented the first IC, but the first micro
CPU was the Intel 4004 in the early 70's, created for a calculator.
```

```
On 20 Sep, 22:17, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I was thinking of Tim Berners-Lee but apparently the 'internet' pre-
dated him. However, being very partisan in my selection of backup bumf
I found this on Wikipedia:

<QUOTE>
The ARPANET was one of the "eve" networks of today's Internet.
Following on from the demonstration that packet switching worked on
the ARPANET, the British Post Office, Telenet, DATAPAC and TRANSPAC
collaborated to create the first international packet switched network
service. In the UK, this was referred to as the International Packet
Stream Service (IPSS), in 1978.
</QUOTE>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Myth Busters

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-13T22:11:40+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ksgmtF5ad3iU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<dldhd39vccjgdgs3g1572hbi2eq2suil7u@4ax.com> <1189392749.625750.86680@y42g2000hsy.googlegroups.com> <4ml9e39bfkd30oa1e0o7tdhs6eef49kdtg@4ax.com> <1189405361.877179.168910@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 12 Sep 2007 22:55:04 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> I didn't say *all* CLC regulars are *always* pedantic.

Yes you did, you just didn't realize it :-)

Spot the difference:

1.All donkeys eat carrots...
2.Donkeys eat carrots...

There is absolutely no difference. They are sweeping statements.

You have to say: "Some donkeys eat carrots..."  "Many donkeys eat carrots" 
or "Most donkeys eat carrots..."

I remember this from a philosophical logic class when a very young man.

"Donkeys eat carrots
You eat carrots
Therefore you are a donkey"

(The famous undistributed middle,


Isn't the omitted number and adverb
> goot enough qualification?
>
Not in Logic, no.


>>Sometimes, it certainly seems as you describe, and I have felt the same 
>>way.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Personal? I didn't mention names because the guilty know who they are.

No, I wasn't referring to your post. You have shown good restraint in not 
responding in kind to some of the comments directed at you. I think that 
even some of the posters have noted this. (I hope so... :-))
>
>>The fact is that there is value here.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> I concur.

Good. It's true :-)
>
>>And most of the regulars (even me... who is no longer actively writing
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Doc and others.

That is a point of view.

Doc and I have debated this at length here and I don't think he's totally 
wrong. I also do not believe the Doc would write crap even if the person 
signing the timesheet required it. :-)

Things are not always black and white.

>
>>I think you have been out there in the real world, working on sites where
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> accomplishment; it makes you seem like a bully.

Yes, it is demeaning and best avoided. (a bit like cutting down anybody, 
really...)
>
> There are two kinds of ignorance: simple and willful. Simple ignorance 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I hope you'll change your mind about that Robert. But I totally respect your 
right not to :-)

Whether ignorance is willful or simple by your definition, it is still 
ignorance.

Here's where the trouble starts.

I see someone is "ignorant". I inform him so he isn't. But he doesn't accept 
my information (willful ignorance, by your definition).

You are saying that such people "deserve no mercy".

Throughout history various churches have done exactly that process. Convert 
to the One True Faith and you will be given mercy. Don't, and you will 
receive no mercy.

It is a staggering testament to the Human Spirit that countless people have 
refused the offer, and chosen instead to be burned, hanged, beheaded, 
tortured, or what-have-you, rather than be untrue to themselves.

Intimidation may be a very powerful weapon, but integrity is even more so.

So, it seems to me, that if you pursue the policy you outlined above, you 
are really little better than the Grand Inquisitor, or any historical 
despotic dictator,  who would not brook dissent.

People have a right to disagree.  Even after they've heard the One True Word 
:-)

It is extremely unlikely that anyone was ever persuaded by having a case 
rammed down their throat... mercilessly :-)

If you really want to bring light to the ignorant, it cannot be done 
mercilessly.

Think some more about dissent. I don't believe you really want the Grand 
Inquisitor role... :-)

>>I therefore contend that your last statement above is wrong on two counts:
>>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> books on that in the '60s and '70s.

Now, it's interesting you should say that... :-)

I thought Daniel McCracken's books were, at best, mediocre :-)

The very best book I ever read on COBOL was by Fritz D. McCameron PhD, (now, 
I think sadly out of print) at the University of Louisiana. "COBOL Logic and 
programming".  He was able to take things like nested IFs, PERFORM...VARYING 
and make them sensible. Sure, today we all understand these things, but as a 
young programmer learning the language, I found his advice outstanding.  I 
still practise some of what I learned from him on the increasingly rare 
occasions when I need to write COBOL.

I think no less of you for liking McCracken; neither do I feel driven to 
persuade you to read McCameron (assuming you could even get a copy), and 
besides, with the degree of experience you have now, you wouldn't learn 
anything anyway.

So, you see, it is possible to disagree peaceably :-)


>The best we have now are Code Complete and Beautiful
> Code.

I've not encountered either of those.

>
>>Leaving aside the rhetoric and logic, as a statement, it doesn't help 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> I must write like a lawyer? Well I did go to law school.

No, Robert, you MUSTn't do anything. You choose what you want to do.

Just take care on the sweeping statements... :-)

Pete.
```

```
implicit.

No, sorry, Howard. On this occasion it is simply a matter of record. There 
are no "degrees of sweeping" even though many people think there are. Check 
the rules in any book on Philosophical Logic or rhetoric, and you will find 
them as I have described.

This is NOT my "opinion", (if it were, I would have said so) :-).

They are simply the rules.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Myth Busters

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-13T10:10:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c1oie3pbntfj2cjoboh96qo2c4ev1pac7v@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4ml9e39bfkd30oa1e0o7tdhs6eef49kdtg@4ax.com> <1189405361.877179.168910@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <5ksgmtF5ad3iU1@mid.individual.net> <rmiie3p33a3udtrbahirf58027shiu2hpr@4ax.com> <5kt15sF5aj47U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Fri, 14 Sep 2007 02:52:43 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>Case 2 is Case 1, in the rules of logic. If "all" is not stated it is 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>They are simply the rules.

They may be rules for a particular rhetoric - but that's not how our
language works in real life.    If I say "People like desserts", I
would be telling the truth.   If I say "All people like desserts", I
would be wrong.

It is very easy to come up with lots of similar examples that are used
in real life.

So the question is - is this forum bound by the rules of a particular
school of philosophy, or is it better served by using the rules of
English?
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5kv6ojF5hmfmU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4ml9e39bfkd30oa1e0o7tdhs6eef49kdtg@4ax.com> <1189405361.877179.168910@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <5ksgmtF5ad3iU1@mid.individual.net> <rmiie3p33a3udtrbahirf58027shiu2hpr@4ax.com> <5kt15sF5aj47U1@mid.individual.net> <c1oie3pbntfj2cjoboh96qo2c4ev1pac7v@4ax.com>`

```


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:c1oie3pbntfj2cjoboh96qo2c4ev1pac7v@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 14 Sep 2007 02:52:43 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> language works in real life.

It may not be how YOUR language works in real life, but it IS how sweeping 
statements work in real life.(And, it is certainly how my language works in 
real life: I say what I mean and I (usually :-)) mean what I say.)

It has nothing to do with the language used (it is actually conceptual).

"Todos los burros...
"Los burros

el mismo... :-)

It has more to do with straight and crooked thinking than it does with 
language.

>    If I say "People like desserts", I
> would be telling the truth.

No, the statement is not true.(If there is one single person (and I know 
several) who does not like desserts, the proposition is ... busted :-))

If you say, "Some people like desserts.."  the statement is true.

If you say, "Many people like desserts" the statement may be true. (It is 
not proven false)

If you say, "Most people like desserts" the statement may be true. (it is 
not proven false)

> If I say "All people like desserts", I
> would be wrong.

Yes, you would be. Exactly as you were above when you implied the "All" by 
dropping it.

>
> It is very easy to come up with lots of similar examples that are used
> in real life.
>
There is a lot of muddled and crooked thinking in real life. :-)

> So the question is - is this forum bound by the rules of a particular
> school of philosophy, or is it better served by using the rules of
> English?

Oh come on, Howard :-)

It has nothing to do with the rules of English. You are simply saying you 
want it bound by how YOU personally interpret those statements.

Your argument comes down to making an argument for bad grammar (for 
example...), because that is how you prefer it, in the "real" world...:-)

By all means, speak (and think) any way you want to. But don't kid yourself 
that in doing so you can be precise and accurate.

My original issue was with Robert's sweeping statement. (A sweeping 
statement can only be true if it applies directly to all individual cases: 
"People need to breathe.", "Islands are surrounded by water". )

This device is called "Dicto Simpliciter" in text books. You'd think that 
"spoken simply" would be a Good Thing in debate, but here the "simple" is 
referring to the missing qualification that makes the statement true or at 
least not proven false.

As I am now sounding pedantic (perish the thought :-)), here's my last word 
on "sweeping statements"...

<quote from Archies Archives>

A friend and I were standing inside a building of a local theme park. We 
were looking outside, and it was an extremely windy day.

The area's custodian, the one who had the job of sweeping up debris, was a 
very small woman (4'10?, 90lbs) and she was having a rough time trying to 
not be blown away.

My friend joked with the lady, telling her that she would have to put heavy 
rocks in her shoes when she went outside to work.

The lady looked at my friend and lisped, "You mean, I have to weigh me down 
to sweep?"

</quote from Archies Archives>

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <5ksgmtF5ad3iU1@mid.individual.net> <rmiie3p33a3udtrbahirf58027shiu2hpr@4ax.com> <5kt15sF5aj47U1@mid.individual.net> <c1oie3pbntfj2cjoboh96qo2c4ev1pac7v@4ax.com> <5kv6ojF5hmfmU1@mid.individual.net> <1189807996.059225.14960@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <8h6te3pin6t51sknbd3mt354gq38s34sea@4ax.com>`

```
On Sep 18, 3:19 am, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> On Fri, 14 Sep 2007 15:13:16 -0700, Alistair
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> How about the Islet of Langerhans?

Isle of Ely is in Cambridgeshire and should not be confused with Eel
Pie Island which is in the Thames.

Many places used to be islands surrounded by, at least, wetlands, but
draining this for agriculture, or other, left it as part of the
surrounding solid ground. The Isle of Dogs in London, for example.
Another is the village from which my surname derives which is recorded
as being on an island when Plegmund, Archbishop of Canterbury in
England from 890 to 914, lived there and called it Plegmundeshamm.
After many name variations, one of which is my surname, it is now
Plemstall and not an island.
```

```
marshy areas until they were drained. I don't know if the Isle was

then the two are analogous.
```

- **References:** `<attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <5ksgmtF5ad3iU1@mid.individual.net> <rmiie3p33a3udtrbahirf58027shiu2hpr@4ax.com> <5kt15sF5aj47U1@mid.individual.net> <c1oie3pbntfj2cjoboh96qo2c4ev1pac7v@4ax.com> <5kv6ojF5hmfmU1@mid.individual.net> <1189807996.059225.14960@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <8h6te3pin6t51sknbd3mt354gq38s34sea@4ax.com> <1190312985.217263.126790@n39g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <5cv5f3h1agml8ffaumlk27hcha3g40g632@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:5cv5f3h1agml8ffaumlk27hcha3g40g632@4ax.com...

<snip>

The field UNALIGNED-NUMBER use in the Unalgned test is indeed on an odd 
numer address.

The Misaligne(1) and Misaligned(2) tests are invalid tests because the 
compiler is smart enough to allocate storage so that 
MISALIGNED-NUMBER(S-SUBSCRIPT) is always on a fullword boundary. See below 
for evidence:



01  MISALIGNED-AREA.                                       0CL32768
    05  ARRAY-ELEMENT OCCURS 4096 INDEXED X-INDEX.         0CL8

        10  MISALIGNED-NUMBER    COMP-5   PIC S9(09) SYNC. 1F
        10  TO-CAUSE-MISALIGNMENT         PIC  X(01).      1C


UNALIGNED-NUMBER
    DECIMAL   92286021
    PRINTABLE 1D57B045
MISALIGNED-NUMBER(1)
    DECIMAL   92306536
    PRINTABLE 1D580068
MISALIGNED-NUMBER(2)
    DECIMAL   92306544
    PRINTABLE 1D580070
MISALIGNED-NUMBER(3)
    DECIMAL   92306552
    PRINTABLE 1D580078
MISALIGNED-NUMBER(4)
    DECIMAL   92306560
    PRINTABLE 1D580080
MISALIGNED-NUMBER(5)
    DECIMAL   92306568
    PRINTABLE 1D580088

```

- **Date:** 2007-09-17T20:27:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NsGdnTKJ4NNJqnLbnZ2dnUVZ_gydnZ2d@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5kt15sF5aj47U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<dldhd39vccjgdgs3g1572hbi2eq2suil7u@4ax.com> <1189392749.625750.86680@y42g2000hsy.googlegroups.com> <4ml9e39bfkd30oa1e0o7tdhs6eef49kdtg@4ax.com> <1189405361.877179.168910@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <5ksgmtF5ad3iU1@mid.individual.net> <rmiie3p33a3udtrbahirf58027shiu2hpr@4ax.com> <5kt15sF5aj47U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
> news:rmiie3p33a3udtrbahirf58027shiu2hpr@4ax.com...
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> them as I have described.

Then "general conversation" does not follow the rules of Philosophical 
Logic or rhetoric.  In general conversation, the omission of the "all" 
does not mean there is an implicit one - it means that something is 
generally true.  (Such as the first sentence of this paragraph...  ;>)

> This is NOT my "opinion", (if it were, I would have said so) :-).
> 
> They are simply the rules.

IMO, this is a level of formality that is unnecessary in this forum. 
With the conversations in here as of late over form vs. content, I would 
think that those who are familiar with such formalities would realize 
that not every person uses them in ordinary day-to-day conversation. 
Knowing this, they might also see why their criticism of others' 
"ordinary language" is viewed as being overly pedantic.

That's really one of the things I don't like about this group.  However, 
it's not enough to make me stop stopping by...  :)  Whenever I get 
frustrated enough with the pedantry, I just drop the thread.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Logic vs. Conversation (Was: Cobol Myth Busters)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-18T15:19:57+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5l8uevF71vi0U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<dldhd39vccjgdgs3g1572hbi2eq2suil7u@4ax.com> <1189392749.625750.86680@y42g2000hsy.googlegroups.com> <4ml9e39bfkd30oa1e0o7tdhs6eef49kdtg@4ax.com> <1189405361.877179.168910@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <5ksgmtF5ad3iU1@mid.individual.net> <rmiie3p33a3udtrbahirf58027shiu2hpr@4ax.com> <5kt15sF5aj47U1@mid.individual.net> <NsGdnTKJ4NNJqnLbnZ2dnUVZ_gydnZ2d@comcast.com>`

```


"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:NsGdnTKJ4NNJqnLbnZ2dnUVZ_gydnZ2d@comcast.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> generally true.  (Such as the first sentence of this paragraph...  ;>)

Thank you for explaining what the omission of "all" means to you.

"Something is generally true"

So you mean "Most" or "Many"...(Is it too hard to add that to the sentence?)

If  I interpret the same statement in accordance with the rules of rhetoric, 
that makes me pedantic in your eyes?

Yet, I am supposed to just intuitively "know" how you interpret it when you 
operate without rules, except for the ones you "feel", or claim are what 
"everybody knows" (accepted mores in your society)?

OK, I'm glad we cleared that up. I now understand better what you are 
saying. :-)

>
>> This is NOT my "opinion", (if it were, I would have said so) :-).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> IMO, this is a level of formality that is unnecessary in this forum.

No, it's only "formal" when I do it.  When you do it, it is OK because it is 
what "everybody does".

> With the conversations in here as of late over form vs. content, I would 
> think that those who are familiar with such formalities would realize that 
> not every person uses them in ordinary day-to-day conversation.

Well see, that's where we have the problem. The people I deal with DO use 
them in ordinary day-to-day conversation. We are used to qualifying things 
when we want to discuss something, and do so instinctively. Sure, 
generalisations are also made, but not when we're actually debating 
something and exploring arguments.

In fact, to simply generalize about people and groups of people is 
considered impolite in our society.

And most of the people I deal with on a daily basis are not computer 
programmers, who might reasonably be expected to know something about logic.

> Knowing this, they might also see why their criticism of others' "ordinary 
> language" is viewed as being overly pedantic.

Knowing that when people feel they are losing an argument they will resort 
to tagging any counter argument with any label that they can get to stick, 
"overly pedantic" is to be expected.
>
> That's really one of the things I don't like about this group.  However, 
> it's not enough to make me stop stopping by...  :)  Whenever I get 
> frustrated enough with the pedantry, I just drop the thread.

Yeah, me too. But for me it isn't the pedantry (sometimes that is actually 
informative); it is the stupidity, especially when it comes from people who 
are not stupid. (And this last was not aimed at you, Daniel; more at people 
who will take one (incidental) line from a post and then make a number of 
repetitive posts about the history of the Isle of Ely, for example...when it 
jas already been covered.  :-))

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Logic vs. Conversation (Was: Cobol Myth Busters)

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-18T05:13:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k8JHi.259683$VU2.153378@fe02.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<dldhd39vccjgdgs3g1572hbi2eq2suil7u@4ax.com> <1189392749.625750.86680@y42g2000hsy.googlegroups.com> <4ml9e39bfkd30oa1e0o7tdhs6eef49kdtg@4ax.com> <1189405361.877179.168910@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <5ksgmtF5ad3iU1@mid.individual.net> <rmiie3p33a3udtrbahirf58027shiu2hpr@4ax.com> <5kt15sF5aj47U1@mid.individual.net> <NsGdnTKJ4NNJqnLbnZ2dnUVZ_gydnZ2d@comcast.com> <5l8uevF71vi0U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete (or anyone else),
  I tried searching online in my "usual" usage and/or grammar sites, but 
couldn't find any discussion of this (either as a rule or as an issue).  NOT in 
the case of "formal logic" but in the rules of English (US, British, generic, or 
other) can anyone point me to an online source that SAYS that the statement

  A verb B
    is equivalent (grammatically or common usage- not just in logic) to
  All A verb B

As  I say, I tried searching either for this as a "rule" or as an "issue" and 
couldn't find anything.  I probably was using the wrong terms for searching - 
but I did try enough variations that I would have THOUGHT that it would have 
come up somewhere.

P.S.  I suppose I would take a discussion of "formal logic" is the default for 
"common usage".  There probably are such when discussing "double negatives" and 
their impact for various dialects and usage.  However, there might be some for 
other issues as well.
```

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **Date:** 2007-09-19T03:41:56+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5la9u4F6dn6cU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<dldhd39vccjgdgs3g1572hbi2eq2suil7u@4ax.com> <1189392749.625750.86680@y42g2000hsy.googlegroups.com> <4ml9e39bfkd30oa1e0o7tdhs6eef49kdtg@4ax.com> <1189405361.877179.168910@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <ds0ce3d60gk2m7apm3icuag2io65thtdlt@4ax.com> <75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <5ksgmtF5ad3iU1@mid.individual.net> <rmiie3p33a3udtrbahirf58027shiu2hpr@4ax.com> <5kt15sF5aj47U1@mid.individual.net> <NsGdnTKJ4NNJqnLbnZ2dnUVZ_gydnZ2d@comcast.com> <5l8uevF71vi0U1@mid.individual.net> <k8JHi.259683$VU2.153378@fe02.news.easynews.com>`

```


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
news:k8JHi.259683$VU2.153378@fe02.news.easynews.com...
> Pete (or anyone else),
>  I tried searching online in my "usual" usage and/or grammar sites, but 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>  All A verb B

You won't find it under rules of English because it has nothing to do with 
English. (I thought I covered all that; sometimes I feel like nobody reads 
or bothers to absorb what I write, but they are really happy to have an 
argument about it. As I said in my original post this is more about straight 
and crooked thinking than it is about idioms (or even idiots :-). The best 
book I have ever read on the subject (apart from formal textbooks) is 
"Straight and Crooked Thinking" by Robert H  Thouless. In this book he lists 
38 dishonest tricks that people use in argument.

Number two on the list:

"Making a statement in which 'all' is implied but 'some' is true."

Every single one of the examples posted in this thread, where no 
qualification has been stated, falls in that category. But, despite my 
having gone to pains  to explain it, with examples, and make the point that 
these are the rules, instead of accepting there might be something in it, it 
is apparently easier to justify loose thinking with talk about the English 
Language, and "common speech" and "we know what we mean"...and whether an 
island really needs to be surrounded by water, and how pedantic I am for 
pointing out the actual rules...

I can honestly say that this thread more than any other has made me question 
my time here. It isn't the fact that I have failed to persuade (although 
that's always disappointing, I totally respect the right of people to agree 
or not), rather, it is the fierce stupidity that has been posted that pisses 
me off.

Never mind. : -) (If I was really worried about stupidity I would've stopped 
coming here long ago.)

I don't really care about it any more because I now understand the problem 
some people (in some places) have with precise forms of speech.

It really doesn't matter.

 I don't see it as arguable, so I'm not going to :-)

However, if you really want to check it out, have a look for "undistributed 
middle"...and "all implied but some true". You might also find the following 
links of use:

http://www.amstat.org/misc/FallaciousArguments.pdf ("Red-Headed people have 
bad tempers")
http://mercury.hau.ac.kr/kggc/Publications/SIGG/SIGG12/SIGG12201_HKKang.pdf 
("Dogs bark" = ALL dogs bark)
http://website.lineone.net/~eshp/grammar2.htm "London buses..." See "The Big 
Question," near the bottom of the article...


Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Logic vs. Conversation (Was: Cobol Myth Busters)

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-18T11:02:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lr00f31qtdcs27rchbn81k1iako6fn6lcc@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <5ksgmtF5ad3iU1@mid.individual.net> <rmiie3p33a3udtrbahirf58027shiu2hpr@4ax.com> <5kt15sF5aj47U1@mid.individual.net> <NsGdnTKJ4NNJqnLbnZ2dnUVZ_gydnZ2d@comcast.com> <5l8uevF71vi0U1@mid.individual.net> <k8JHi.259683$VU2.153378@fe02.news.easynews.com> <5la9u4F6dn6cU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Wed, 19 Sep 2007 03:41:56 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>"Making a statement in which 'all' is implied but 'some' is true."
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>me off.

I'm sorry you feel that way.   This thread is the first that gives me
a clue that people will infer that "People like to drink" means "All
people like to drink".    It never crossed my mind before, so I
learned something.

And now you have learned that when an American says "People like to
drink", he is not implying "All People like to drink".   But your
above post indicates that you haven't yet reached that understanding.

I don't imply anything when I say "People like to drink".   It isn't
one of Thouless' dirty tricks, it is a matter of being separated by a
common language.

That is the lesson we should learn from this thread.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:lr00f31qtdcs27rchbn81k1iako6fn6lcc@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 19 Sep 2007 03:41:56 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
<snip>
> And now you have learned that when an American says "People like to
> drink", he is not implying "All People like to drink".   But your
> above post indicates that you haven't yet reached that understanding.
>
Howard,
   What I have taken away from this is that when communicating within CLC, the 
statement
        People like to  drink",
is ambiguous (i.e. that some readers read it one way and some the other way.  It 
appears that MORE Americans seem to use it to mean "Some people like to  drink", 
than English speakers from other countries (who participate in CLC).  However, 
that this interpretation is neither universal among Americans nor OBVIOUSLY (to 
me) regional within the US.

On the other hand, when posting in CLC, both of the followin statements seem to 
be "clear" with agreed meaning,
        "All people like to  drink",
                and
        "Some people like to  drink",

Therefore, my conclussion is that one of those two forms is a better means of 
communication within this group than the original statement (no matter WHICH one 
you mean, use one of these two, not the original).

Like you, I don't think that I had externalized this in my thinking before this 
thread.
```

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AXTHi.438709$sR4.77418@fe08.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <5ksgmtF5ad3iU1@mid.individual.net> <rmiie3p33a3udtrbahirf58027shiu2hpr@4ax.com> <5kt15sF5aj47U1@mid.individual.net> <NsGdnTKJ4NNJqnLbnZ2dnUVZ_gydnZ2d@comcast.com> <5l8uevF71vi0U1@mid.individual.net> <k8JHi.259683$VU2.153378@fe02.news.easynews.com> <5la9u4F6dn6cU1@mid.individual.net> <lr00f31qtdcs27rchbn81k1iako6fn6lcc@4ax.com> <yNTHi.26485$tu2.13541@fe09.news.easynews.com>`

```
My "unambiguous" sentences should have talked about using:
   Some people like to drink
        vs
    Most people like to drink
        vs
   All people that I know like to drink
        vs
   Most of the people that I know like to drink
         vs
    (etc)

And using whichever one that is MEANT to best express (communicate) what is 
desired - with each of these being BETTER than
    People like to drink
if one of the above is what is intended.
```

```
On Tue, 18 Sep 2007 17:20:30 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Therefore, my conclussion is that one of those two forms is a better means of 
>communication within this group than the original statement (no matter WHICH one 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>thread.

As a programmer, I like the fact that in English, double negatives are
not widely accepted.   It is nice to have "clean" logic in language.
But having lived in Mexico, I was introduced to a different standard,
and with maturity have learned that an English vernacular that uses it
isn't so much *wrong*, as different.

By the same token, I note that the U.S. president who was a nuclear
engineer in the Navy pronounced the word "nuclear" with the same
regional pronunciation as the current president.    So I reject this
particular insult when used by Democrats to explain how the candidate
who was smart enough to beat their candidates is so dumb that he can't
pronounce this word the more standard way.   (Surely, it isn't that
hard to find more meaningful insults).

I see so much damage done by Righteous people, that I try to examine
my own desire to declare my ways "right", when other ways work as
well.   I suspect I miss some of these (on both directions), but hope
to continue the good effort.   It really isn't easy.
```

- **References:** `<75vFi.2942$3Y1.159@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <attee3hhoje0r9r6e67f6v1cnrj1jd5j64@4ax.com> <5kpusaF4s10iU1@mid.individual.net> <s8bhe31e2r17phgjsrkquj8pij0btreeea@4ax.com> <5ksgmtF5ad3iU1@mid.individual.net> <rmiie3p33a3udtrbahirf58027shiu2hpr@4ax.com> <5kt15sF5aj47U1@mid.individual.net> <NsGdnTKJ4NNJqnLbnZ2dnUVZ_gydnZ2d@comcast.com> <5l8uevF71vi0U1@mid.individual.net> <k8JHi.259683$VU2.153378@fe02.news.easynews.com> <5la9u4F6dn6cU1@mid.individual.net> <lr00f31qtdcs27rchbn81k1iako6fn6lcc@4ax.com> <yNTHi.26485$tu2.13541@fe09.news.easynews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> Howard,
>   What I have taken away from this is that when communicating within CLC, the statement
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Like you, I don't think that I had externalized this in my thinking before this thread.

I wonder if the difference is rather not in the way people think, but in the way
collective words like "people" are used? There are some differences between
the U.S. and the UK in this area. For example, when referring to conglomerates,
people in the UK would use plural ("NASA are"), while people in the U.S. use
the singular ("NASA is"). Maybe this issue is similar?

I would certainly expect an intelligent, educated American to understand the
phrase "people drink" to mean "some people drink," and would be surprised
if they understood it any other way, unless context indicated so. (I don't mean
to imply that anyone who did not understand it that way is dumb or ignorant, I
only mean someone who would be expected to understand subtle nuances of
syntax and semantics.) If there's any regional variance in this, I haven't noticed
it, and I've traveled around the U.S. quite a bit. Most of my communication
problems with other Americans involves my Southern accent. :-)
```

```
Americans are ambiguous in using sports nick names, although "are" is
more common - at least with teams whose nick names end with a "s"
sound.

But we go to school, but we don't go to university nor to hospital.

I wonder how hard it is for someone who doesn't know a word of English
to learn CoBOL.    I've read that there is a French language CoBOL,
but the rest of the world uses our English version.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:do50f35f14bjbun0u2algm15704o7k8boq@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 18 Sep 2007 12:58:36 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
> <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> but the rest of the world uses our English version.

I have compiled COBOL with both French and German compilers. (I speak both 
these languages. It would be difficult (but certainly to impossible...) to 
follow if you didn't).

They were of limited appeal and I haven't heard anything of them for over 20 
years now.

I don't know whether the French COBOL  compiler is in much use in France or 
Canada; maybe someone here can tell us.

In Spain people used normal COBOL and I never saw or heard of a Spanish 
COBOL compiler.

With some implementations of COBOL it is pretty easy to build a local 
language compiler simply by translating Reserved Word lists.

Maybe someone here could produce a Klingon COBOL compiler :-)?

Pete.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
