<a id="t-6e159bc856"></a>
## Thread: Discussions of COBOL philospphy — 86 this month / 120 total

### Discussions of COBOL philospphy

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-08T18:10:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com>`

```
Just wanted to say that this group has had a number of threads on

    GO TO
        versus
    Perform

Of paragraphs (sections - another philosophical thread) that contain STOP RUN 
(GOBACK, etc) - or "ABEND" routines - or EXEC CICS XCTL, etc.

I believe (but could be mistaken) that all (or close to all) programs that use 
PERFORM for such paragraphs (sections) understand that control never comes 
back - and that "GO TO" would do the same thing.  However, I have (again 
personally) never heard of a program "failing" - either in initial design or 
maintenance because PERFORM was used instead of GO TO (or even "inline" code).

We can talk about this, but I can't imagine any individual programmer will 
change their mind - or that any shop will change its internal standard on such.
```

#### ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-08T18:03:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Sat, 08 Sep 2007 18:10:15 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Just wanted to say that this group has had a number of threads on
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>change their mind - or that any shop will change its internal standard on such.

One type of shop whose style could be changed for the better is the department of a large
company 'stuck' with maintaining Cobol. Its workers came from other programming cultures;
Cobol is not their first language.  They are intelligent, write good code in other
languages, weren't working in the '70s, write bad Cobol because they simply don't know
better. They're following the style they've seen and think that's the only way Cobol can
be written.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-09T12:25:57+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 08 Sep 2007 18:10:15 GMT, "William M. Klein" 
> <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> be written.

Yes, there are probably some of those around, although I haven't personally 
seen any.

I think that in such a place, because COBOL has probably been scheduled for 
phased replacement.They aren't too concerned about whether it is good,bad , 
or indifferent, as long as it does the job.

It's hard to justify spending money on training for what most perceive as a 
dying language.

The trouble is not with the language, which has served us well for decades, 
it is with the expectations that people now have. There are other (better?) 
options for system development (always assuming that you even want to be in 
the system development game, and many CEOs would much rather not be). The 
concept of writing code line by line and changing it line by line is simply 
outdated, labour intensive, and therefore (even with some temporary relief 
from outsourcing offshore), simply too expensive to be viable.

Shops where they have a COBOL legacy will be spending money to get rid of 
it, not to train their people in keeping it.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-09T13:01:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Sun, 9 Sep 2007 12:25:57 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
>news:jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com...

>> One type of shop whose style could be changed for the better is the 
>> department of a large
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>dying language.

This assumes employees begin with a blank slate, upon which is writ only knowledge
provided by employer training. If that were true, employers wouldn't ask about skillset or
experience. 

Here's an example. I worked at a place where database types were abstracted into include
files. For C programs, the include file contained typedefs; for Cobol programs, there was
a copybook for each element containing only a picture e.g. pic x(10). This created
thousands of copybooks. When asked why they didn't use typedefs for Cobol, they said there
is no such thing, they'd never seen a typedef in a Cobol program. 

Sure, they could have read the Standard or compiler manual. They didn't. Their knowledge
of Cobol capability was based on reading Old Style programs. They didn't need training  in
how to use typedef, they were already doing it in C.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-09T15:24:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com>`

```
On Sep 10, 6:01 am, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:

> Here's an example. I worked at a place where database types were abstracted into include
> files. For C programs, the include file contained typedefs; for Cobol programs, there was
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> how to use typedef, they were already doing it in C.

You show your ignorance of the Cobol Standard once again. Typedef was
a MicroFocus extension until the 2002 standard and there are no 2002
standard compilers.

Reading the standard and the compiler manuals appropriate to their
compiler would show that Typedef is not standard Cobol.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-10T13:42:32+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5kjloaF2lo0uU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```


"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com...
> On Sep 10, 6:01 am, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> standard compilers.

Not just a MicroFocus extension. Fujitsu NetCOBOL also implements TYPEDEF. 
(Maybe others do too?) I think Robert's ignorance of the standard can be 
forgiven in this instance; most programmers assume that what is in the 
manual they use daily, is in the COBOL standard, and really don't care 
whether it is or not.

(I have never met a programmer on a COBOL shop floor who was concerned with 
what the COBOL standard mandated. They are concerned with their local 
programming standards (and getting code past peer review). They are further 
concerned with what their particular implementation supports; if it's in the 
manual and doesn't violate local standards, it's OK to use. I have not 
encountered anyone anywhere who is concerned about the fact that they might 
be using "Non-Standard" extensions to the COBOL language, provided by their 
vendor. In fact, the majority would consider it is part of their competitive 
advantage, by going with that vendor, to use extensions provided. The days 
when the possibility that the COBOL might be ported, and standard COBOL was 
therefore viewed as a Good Thing, are long gone. They disappeared about the 
same time the standards committee lost all credibility by taking 17 years to 
produce a standard revision.)

It is a pity that the 2002 standard, which is actually trying to do some 
useful things with the language, has inherited this legacy. I guess you 
could say: "Too little, too late..."

>
> Reading the standard and the compiler manuals appropriate to their
> compiler would show that Typedef is not standard Cobol.
>
>

While it may not be standard COBOL, it is certainly an "interesting" 
construct.

For the benefit of many who may be wondering what this is all about, here is 
a small sample program (reproduced from Fujitsu docs, which are publicly 
downloadable) that explains the concept very well, in my opinion.

000010 @OPTIONS MAIN
000020*----------------------------------------------------------------------
000030* The TYPEDEF clause can define an arbitrary data type.
000040* The defined data type can be referenced by the TYPE clause.
000050*
000060* This sample uses a date type to measure the execution performance of
000065* the ADD statement.
000070*----------------------------------------------------------------------
000080 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000090 PROGRAM-ID. SAMPLE.
000100 DATA DIVISION.
000110 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000120*----------------------------------------------------------------------
000130* The TYPEDEF clause is used to define the date type (DATE-EDITED).
000140*----------------------------------------------------------------------
000150 01 DATE-EDITED TYPEDEF.
000160 02 YEARS PIC 9(4).
000170 02 PIC X(1) VALUE "/".
000180 02 MONTHS PIC 9(2).
000190 02 PIC X(1) VALUE "/".
000200 02 DAYS PIC 9(2).
000210 02 PIC X(1) VALUE " ".
000220 02 HOURS PIC 9(2).
000230 02 PIC X(1) VALUE ":".
000240 02 MINUTES PIC 9(2).
000250 02 PIC X(1) VALUE ":".
000260 02 SECONDS PIC 9(2).
000270 02 PIC X(1) VALUE ".".
000280 02 M-SECS PIC 9(2).
000290*----------------------------------------------------------------------
000300* The date type defined above can be used by specifying the TYPE
000305* clause.
000310*----------------------------------------------------------------------
000320 01 STARTED TYPE DATE-EDITED.
000330 01 ENDED TYPE DATE-EDITED.
000340*----------------------------------------------------------------------
000350 01 WK-DATE.
000360 02 YEARS PIC 9(4).
000370 02 MONTHS PIC 9(2).
000380 02 DAYS PIC 9(2).
000390 02 HOURS PIC 9(2).
000400 02 MINUTES PIC 9(2).
000410 02 SECONDS PIC 9(2).
000420 02 M-SECS PIC 9(2).
000430 01 COUNTER PIC S9(8) VALUE 0.
000440 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000450 MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO WK-DATE.
000460 MOVE CORR WK-DATE TO STARTED.
000470 DISPLAY "STARTED-TIME IS " STARTED.
000480**
000490 PERFORM 1000000 TIMES
000500 ADD 1 TO COUNTER
000510 END-PERFORM.
000520**
000530 MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO WK-DATE.
000540 MOVE CORR WK-DATE TO ENDED.
000550 DISPLAY "ENDED-TIME IS " ENDED.
000560 EXIT PROGRAM.
000570 END PROGRAM SAMPLE.


Pete.
```

<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Richard" <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> whether it is or not.

If it is MicroFocus then his ignorance cannot be 'forgiven'. The MF
manuals are quite explicit about what is in the standard and what is
an extension. All extensions are boxed and marked as to what set of
extensions it belongs to. In the case of TYPEDEF it is marked with
"MF".

Fujitsu may well have copied some MicroFocus extensions.

From a QUICK look:

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ TYPEDEF - Micro Focus and Starndard (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-10T05:31:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oE4Fi.53478$qQ1.15109@fe05.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <5kjloaF2lo0uU1@mid.individual.net> <1189389832.912510.236190@57g2000hsv.googlegroups.com>`

```
Just wanted to point out (confirming somewhat what is below) that Micro Focus is 
QUITE clear that there definition of "TYPEDEF" is an extension and does NOT 
conform to the ISO 2002 Standard definition of such.  (They don't even have a 
TYPE clause as specified in the '02 Standard).

P.S.  "Reading" the LRM of the compiler that you ARE using seems a good idea to 
me (and also one that I don't think has ever been very common).  I do know of 
people who STILL make their livings training COBOL programmers in "new features" 
available in specific implementations.  However, I would also agree that most 
shops to actively promote this and few programmers try and pursue it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philosophy

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-10T05:06:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9h4Fi.518514$p47.415377@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5kjloaF2lo0uU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <5kjloaF2lo0uU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
> news:1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com...
…[118 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Perhaps I am missing something here about the benefits of typedef in 
COBOL (or perhaps I don't understand typedefs at all).

As far as I can tell, it appears the only advantage to using typedef 
is that it creates invisible variables under the group names STARTED 
and ENDED with predictable non-unique names, in order to save two 
lines of code in the procedure division that would read "MOVE 
EDITED-DATE TO STARTED" and "MOVE EDITED-DATE TO ENDED".  And to get 
this advantage I would have to use qualification and MOVE 
CORRESPONDING, which is strongly discouraged by my shop standards.

If I wanted to simulate typedefs or use them directly, I'm not sure I 
would want to put each one in a copybook by itself.  Surely in this 
day and age we are not that short of WORKING-STORAGE memory.

With kindest regards,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ WHY TYPEDEFs (was: Discussions of COBOL philosophy

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-10T05:47:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yT4Fi.53480$qQ1.27232@fe05.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <5kjloaF2lo0uU1@mid.individual.net> <9h4Fi.518514$p47.415377@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold,
  I don't (quickly) have many advantages for WEAK "typedefs" - but the '02 
Standard also supports "strongly typed" data items.  This means that MOVEs and 
more importantly CALL statements (with CALL prototypes) can check that passed 
and received arguments can be checked for "conformance" at compile time (or in 
some cases run-time).  This certainly can help prevent "data pollution" and is 
one advantage of them.

Other than the fact that they correspond to features of OTHER languages, I have 
some difficulty figuring out any advantage of weak types as opposed to 
copybooks - and would be interested in input from those familiar with this 
feature in other languages as to what this does gain the language or the 
programmer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WHY TYPEDEFs (was: Discussions of COBOL philosophy

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T14:17:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13edn41sli7us4e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <5kjloaF2lo0uU1@mid.individual.net> <9h4Fi.518514$p47.415377@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <yT4Fi.53480$qQ1.27232@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:yT4Fi.53480$qQ1.27232@fe05.news.easynews.com...
[snip]
> Other than the fact that they correspond to features of OTHER languages, I
have
> some difficulty figuring out any advantage of weak types as opposed to
> copybooks - and would be interested in input from those familiar with this
> feature in other languages as to what this does gain the language or the
> programmer.

Types and operations on types are concepts from Mathematics
that apply to Computer Science. [For those who have the book
"Algorithms + Data Structures = Programs" by Niklaus Wirth,
there is introductory material on types in Chapter 1. For those
who don't have the book, your on your own!] So, for a start,
the inclusion of types into COBOL makes the language more
"computer-sciencey".

COBOL has always had types, in the mathematical sense; but
the user-defined types given in record descriptions are not
always readily identifiable as such. (For example, maintenance
may have added a field to a type; but the field is added to the
end of the record, rather than grouped with rest of the type.)
Using typedefs to group and name user-defined types makes
these easier to use by CS-types attempting to analyze COBOL
programs. [I read an article or three about such efforts in
connection with formal methods and automated transformations
of COBOL to other languages.]

Recognizing types and operations on those types is one means
of identifying potential OO classes in existing COBOL code.
Using typedefs, particularly for groups, provides emphasis.
In OO terms this has been called containment (Martin) and
object composition (Gamma, et al). Some authors consider
these relationships to be more common and more useful than
inheritance in "real" systems.

These go beyond the uses in other languages. There is not a
great deal of difference between typedef and copy in COBOL;
except that with typedef fewer copy statements are required.
But, using typedefs opens up a number of possibilities that are
not apparent if one thinks of typedef only in terms of copy.
```

On Mon, 10 Sep 2007 05:06:13 GMT, Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
wrote:

>Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[134 more quoted lines elided]…
>day and age we are not that short of WORKING-STORAGE memory.

 For two edited dates with one description, a simpler solution is:

    05  date-edited occurs 2. 
          (structure description here)

This is the usage I was talking about.
With typedef, we have a single copy file:

01  name-type  typedef  pic x(50).
01  address-type typedef pic x(30).
01  working-telephone-type typedef pic x(10).

.. and references like this:

01  customer-row.
     05  cust-name      type name-type.
    05  cust-address occurs 3 type address-type.
    05  WTN  type working-telephone-type.

Without typedef, we have hundreds of .cpy files

name-type.cpy
     pic x(50).
address-type.cpy
    pic x(30).
working-telephone.cpy
   pic x(10).

.. and references like this:

01  customer-row.
     05  cust-name            copy name-type.
    05  cust-address occurs 3 copy address-type.
    05  WTN                      copy working-telephone-type.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-10T20:48:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%4iFi.310031$jE4.60348@fe12.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <5kjloaF2lo0uU1@mid.individual.net> <o9fFi.15963$Y7.1646@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`

```
IT seems to me that during the days of "Windows 3.1" and Unix (early or 
pre-Linux) when Micro Focus had implemented the entire '85 Standard (allowing 
for "structured" code) *AND* they had most of their "C-ification" in place (so 
that you could use all non-COBOL API's) *and* there were low or no run-time fees 
*AND* that Fujitsu did their 3.0 free compiler with much the same support, that 
it was ALMOST all "perception" that kept COBOL from being used widely in 
non-mainframe environments.  I know that there were MORE people who did port 
COBOL in those days (or at least it seemed so to me) but COBOL was still NOT 
viewed as a "popular" language for workstation development.

Once (larger) run-time fees and OO came in, things got worse - but I don't think 
that those were really what stopped COBOL from being a common language in 
non-mainframe environments.

P.S.  I may have my timeframes mixed up on when some of these features came in, 
but I think the "general" view is correct.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T19:18:02+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5kmtpdF47p1tU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <5kjloaF2lo0uU1@mid.individual.net> <o9fFi.15963$Y7.1646@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:o9fFi.15963$Y7.1646@bignews3.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> have been "Enough, in time."

You may well be right.

It is pretty easy to speculate given hindsight; there is really no way of 
knowing...

Here's a possible scenario...

1. Suppose, there had been a release of the COBOL 90 standard, that included 
OO.
2. Suppose this had been eagerly embraced by the community at that time. 
(This would have had a "knock on" efffect in causing new methodologies for 
which OO is well suited to be investigated.)
3. Suppose it was possible for people to download free or very reasonably 
priced versions of compilers written to this standard, without run time fees 
being charged.
4. Suppose OO COBOL had been so eagerly embraced that network development 
and remote procedure calling would be implemented in it, as well as the 
traditional batch processing role.
5. Suppose a decent IDE and support tools had been available, that worked 
across platforms providing the same support in mainframe and network 
environments.
6. Suppose the obvious success of COBOL led to the implementation of graphic 
tools that could build COBOL components and assemble them into 
sub-assemblies in seconds. This would allow COBOL support for non-procedural 
solutions.

Would the story have been any different?

Pete.
```

than switching to other languages through the 80s (not every computer
science graduate is going to enter a career writing compilers).
Suppose the systems on which we ran cobol had been much cheaper.

If ifs and ands were pots and pans....

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T11:59:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aelde3pgaaf6ml9tge10njqooqavsv0krt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <5kjloaF2lo0uU1@mid.individual.net> <o9fFi.15963$Y7.1646@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5kmtpdF47p1tU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Tue, 11 Sep 2007 19:18:02 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Here's a possible scenario...
...

>Would the story have been any different?


Maybe.    But maybe the biggest strength of CoBOL is also its biggest
problem.

CoBOL is rugged - it is used to write programs that are expected to
last forever.    Maybe it is harder to get rid of this concept than
any other characteristic of the language.

Sometimes we need to be like the person who moves every 5 years as an
alternative to cleaning out the garage.    Throw everything else and
start over.    Switching databases, platforms, and language makes sure
we do a thorough job of this.

One reason Macintoshes are virtually virus free is because Apple
decided to throw out its core code and start over.   Meanwhile Windows
tries to do the same thing a bit at a time.    "Fixing" the CoBOL
environment is the slow change philosophy.   That might not be
optimal.

This does not address whether CoBOL is an effective tool, only whether
we are better off making sure we have a clean start.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ RW error (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-10T05:42:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:vch9e314671b25do4scg6p8ul4h62gq7g1@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 09 Sep 2007 15:24:42 -0700, Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>
>>Reading the standard and the compiler manuals appropriate to their
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Typedefs are NOT compatible between Micro Focus and Fujitsu
    but
More importantly, even to this day there is no IBM compiler that supports them 
(on any platform).

If you (RW) think there is - or was - please provide a link to an LRM showing 
support for them.

P.S.  The iSeries compiler does support "LIKE" - which is VERY similar to the 
'02 Standard "SAME AS" - but certainly not the same as TYPEDEF.
```

On Mon, 10 Sep 2007 05:42:32 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
>news:vch9e314671b25do4scg6p8ul4h62gq7g1@4ax.com...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>support for them.

ILE COBOL for AS/400
Programmer's Guide
Version 4
dated 1999

What's New in V4R2?

ï¿½ User-defined data types
A user-defined data type is defined by specifying a level-01 entry containing the
TYPEDEF clause; all entries that are subordinate to the level-01 entry are considered
part of the user-defined data type. A user-defined data type can be
used to define new data items of level-01, -77, or -02 through -49, by specifying
a TYPE clause for the new data item, that references the user-defined data
type.

http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/infocenter/iadthelp/v6r0/index.jsp?topic=/com.ibm.etools.iseries.pgmgd.doc/c092540528.htm

ILE COBOL Language Reference

+-------------------------------IBM Extension--------------------------------+

TYPEDEF Clause

The TYPEDEF clause is used to create a new user-defined data type, type-name. The name of
the new user-defined data type is the subject of the TYPEDEF clause. Data-name-1 must be
specified with the TYPEDEF clause: FILLER cannot be used. The TYPEDEF clause must
immediately follow data-name-1. After defining a new data type using the TYPEDEF clause,
data items can be declared as this new data type using the TYPE clause. For more
information about the TYPE clause, refer to TYPE Clause. 

http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/infocenter/iadthelp/v7r0/index.jsp?topic=/com.ibm.etools.iseries.langref.doc/c0925395283.htm

>P.S.  The iSeries compiler does support "LIKE" - which is VERY similar to the 
>'02 Standard "SAME AS" - but certainly not the same as TYPEDEF.

It also had a date data type, XML and pointers.
```

- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <vch9e314671b25do4scg6p8ul4h62gq7g1@4ax.com> <cP4Fi.82726$M%1.72245@fe10.news.easynews.com> <vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com>`

```
On Sep 11, 5:28 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Tue, 11 Sep 2007 04:58:20 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> know typedef was available.

What would be the point of rewriting all the code to remove the
hundreds of COPYs and replace them with hundreds of TYPEs ?

And then of course it would need to be completely retested and
debugged.

THEN, after reading the '02 standard to learn TYPEDEF they find that
MF doesn't support '02 TYPEDEF, only a MF extension that isn't '02,
and all their changes have to be redone.

But then again, why would they care to use a solution designed for a
completely different language. TYPEDEF is a solution for the C
language and was implemented by MF and Fujitsu (along with 78 and some
other stuff) so it could deal with the MS Windows C based #include
files for writing Windows API code.

Using COPY, as you described, is a Cobol solution to the problem.

TYPEDEF is a 35 year old solution. You may think yourself 'modern' and
'progressive' by using it, but C++ (25 year old) discards it and Java
(a better C++) doesn't even implement it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW error (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T07:58:50-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13ed0un9251ec6d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <vch9e314671b25do4scg6p8ul4h62gq7g1@4ax.com> <cP4Fi.82726$M%1.72245@fe10.news.easynews.com> <vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <1189492597.664492.70260@19g2000hsx.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:1189492597.664492.70260@19g2000hsx.googlegroups.com...
[snip]
> Using COPY, as you described, is a Cobol solution to the problem.
>
> TYPEDEF is a 35 year old solution. You may think yourself 'modern' and
> 'progressive' by using it, but C++ (25 year old) discards it and Java
> (a better C++) doesn't even implement it.

C++ does not discard typedefs; they may be used as
synonyms for both intrinsic types and user-defined types.
----- (Checked using Microsoft Visual C++ 5.0)
typedef int int32;

struct REC1 { int32 a, b; };
REC1 inrec1, outrec1;

typedef struct { int32 a, b; } REC2;
REC2 inrec2, outrec2;
-----
int32 is a synonym for int.
REC1 is the name of a user-defined type.
inrec1 and outrec1 are instances of REC1.
REC2 is a synonym for an unnamed user-defined type.
inrec2 and outrec2 are implementations of REC2.

[C requires the 'struct' keyword before REC1; but is the
same as C++ when using typedef.]

In this limited context, COBOL library text (COPY)
is similar to the REC1 construct and COBOL typedefs
are similar to the REC2 construct.

Using a single COPY statement to include all typedefs
versus individual COPY statements was mentioned
elsewhere.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW error (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T11:01:43-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13edblkfgrebrc5@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <vch9e314671b25do4scg6p8ul4h62gq7g1@4ax.com> <cP4Fi.82726$M%1.72245@fe10.news.easynews.com> <vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <1189492597.664492.70260@19g2000hsx.googlegroups.com> <13ed0un9251ec6d@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:13ed0un9251ec6d@corp.supernews.com...
[snip]
> ----- (Checked using Microsoft Visual C++ 5.0)
> typedef int int32;
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> -----
[snip]
> In this limited context, COBOL library text (COPY)
> is similar to the REC1 construct and COBOL typedefs
> are similar to the REC2 construct.

Upon re-reading this, I realize that I probably should not
have written "similar to"; I think "closer to" is better.
However, these C++ constructs are so similar it is difficult
to translate them to analogous COBOL constructs. Given
that the action of a typedef (as in the REC2 construct) is
closer to a COBOL REPLACE statement, that would leave
the COBOL COPY statement as closer to the REC1
construct. Though I am probably splitting hairs, here. <g>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW error (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T19:23:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1189563799.539901.88770@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<13ed0un9251ec6d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <vch9e314671b25do4scg6p8ul4h62gq7g1@4ax.com> <cP4Fi.82726$M%1.72245@fe10.news.easynews.com> <vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <1189492597.664492.70260@19g2000hsx.googlegroups.com> <13ed0un9251ec6d@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sep 11, 11:58 pm, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
> "Richard" <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> typedef int int32;

'Discard' was perhaps the wrong word. Obviously typedef is still in
the C language underlying C++.

Many of the 'problems' in C that typedef is used to 'fix' has a
different solution in C++.  For example in C typedef may be used to
make function pointers more readable. You shouldn't be using function
pointers in C++.

>
> struct REC1 { int32 a, b; };
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> same as C++ when using typedef.]

Exactly. In C a typedef may be used just so the struct keyword does
not have to be specified each time, but in C++ a struct is equivalent
to a class with all public members and so the typedef usage is
redundant (and thus discarded).


> In this limited context, COBOL library text (COPY)
> is similar to the REC1 construct and COBOL typedefs
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> elsewhere.

It seems from what Robert said that the generator output a COPY file
for every database field. Thus there would be a Product_Code.cpy file
containing "PIC X(12)." and a Product_Description.cpy containing "PIC
X(40)." and so on.

This would cater for the database changing the product code to 16
wherever it was used without changing all X(12) fields to X(16). I
don't see that typedef would be any better, just different for the
sake of using a 1970's solution.

As it happens I do generate COPY files from SQL tables (using Python)
but each contains data items for a complete table not just a PIC.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW error (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "robertwessel2@yahoo.com" <robertwessel2@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T21:49:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1189572573.695200.140090@57g2000hsv.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1189563799.539901.88770@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <vch9e314671b25do4scg6p8ul4h62gq7g1@4ax.com> <cP4Fi.82726$M%1.72245@fe10.news.easynews.com> <vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <1189492597.664492.70260@19g2000hsx.googlegroups.com> <13ed0un9251ec6d@corp.supernews.com> <1189563799.539901.88770@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Sep 11, 9:23 pm, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> Exactly. In C a typedef may be used just so the struct keyword does
> not have to be specified each time, but in C++ a struct is equivalent
> to a class with all public members and so the typedef usage is
> redundant (and thus discarded).


It's more that structs (and classes) have an implicit typedef in C++.

It is true that typedefs are rarer in C++ than in C code, but they're
still often useful for removing redundant/repetitive type definitions,
just like in C.  A common use is to define a container object as a
typedef, then the container itself with a reference to the type, and
then iterators using the typedef as a base - that way you're not
having to repeat the definition of the container when you create an
iterator for it.
```

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T23:34:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l7qee3l2khkgihmalldbkbisssja9rchsu@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <vch9e314671b25do4scg6p8ul4h62gq7g1@4ax.com> <cP4Fi.82726$M%1.72245@fe10.news.easynews.com> <vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <1189492597.664492.70260@19g2000hsx.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Mon, 10 Sep 2007 23:36:37 -0700, Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Sep 11, 5:28 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Tue, 11 Sep 2007 04:58:20 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>hundreds of COPYs and replace them with hundreds of TYPEs ?

1. A developer could SEE all pictures by loading two files into the editor -- the source
and the typedef file. 

2. Since semantics are the same between C and Cobol, it is less noisy to use the same
grammar.


>And then of course it would need to be completely retested and
>debugged.

Negative-finding regression tests are easy, the output files are identical.

>THEN, after reading the '02 standard to learn TYPEDEF they find that
>MF doesn't support '02 TYPEDEF, only a MF extension that isn't '02,
>and all their changes have to be redone.

There is a compiler option to replace the word TYPE with a null string. (They don't use
report writer.)

>But then again, why would they care to use a solution designed for a
>completely different language. TYPEDEF is a solution for the C
>language and was implemented by MF and Fujitsu (along with 78 and some
>other stuff) so it could deal with the MS Windows C based #include
>files for writing Windows API code.

They would want to use it because C-like languages are their native tongue.

>Using COPY, as you described, is a Cobol solution to the problem.

It's clunky. They thought it was the only way to get TYPEDEF functionality.

>TYPEDEF is a 35 year old solution. You may think yourself 'modern' and
>'progressive' by using it, but C++ (25 year old) discards it and Java
>(a better C++) doesn't even implement it.

Of course OO languages don't use it, they have a more elegant way of abstracting data
types. The company could define each distinct type as a class in OO Cobol, but they're not
going to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW error (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T23:02:46-07:00

On Sep 12, 4:34 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Mon, 10 Sep 2007 23:36:37 -0700, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> >On Sep 11, 5:28 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> and the typedef file.

grep . *.cpy >allcopy

Then the developer can SEE all pictures by load two files ...

<shrug>

> 2. Since semantics are the same between C and Cobol, it is less noisy to use the same
> grammar.

I prefer to have Cobol semantics in Cobol programs. Maybe they do too.

I also prefer to have a solution that has been done and works rather
than re-engineering all the code to a different solution with no
_actual_ benefit.

> >And then of course it would need to be completely retested and
> >debugged.
>
> Negative-finding regression tests are easy, the output files are identical.

Only for the conditions being tested.

But why go through all that change just because some grandstanding
pundit thinks it would be a 'good idea'.

> >THEN, after reading the '02 standard to learn TYPEDEF they find that
> >MF doesn't support '02 TYPEDEF, only a MF extension that isn't '02,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> They would want to use it because C-like languages are their native tongue.

No. They seem to want to _NOT_ do it. I got the impression that it was
only _you_ that wanted them to do it.


> >Using COPY, as you described, is a Cobol solution to the problem.
>
> It's clunky. They thought it was the only way to get TYPEDEF functionality.

The only bit that seems 'clunky' is 'seeing two files in the editor'.
That just isn't hard to fix.

In any case with my editor I have one keystroke to open a file whose
name in under the cursor.  How hard is that ?

It also has one keystroke to search for a text item under the cursor
in all open files so where there is

       01  Customer-Code COPY "Customer_Code.cpy".

or similar I can search in my grep output file or open the copy file
with a keystroke.  How 'clunky' are your tools ?


> >TYPEDEF is a 35 year old solution. You may think yourself 'modern' and
> >'progressive' by using it, but C++ (25 year old) discards it and Java
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> going to.

It seems they're not going to use typedef either.

<shrug>

> On Tue, 11 Sep 2007 23:02:46 -0700, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> >It also has one keystroke to search for a text item under the cursor

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Because I don't want to search all files in the path or all files in
the directory, whether it could do that is irrelevant. I want it to do
what _I_ want not some random actions that you may think up.

In the case of having open a single file created by a grep, or by
something similar to ctags, then searching in the open files is what I
would want. It achieves the purpose of seeing the source and the
picture of the item.

Opening the COPY file with one stroke would also achieve that too. Two
different solutions to your perceived problem that you think requires
re-engineering the whole system.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW error (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-12T08:21:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46pfe350qaindq6ispq2bqv11mh5msspbv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cP4Fi.82726$M%1.72245@fe10.news.easynews.com> <vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <1189492597.664492.70260@19g2000hsx.googlegroups.com> <l7qee3l2khkgihmalldbkbisssja9rchsu@4ax.com> <1189576966.594281.3170@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <vd2fe3h3g1mpmabpu9lknoetin396hahr2@4ax.com> <1189581216.236719.252820@19g2000hsx.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 12 Sep 2007 00:13:36 -0700, Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Sep 12, 6:44 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Tue, 11 Sep 2007 23:02:46 -0700, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>what _I_ want not some random actions that you may think up.

You want to do the same thing the compiler does, which is search the copy path. Micro
Focus calls it COBCPY. 

>In the case of having open a single file created by a grep, or by
>something similar to ctags, then searching in the open files is what I
>would want. It achieves the purpose of seeing the source and the
>picture of the item.

The grep list cannot be prepared in advance. It sounds like you're shelling to a grep like
this 'find . -name \*.cpy -follow | xargs grep foo >mygrep', then opening and searching
mygrep. My editor does that internally. The file name need not be under the cursor, it can
be anywhere on the line. It tries he word under the cursor first, then quoted words, then
upper case, lastly lower case. It looks in open files first, before going to the file
system. When viewing search results, you can put the cursor on a line and hit one key to
see it in context, a second press of the same key takes you back to the search results. 

Here's the best part: the search document is editable, including with group changes. It
doesn't contain copies of the lines, it contains pointers to them. You're actually looking
at lines in the original files. One action can make changes to 100 files. 

>
```

```
On Sep 13, 1:21 am, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Wed, 12 Sep 2007 00:13:36 -0700, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> >On Sep 12, 6:44 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Focus calls it COBCPY.

You are confused and arguing the wrong point yet again. Two different
things: "open a file" and "search for a text item". I do not want to
search for a text item in all the files in the path, or directory
tree, I only want to search for a text item in the open files.

When I open a file it knows where to look.

> >In the case of having open a single file created by a grep, or by
> >something similar to ctags, then searching in the open files is what I
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The grep list cannot be prepared in advance.

Of course it can.  It can be done before starting the edit or by the
run of the generator program, or anytime in between. Try and work out
what the grep is actually doing.

> It sounds like you're shelling to a grep like
> this 'find . -name \*.cpy -follow | xargs grep foo >mygrep', then opening and searching
> mygrep.

No. Wrong. That is not what it is doing. I gave you the command line.
It is building a complete list of all the definitions regardless of
whether a particular program uses them and winds up with a single file
with the same data in it that your speculative typedef file would
have.

[irrelevant self promotion snipped]

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8U2Gi.111199$M%1.99823@fe10.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<vch9e314671b25do4scg6p8ul4h62gq7g1@4ax.com> <cP4Fi.82726$M%1.72245@fe10.news.easynews.com> <vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <1189492597.664492.70260@19g2000hsx.googlegroups.com> <l7qee3l2khkgihmalldbkbisssja9rchsu@4ax.com> <1189576966.594281.3170@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <vd2fe3h3g1mpmabpu9lknoetin396hahr2@4ax.com> <euZFi.109266$M%1.69918@fe10.news.easynews.com> <iitge35rthucrb8h5rv9d32ldhncln16n4@4ax.com>`

```
Check out the "Chapter 7: Editor" in the Server Express 5.0  Utilities Guide. 
Look for the bullet that says,

"Copy library support, which enables you quick access to parts of program source 
code held in separate files."

My use of "IDE" might have mislead you - or you (or the site you were talking 
about might not LIKE the Micro Focus interface), but it does exist and does 
provide the specific facility that I mentioned.  (And checking the manual would 
have found it - just as it would have found LRM references to TYPEDEFs - as an 
extension)

P.S.  I can't remember which release of "Unix" products first provided this 
functionality, but I believe it has been there for MANY years.
```

VisualStudio IDE.  I thought you were looking for a "Unix only" solution - which 
as I say DOES exist and does provide the facility (no matter HOW old it is) that 
you want (for handling COPY statements).

If you are doing your editing on Windows and your compiling and debugging on 
Unix, that seems counter-productive to me - but I have never had to do it that 
way, so I won't swear to it.

The bottom-line is simply that IF the only issue were (and I am not claiming 
that you said it was) that your editor would allow you to see TYPEDEFs and "main 
source" at the same time while it wouldn't for COPY and main source, then that 
does mean that a better selection of available editors would solve this problem 
for you (and would have any time in the last decade-plus)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW error (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-13T20:24:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sgmje3hkn4599lgr036k4u8gj81b431uhr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <1189492597.664492.70260@19g2000hsx.googlegroups.com> <l7qee3l2khkgihmalldbkbisssja9rchsu@4ax.com> <1189576966.594281.3170@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <vd2fe3h3g1mpmabpu9lknoetin396hahr2@4ax.com> <euZFi.109266$M%1.69918@fe10.news.easynews.com> <iitge35rthucrb8h5rv9d32ldhncln16n4@4ax.com> <8U2Gi.111199$M%1.99823@fe10.news.easynews.com> <l8hhe3ljuiqb5un8bnr3s4manuos4ji84i@4ax.com> <FM3Gi.144185$1J4.25489@fe06.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Thu, 13 Sep 2007 05:20:38 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>If you have windows,

EVERYone has Windows.

> then I would expect to use either the Windows IDE or the 
>VisualStudio IDE.  

It's bundled with Net Express, which costs thousands of dollars per seat.

>I thought you were looking for a "Unix only" solution - which 
>as I say DOES exist and does provide the facility (no matter HOW old it is) that 
>you want (for handling COPY statements).

Thank you.

>If you are doing your editing on Windows and your compiling and debugging on 
>Unix, that seems counter-productive to me - but I have never had to do it that 
>way, so I won't swear to it.

It's the norm. Samba and NFS mount the Unix file system on a Windows driver letter. It
looks like another drive on the Windows file system. We can edit with Windows editors,
drag and drop directories to and from other drives, etc. 

The link is bidirectional -- the Windows file system looks like another mountpoint
(directory) to Unix. A program running on Unix can read and write files to a Windows disk.
Techniques like FTP have been replaced by a copy command. 

Some (rare) enlightened shops do all development and TESTING on Windows, even though the
target platform is Unix. When it's time to deploy, they do a final compilation on Unix but
never test it there. So long as the program doesn't issue OS commands nor make API calls,
the programs always work the same. Java people are comfortable with the concept of
cross-platform compatibility, most Cobolers aren't.

>The bottom-line is simply that IF the only issue were (and I am not claiming 
>that you said it was) that your editor would allow you to see TYPEDEFs and "main 
>source" at the same time while it wouldn't for COPY and main source, then that 
>does mean that a better selection of available editors would solve this problem 
>for you (and would have any time in the last decade-plus) 

All modern editors can open multiple files at once. They balk at opening hundreds to
thousands of files, even containing one line. The task bar fills up and navigation is
difficult.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW error (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 18)_

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW error (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-12T00:22:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v2ree3lqtjh8nujm8nlghflta2na4dg726@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <vch9e314671b25do4scg6p8ul4h62gq7g1@4ax.com> <cP4Fi.82726$M%1.72245@fe10.news.easynews.com> <vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <NtwFi.62984$CK1.34073@fe04.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Tue, 11 Sep 2007 13:11:09 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>As the MF extension doesn't seem to support STRONG typing (only week), can you 
>tell me what advantage that has over COPY.  (See my other note asking for any 
>GENERAL advantages of "weak" typedefs.  I know the advantages of strong typing).

The advantage is a single copybook describing all types, a opposed to a separate copybook
for each type.

Micro Focus offers strong typing in USAGE OBJECT REFERENCE  class-name ONLY. Adding strong
typing to non-OO Cobol is no guarantee of type safety because programmers can bypass it
with pointers, just as C++ programmers do by dropping into C.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW error (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-12T22:07:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MqZFi.109178$M%1.62167@fe10.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <vch9e314671b25do4scg6p8ul4h62gq7g1@4ax.com> <cP4Fi.82726$M%1.72245@fe10.news.easynews.com> <vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <NtwFi.62984$CK1.34073@fe04.news.easynews.com> <v2ree3lqtjh8nujm8nlghflta2na4dg726@4ax.com>`

```
As MF and the current (and recent) Standards allow nested COPY statements, you 
could certainly have a single COPY member with all the COPY statements for the 
individual structures (as long as you didn't become recursive, this could go 
down multiple levels).

Also, STRONG types in the '02 Standard do provide protection *IF* the programmer 
uses them.  If they decide to avoid those rules, then (like REDEFINES and a 
variety of COBOL features) the results are unpredictable.

Certainly doesn't sound to me as if there are many (if any) reason for a shop 
with competent COBOL programmers (even if they do also use other languages) to 
change from standard COPY members to the MF non-Standard TYPEDEF extension.  (If 
a shop DID already use COBOL TYPEDEFs, then I wouldn't see much advantage to 
changing to COPY statements either.  This is simply a two equivalent styles to 
do the same thing.)
```

On Wed, 12 Sep 2007 22:07:41 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>As MF and the current (and recent) Standards allow nested COPY statements, you 
>could certainly have a single COPY member with all the COPY statements for the 
>individual structures (as long as you didn't become recursive, this could go 
>down multiple levels).

You can't do that when the copy file contains nothing but the PIC clause.

>Also, STRONG types in the '02 Standard do provide protection *IF* the programmer 
>uses them.  If they decide to avoid those rules, then (like REDEFINES and a 
>variety of COBOL features) the results are unpredictable.

What's that based on? IBM and Micro Focus don't support strong typing. Fujitsu is the only
compiler that does. 

>Certainly doesn't sound to me as if there are many (if any) reason for a shop 
>with competent COBOL programmers (even if they do also use other languages) to 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>do the same thing.)

As I said, they already have C typedefs. They created Cobol copy files because they
thought it was the only way. Now they have two sets of code to maintain.
```

- **References:** `<r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <1189376682.979533.132800@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <vch9e314671b25do4scg6p8ul4h62gq7g1@4ax.com> <cP4Fi.82726$M%1.72245@fe10.news.easynews.com> <vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <NtwFi.62984$CK1.34073@fe04.news.easynews.com> <v2ree3lqtjh8nujm8nlghflta2na4dg726@4ax.com> <MqZFi.109178$M%1.62167@fe10.news.easynews.com> <2guge3pcm5ifpmau9ei2ahjpscdkbc6ssg@4ax.com>`

```
On Sep 13, 12:01 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Wed, 12 Sep 2007 22:07:41 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> thought it was the only way. Now they have two sets of code to maintain.

Geez, Robert, that is just silly. It is as you are grasping for some,
any, spurious justification for your ideas.

1. They can't use C typedefs in a Cobol program so they have to have
different Cobol files from the C files whether it is COPY or typedef.

2. From what you said the Cobol COPY files were generated, as
presumably was the C typedef. They don't have to 'maintain' these,
they just regenerate.

Another of your claimed 'advantage' was that typedef would allow just
two files to be opened (source plus typedef), that was solved with one
minutes thought by using grep or adding a grep to the generator
script, or code directly into the generator program to output a
reference file.

It seems to me that you don't want people to have _a_ solution to each
problem, you want them to have _your_ solution regardless of its
merits (or lack thereof) or costs.
```

On Sep 13, 3:16 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Wed, 12 Sep 2007 17:25:44 -0700, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> >On Sep 13, 12:01 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> They don't regenerate for maintenance releases; they change or create the files manually.

I had thought they would have generated these automatically, it isn't
hard to do so.

But regardless of that the C typedef can't be used by Cobol so they
still need different files for C and Cobol. They could generate from
the C typedef file to either a Cobol typedef or to the Copybooks. I
don't see much difference here between what they appear to be doing
and using typedefs.

But to use typedefs they would have to re-engineer the system, for
what ? to keep you happy ?


> >Another of your claimed 'advantage' was that typedef would allow just
> >two files to be opened (source plus typedef), that was solved with one
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> You are grepping copybooks with whole structures.

No I'm not.

> These copybooks do not have element
> names, just pictures (type definitions). There are hundreds to thousands of them.

You haven't worked out what the grep does yet, you are too obsessed
with 'how clever your editor is' that you can't see anything
different.

>From what you say the programs have something like:

       05  Product-Code   COPY Product-Code.cpy.

where Product-Code.cpy is one of hundreds of files and has a content
of something like:

            PIC X(16).

Your 'advantage' was that a typedef would have all these types in one
file easily searched. I will have to explain to you what the output of
the grep will be, it will be one file with a line for each copybook
like:

...
Product-Code.cpy:             PIC X(16).
Product-Description.cpy:      PIC X(40).
Product-Group.cpy:            PIC X(4).
...

Thus from the program source file one could hit a keystroke and the
picture would be displayed (in a small window, say) that matches the
copybook name.

So, what 'advantage' does your typedef file have for this ?


> >It seems to me that you don't want people to have _a_ solution to each
> >problem, you want them to have _your_ solution regardless of its
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> solution.

I am not sure that _they_ think it is a problem.

Yours may well be a solution, but you claim advantages for it that are
easily done in other ways.
```

- **References:** `<vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <NtwFi.62984$CK1.34073@fe04.news.easynews.com> <v2ree3lqtjh8nujm8nlghflta2na4dg726@4ax.com> <MqZFi.109178$M%1.62167@fe10.news.easynews.com> <2guge3pcm5ifpmau9ei2ahjpscdkbc6ssg@4ax.com> <1189643144.934583.268190@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <ieahe3dprferli0c4svqcemckkcqk6uftm@4ax.com> <1189656641.718248.101190@57g2000hsv.googlegroups.com> <nj3ke3hmht7k5gimrpi54k70lqqtt8terv@4ax.com>`

"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:nj3ke3hmht7k5gimrpi54k70lqqtt8terv@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 12 Sep 2007 21:10:41 -0700, Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
<snip>
>>So, what 'advantage' does your typedef file have for this ?
>
> A single file. What is the advantage of hundreds of copybooks?
>

Unless I have missed something in the story of how the shop actually works 
today, the advantage(S) of COPY are:

1) The current system CAN generate the COPY statements without any modification

2) The COPY approach is in use for existing applications (and "understood by 
existing programmers

3) Possibly (probably) works with any COBOL sensitive editors.  (I don't know if 
there are editors already that "expand" TYPE - or MF USAGE - statements "in 
place" - rather than looking at/for another file.

P.S. I would certainly try to discourage manually creating/modifying COPY files 
rather than using their automated system.  However, not knowing how/when they do 
this, I might be mistaken on this.
 Furthermore, when/if they actually reach the stage that the majority of those 
maintaining and working with the COBOL source code actually ARE C/C++/C# 
programmers, then I would question why COBOL was still being used at all; rather 
than why a traditional COBOL technique was still being used for this one - to me 
MINOR - part of the application code.
```

command line and reading time of day.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW error (was: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-13T22:52:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1189749132.063270.30720@o80g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<nj3ke3hmht7k5gimrpi54k70lqqtt8terv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vplbe39v4n96qu5lhs354tp5smc2bb2ken@4ax.com> <MfpFi.62164$CK1.57058@fe04.news.easynews.com> <er8ce398t85id9kusf5ruaf4980e9do0ba@4ax.com> <NtwFi.62984$CK1.34073@fe04.news.easynews.com> <v2ree3lqtjh8nujm8nlghflta2na4dg726@4ax.com> <MqZFi.109178$M%1.62167@fe10.news.easynews.com> <2guge3pcm5ifpmau9ei2ahjpscdkbc6ssg@4ax.com> <1189643144.934583.268190@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <ieahe3dprferli0c4svqcemckkcqk6uftm@4ax.com> <1189656641.718248.101190@57g2000hsv.googlegroups.com> <nj3ke3hmht7k5gimrpi54k70lqqtt8terv@4ax.com>`

```
On Sep 14, 4:39 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Wed, 12 Sep 2007 21:10:41 -0700, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> >On Sep 13, 3:16 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
…[88 more quoted lines elided]…
> You acknowledge the benefit of having all types in a single file.

No, I acknowledged that _you_ claimed it as an advantage** for using
typedefs, and then I showed that there was no disadvantage to
copybooks in that respect for this particular purpose.

** note that I put 'advantage' in apostrophes to indicate, as you had
earlier explained, that it wasn't being used in its normal meaning.


> Why not use typedef?

For the particular software the use of typedefs would involve the cost
of re-engineering the complete source code. For the claimed
'advantage' that can be obtained at no cost at all.

If the system were to be re-implemented from scratch today then
perhaps it could be used, or perhaps OO could be.

Typedef is a 1970s solution for another language that was implemented
in Cobol to allow it to access Windows API in Windows 3.0 (and later).

> Then you'd be editing primary code rather than a derivative copy.

They would be better off generating these from the primary source, the
SQL database itself when they change that, rather than letting
programmers apply arbitrary edits. In fact using a derivitive copy is
safer.

In any case if they want to edit the picture the file is just a single
keystroke away for any reasonable editor.


> Another way is to edit the listing from your previous compilation.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> A single file. What is the advantage of hundreds of copybooks?

There isn't any, but it is what they have. It works, changing will
cost time and money and lead to risk, and so far you haven't found any
'advantage' that isn't easily and cheaply done another way.

You haven't even identified that what they are doing is actually a
problem for them, only that you don't like it and want to show how
clever you are in knowing about typedef (which I actually used in mid
1991 with an early release MF version 2.5 for Windows 3.0).

I probably wouldn't do this either way, but I wouldn't re-engineer the
whole system for no _actual_ advantage as you seem to want to.
```

I meant the current standard. The company didn't know about typedef in 2006.

>If you had read the compiler manual you would have known that.
>
>As to whether you 'knew' it would be in the next standard, it seems
>you don't even know that various implementations (MF and Fujitsu) were
>incompatible let alone which one would have made it into '02.

The incompability is trivial, especially in generated code. The Standard requires the word
TYPE, Micro Focus doesn't allow it. That could be changed in less than an hour.

>Also, Fujitsu did not have TYPEDEF of any kind until version 5 so I
>doubt that you had this 'when the software was written'.

Version 5 came out in 1999.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-10T13:09:09+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5kjjpmF3vsa9U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 9 Sep 2007 12:25:57 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> experience.

Well, not exactly. You stated that:

"Its workers came from other programming cultures; Cobol is not their first 
language.  They are intelligent, write good code
in other languages, weren't working in the '70s..."

Given that scenario, it would be reasonable to expect the company to train 
them, if it required them to maintain COBOL.

In that context my comment is fair.


>
> Here's an example. I worked at a place where database types were 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It's a very good example of how there can be some skills transfer from other 
languages to COBOL. The problem is as you identified; people just don't know 
what is available in modern COBOL.

(I know from my own experience that nobody associates OO with COBOL and I 
have had a number of occasions where I've pulled rabbits out of hats and the 
response has been "I didn't know you could do THAT with COBOL!". 
Unfortunately, the COBOL innovations have been overtaken by events, and, 
apart from scenarios like the one you describe, where there is a COBOL 
legacy, nobody is interested in whether COBOL can do it or not. The 
languages they ARE taught, and are facile in using, CAN do it, so the rest 
is of only passing interest. How interested do you think a COBOL programmer 
who has just written a "GO TO...DEPENDING"  is, on what the CASE statement 
in C# or Java does? We use the tools we know.)

I'm wondering how you might address that, if you were a manager.

Requiring busy people to read the manual or (far worse still) the Standard 
(especially a standard that is incredibly hard to read for anyone who is not 
an Oxford Scholar or Queens Counsel, isn't implemented by anyone, and shows 
no sign of going to be anytime soon), for a language that is not going to 
further their careers in any way at all (apart from general IT background 
and knowledge), is a bit like asking a Modern English  graduate to write a 
thesis in the Pelasgian Arimic dialect of  Latin.

Apart from demonstrating the ability to study, and general erudition, what 
does it achieve?

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-09-10T09:38:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fc33a7$iul$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <5kjjpmF3vsa9U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5kjjpmF3vsa9U1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>(I know from my own experience that nobody associates OO with COBOL and I 
>have had a number of occasions where I've pulled rabbits out of hats and the 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>thesis in the Pelasgian Arimic dialect of  Latin.

There's the... or at least 'a' rub, Mr Dashwood.  On the one hand folks 
say that COBOL is being abandoned because it cannot do (x); since they 
'know' it cannot do (x) the language and its study are neglected.

On the other hand... since the language and its study are neglected it is 
no longer learned that COBOL *can* do (x); the prophecy seems to 
self-fulfil.

What's a manager to do?  Why, allocate, co-ordinate and motivate personnel 
and resources towards the accomplishment of a stated Executive goal, of 
course.  

Does the goal require use of (x) by COBOL in order to achieve it?  Then 
hire/contract with somebody (don't train your current crew... never, ever 
train them, they might get Delusions of Adequacy!) who knows how to do it 
and leech his brain.

Does the goal require use of (x) somewhere else, where further training is 
not needed?  E'en better, take the saved budget and go on a 'fact-finding' 
mission (or its industry-specific equivalent) to (exotic locale).

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T00:25:34+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5kkrdvF4aalhU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <5kjjpmF3vsa9U1@mid.individual.net> <fc33a7$iul$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```


<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fc33a7$iul$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <5kjjpmF3vsa9U1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'know' it cannot do (x) the language and its study are neglected.

In my opinion, COBOL is not being abandoned because it cannot do (x). It is 
much more complex than that and there are many factors which have led to its 
demise.

There have been major chnages in the ways that computers are used (advent of 
PCs and the network) and it isn't that COBOL CAN'T do it, rather that it is 
too expensive to do it with COBOL, when there are other options that cost a 
fraction as much, and can deal with it in a more facile way.

However, I would agree that "perception" is one of the factors that have not 
helped COBOL.
>
> On the other hand... since the language and its study are neglected it is
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> and leech his brain.

Buying expertise for the short term is a legitimate business strategy and I 
would certainly do that. However, I do consider staff training to be very 
important, so the fact that expertise is contracted in one area doesn't mean 
the staff don't get trained in other areas. As for "Delusions of Adequacy" 
the people I select to work with (when I am allowed to do that; sometimes 
you have to make the best of what is available) are not so held down and 
insecure that they doubt their own ability. I've mentioned before that one 
of the major satisfactions I get when wearing the manager hat is seeing 
people grow, stimulating them, bolstering their confidence and supporting 
them.(It isn't altruistic; I get to grow through those same experiences.) A 
good manager will not have a team who are doubtful of their ability; he will 
have ensured that they are allowed to shine, and will see that as a major 
part of his role. I'm really not interested in discussing what bad managers 
do, before you start, Doc :-)

Hmmm... about the leeching :-)

Having (like you) been a contractor for a major part of my life, I don't 
think selling my knowledge lets people leech off my brain.  Skills transfer 
to the in-house people is part of the contract I have gladly entered into 
(unless I struck out those paragraphs of the contract, and I don't recall 
ever having done that, so I disagree about leeching.)

People who are Cyber whores (and I count myself in that category because I 
will take on work I don't particularly enjoy or want to do, if the price is 
right :-),  can hardly accuse their clients of leeching :-)

These days, because I'm getting older and because I don't have the economic 
necessity I once had, I am starting to be much more selective about the work 
I take on (guess that makes me less of a whore :-)); I plan to "retire" 
(inasmuch as I won't work on site, unless they make me an offer I really 
can't refuse :-)) by 2012 anyway, and would have done so years ago were it 
not for some traumatic events in my personal life.

Summing up my arguments above:
1. The demise of COBOL is not about what COBOL can or cannot do; rather it 
is a combination of many factors, a dramatically changed environment from 
that when COBOL was invented, and what COBOL has to compete with.
2. Companies not spending money on COBOL training is not (necessarily) 
because they are evil and want to keep their employees knuckled down, (it 
might be in some cases, but I would hope that people in such a place would 
leave as soon as they possibly could); rather, it is because there are no 
long term plans to keep this code, so investing more money in it than 
absolutely necessary for the short term is simply not good business sense. A 
good compromise here is to get a very capable COBOL guy in on a short term 
contract, and write a skills transfer clause into his contract. (If you want 
to call it "leeching", fine, but I don't think it is.)


> Does the goal require use of (x) somewhere else, where further training is
> not needed?  E'en better, take the saved budget and go on a 'fact-finding'
> mission (or its industry-specific equivalent) to (exotic locale).

Ah, the famous "boondoggle" :-)  Becoming less frequent in today's economic 
climate, I think.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** donald tees <donaldtees@execulink.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-10T09:24:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JKqdndNnBeYN2HjbnZ2dnUVZ_gqdnZ2d@golden.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5kkrdvF4aalhU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <5kjjpmF3vsa9U1@mid.individual.net> <fc33a7$iul$1@reader1.panix.com> <5kkrdvF4aalhU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> 
> In my opinion, COBOL is not being abandoned because it cannot do (x). It is 
> much more complex than that and there are many factors which have led to its 
> demise.
> 
Runtime fees wiped out it's use on small machines, pure and simple.  No 
PC developer in the world is going to use a language that prevents them 
from selling the code without paying the compiler producer royalties on 
their own code. It became too expensive to use in the marketplace.

Once the language was relegated to main frames only, it was doomed to be 
a small niche. Mainframes *are* a viable platform, sure, but they are 
still a very small proportion of programmable devices.

I, and a dozen other users I know converted everything for the simple 
reason that it enabled us to continue to make a living. What I did not 
re-write, I scrapped.  I still have a couple systems that I maintain in 
Cobol, but I have not purchased or updated a compiler in years.  To do 
so, I would have to charge the customer an amount they are not willing 
to pay.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** donald tees <donaldtees@execulink.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-10T23:20:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I6CdnVNXubvxlHvbnZ2dnUVZ_remnZ2d@golden.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e%eFi.15956$Y7.13547@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <5kjjpmF3vsa9U1@mid.individual.net> <fc33a7$iul$1@reader1.panix.com> <5kkrdvF4aalhU1@mid.individual.net> <JKqdndNnBeYN2HjbnZ2dnUVZ_gqdnZ2d@golden.net> <e%eFi.15956$Y7.13547@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> "donald tees" <donaldtees@execulink.com> wrote:
>> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hi, Donald. Ditto for me.

Hello Judson, it has been a while, hasn't it?

Funny, I ran into a customer that I have had for 25 years now, they are 
still using a Cobol System that I converted to GUI for them with the 
advent of XP. They had a hard disk crash, and need a re-install.  They 
were bemoaning to me that they did not know what they would do when it 
came to the point it would not run on the latest platform.

They had even gotten a quotation on a new re-write in a "Modern 
language".  They were quoted $75,000 for a basic system, with 1/5 the 
features of the current system. I told them their best bet was to run it 
under an XP emulation on a modern machine, and declined to quote. I also 
gave them an hourly rate, and offered to do it on their own machines, 
using software tools that I would specify, with them paying me the same 
rate they pay their contract truck drivers, but under the stipulation 
that I would do it all from my desk under PC-anywhere.

It looks like that is the way they are going to go, but I know well that 
I will not write it in Cobol.  They language is far too primitive, 
although nice and simple to read.

Donald
```

>I will not write it in Cobol.  They language is far too primitive, 
>although nice and simple to read.

What does "primitive" mean here?    To me, C and Assembly are
primitive as they are closer to the bits and bytes of the machine, as
opposed to the components of a GUI application generator.

- **From:** donald tees <donaldtees@execulink.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T21:15:33-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RJGdnROqyrAgoHrbnZ2dnUVZ_tWtnZ2d@golden.net>`
