- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <5kjjpmF3vsa9U1@mid.individual.net> <fc33a7$iul$1@reader1.panix.com> <5kkrdvF4aalhU1@mid.individual.net> <JKqdndNnBeYN2HjbnZ2dnUVZ_gqdnZ2d@golden.net> <e%eFi.15956$Y7.13547@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <I6CdnVNXubvxlHvbnZ2dnUVZ_remnZ2d@golden.net> <g4nde3tu7r4kiqrembuie3fii6opng08u4@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Mon, 10 Sep 2007 23:20:09 -0400, donald tees
> <donaldtees@execulink.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> opposed to the components of a GUI application generator.

What *I* mean by it is the lack of high level constructs, and automatic 
program creation from data dictionaries. For example, today I converted 
a program that created invoices on a piece of paper to one that created 
invoices in an email queue by simply clicking a check box in the 
prototype. Doing it in Cobol would take at least a day or two's work, 
unless, of course, I had spent a year or two's work setting it up at the 
system's level.

By the time you get a Cobol system that level, it has typically become 
mired down in sheer complexity.

Donald

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T00:54:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<et9ce39ipu7ei2919h7avhhcgkq8a8d9a3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bABEi.139259$VU2.26523@fe02.news.easynews.com> <jf86e3dsbej505er2esukiqhh8vfdttrbj@4ax.com> <5kgssnF3k643U1@mid.individual.net> <r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com> <5kjjpmF3vsa9U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Mon, 10 Sep 2007 13:09:09 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
>news:r0c8e3p94hth82u1ur807goa7ig9543k4c@4ax.com...

>(I know from my own experience that nobody associates OO with COBOL and I 
>have had a number of occasions where I've pulled rabbits out of hats and the 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>is of only passing interest. 

>I'm wondering how you might address that, if you were a manager.

I think employee selection is the most important factor. What you don't want is a cyber
whore who'll do anything for money. What want is someone who loves programming, can't
believe they're paying him for having so much fun', comes to work early and leaves late
because he or she doesn't want to quit while the juices are flowing. 

Aside from enthusiasm, the other requirement is high intelligence, at least 2 sigma,
preferably 3 or 4. They should be especially adept at spatial relationships. 

Technical skills can be taught.

>How interested do you think a COBOL programmer 
>who has just written a "GO TO...DEPENDING"  is, on what the CASE statement 
>in C# or Java does? We use the tools we know.)

Cobol has a case statement in EVALUATE. GO TO .. DEPENDING is ugly not only because it
breeds more GO TOs, to the end of the section,  but more importantly because it bypasses
the 88 levels defining the conditions. 

>Requiring busy people to read the manual or (far worse still) the Standard 
>(especially a standard that is incredibly hard to read for anyone who is not 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>does it achieve?

Someone with a negative opinion of Cobol cannot be enthusiastic about using it, He should
work in a language he respects.
```

"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:et9ce39ipu7ei2919h7avhhcgkq8a8d9a3@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 10 Sep 2007 13:09:09 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> because he or she doesn't want to quit while the juices are flowing.

You don't think it is possible to be a whore who enjoys the job? :-)  It's 
worked for me for over 4 decades... :-)

>
> Aside from enthusiasm, the other requirement is high intelligence, at 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> the 88 levels defining the conditions.

I deliberately used GO TO ... DEPENDING as an example, because I just KNEW 
SOMEBODY would jump on it.:-) Thanks for not disappointng me ;-) (Did you 
seriously think I don't know about the relationship between CASE and 
EVALUATE? :-)) Lol!

>
<snip>
>>Apart from demonstrating the ability to study, and general erudition, what
>>does it achieve?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> work in a language he respects.

I agree that enthusiasm is a very powerful force when it comes to any 
endeavour. Unfortunately, should you find yourself in a rubber raft in the 
middle of the ocean, all the enthusiasm in the world will not repair the 
punctures, once the glue and patches run out...

COBOL no longer meets my development needs, and I DO use another language. 
That has not lessened my respect or even regard for COBOL. I just believe 
its time is over, apart from legacy and batch applications.

Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: Discussions of COBOL philospphy

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-10T11:38:04-06:00

```
On Sat, 08 Sep 2007 18:10:15 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I believe (but could be mistaken) that all (or close to all) programs that use 
>PERFORM for such paragraphs (sections) understand that control never comes 
>back - and that "GO TO" would do the same thing.  However, I have (again 
>personally) never heard of a program "failing" - either in initial design or 
>maintenance because PERFORM was used instead of GO TO (or even "inline" code).

I have read that this actually has some real utility - some optimizers
process code differently when they do not find a GO TO in the code.
```

How about

GO-AWAY.
   EXIT PROGRAM.

On Tue, 11 Sep 2007 05:00:45 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>The current (follow-on) product is WDz which also includes PL/I and other 
>bits-and-pieces.  Are you interested in only those specific (out-of-service) 
>products or are you interested in who is using the current products.

Short answer - both.

Primarily I have a technical questions related to the out-of-service
products, particularly regarding a CICSLOAD batch file that does an
FCT update and won't deliver the correct result.
Then, EXACTLY what needs to be done to my 'flat fixed length
formatted' file to make it KSDS, concurrent with the FCT entry, and
thus acceptable to a CICS program.
The documentation is at best confusing and I've tried. So am searching
for folk who've actually done this stuff and/or code examples.

Secondly, since I am considering WDz (despite its awful price) for a
standalone Windows XP environment, am also interested in how many work
in such a scenario, how many WDz'ers there are (is this product a
'goer', type of usage, experiences.
```

<a id="t-9d91f178f1"></a>
## Thread: Error with COMP-3 — 31 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Error with COMP-3

- **From:** star6683@gmail.com
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T06:58:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1189519119.951558.186900@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```
hi ppl,
         I have problems reading the COMP-3 data.Below is the
description of the problem.
        I have a file with COMP-3 data on IBM mainframe.It is in a PS
file.
        As a next step i FTP it to the windows enviornment.Also we
have microfocus server express 4.0
       installed in the UNIX.
        After FTPing it in ascii mode i need to run a cobol program
which can read this COMP-3 data.
        To compile and run the program we are using the microfocus
compiler.
        The result of running the program is as below:
        "Error -163 llegal character in the numerical field"
         The variable declaration in my cobol program is as follows:
         03  TRID-10-10-LAST-STAT-DATE    PIC 9(6) COMP-3.

         I created a structure file using the Net Express record
layout and
          tried to view the data in formatted mode,
         I can view the right data if FTPed in binary mode.

         Ultimately the aim is to read the data (COMP-3 and non-COMP
data) using the cobol program.


         Kindly give me some inputs on the above problem.
```

#### ↳ Re: Error with COMP-3

- **From:** Louis Krupp <lkrupp@pssw.nospam.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T10:35:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13edguesbpkar89@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1189519119.951558.186900@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1189519119.951558.186900@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```
star6683@gmail.com wrote:
> hi ppl,
>          I have problems reading the COMP-3 data.Below is the
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The IBM mainframe uses the EBCDIC character representation (as you 
probably knew), and the other systems use ASCII.  Alphanumeric data 
(i.e., DISPLAY) is properly converted when you FTP in ASCII mode, but 
that same translation destroys packed decimal data (COMP-3).

The simplest solution is to convert all of your fields to DISPLAY on the 
mainframe before doing the FTP.  If that's not possible, there are folks 
here who will know of other ways to move the data.

Louis
```

#### ↳ Re: Error with COMP-3

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T22:36:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cMEFi.82898$qQ1.35640@fe05.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1189519119.951558.186900@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```
You can search google groups for a variety of solutions to this (well known) 
problem.  BASICALLY, they boil down to:

1) On the mainframe, change all non-DISPLAY fields to DISPLAY (sign is 
separate), then down load with conversion. (You can "re-pack" the fields and 
convert to binary if required.

2) Download in Binary and use CHARSET(EBCDIC)  (Micro Focus setting) on the 
workstation

3) Download in binary and use an "intelligent" conversion tool (home-made or 
available with SOME - not all - Micro Focus product) to convert from EBCDIC to 
ASCII for "character" data and sign-fixes for packed fields.
```

#### ↳ Re: Error with COMP-3

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T20:13:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10fee31pbg19f1q332n3mjo83nlsfia5ub@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1189519119.951558.186900@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Tue, 11 Sep 2007 06:58:39 -0700, star6683@gmail.com wrote:

>hi ppl,
>         I have problems reading the COMP-3 data.Below is the
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>         Kindly give me some inputs on the above problem.

Server Express ships with a program named DFCONV that does the conversion. The manual is
here:
http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/nx31sp1/nx31indx.htm
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Error with COMP-3

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <mmattias@talsystems.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-12T05:53:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nFPFi.2604$Sd4.1449@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com>`
- **References:** `<1189519119.951558.186900@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <10fee31pbg19f1q332n3mjo83nlsfia5ub@4ax.com>`

```
>>hi ppl,
>>         I have problems reading the COMP-3 data.Below is the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>which can read this COMP-3 data.

You cannot FTP COMP[-n] or other non-DISPLAY data in ASCII mode . What's 
happening is you are getting an ASCII-EBCDIC conversion on that data, which 
you cannot do. Also you could get spurious control characters (eg CR, LF) in 
the data stream.

There have been several threads on this exact same topic over the past six 
months or so. Same solutions each time:

- FTP BINARY and handle EBCDIC-ASCII conversions on either the source or 
target end
- Change the data to be FTP'd to DISPLAY format for transfer purposes, FTP 
ASCII (and allow EBCDIC-ASCII conversions during transfer) and convert data 
back to desired format on arrival.

General tutorial info re character sets and non-ASCII ("packed") data on my 
web site
http://www.talsystems.com/tsihome_html/downloads/C2IEEE.htm
```

#### ↳ Re: Error with COMP-3

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-11T20:15:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbfee3pms76m33ppmphdlteohe7tqknino@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1189519119.951558.186900@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Tue, 11 Sep 2007 06:58:39 -0700, star6683@gmail.com wrote:

Correction: *Net* Express ships with dfconv.
```

#### ↳ Re: Error with COMP-3

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-12T08:29:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76qfe3p98kpn36tul21e6826lgfv4nmn5g@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1189519119.951558.186900@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Tue, 11 Sep 2007 06:58:39 -0700, star6683@gmail.com wrote:

>hi ppl,
>         I have problems reading the COMP-3 data.Below is the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>which can read this COMP-3 data.

Companies that exchange thousands of files every day, those in the data warehousing
business, have standardized on XML for every file. Even internal transfers between their
own systems. 

Tell the sender you want an XML file *with schema*. If he says he can't, tell him to check
his calendar. He'll find it's now 2007.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Error with COMP-3

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-12T08:50:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89SFi.89736$jH3.6216@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<1189519119.951558.186900@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <76qfe3p98kpn36tul21e6826lgfv4nmn5g@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote:
> star6683@gmail.com wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> his calendar. He'll find it's now 2007.

He will still have the same problem: comparing multiple input fields against
the database, to see what's been changed. And there's the added overhead
of decoding the XML. Coding that is not particularly trivial, unless some
flavor of function to do it is available in his environment.
```

'display' data and the EBCDIC-ASCII conversion does not screw up the BCD and 
other 'binary' data fields!

Speaking of XML, you might be able to help me here...

As you may be aware, VS 2005 is a very powerful, feature rich, IDE. One of 
the features (which I have never got around to using), is called "Code 
Snippet Manager"

Yesterday I was doing some stuff with Stream Readers on COBOL source files 
and I was pretty happy with some of the C# I wrote. I thought I'd save the 
TRY...CATCH block so I could re-use it next time I had a similar 
requirement. I duly selected the block of code and saved it into the code 
snippets folder for C#.  But I couldn't get it back :-) Puzzled, I had a 
look at some of the other snippets in the folder and found they were all in 
XML! Mine would need to be similarly wrapped and I had no time or 
inclination to look up the bloody schema for it. There was a lot of swearing 
and I saved it in MyDocuments as a .TXT file... bit of a wimpy cop out, I 
know, but I simply didn't have time or inclination to wade through MS Help

This is plain simple source code. Can you imagine why they require to manage 
it in XML?

What am I missing here?

Pete.
```

needed tags using a UI.  See if there's an "Add to Code Snippet Library"

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error with COMP-3

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-12T19:21:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ivge35p788nmi19n15jspdi8cm7q3o23c@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1189519119.951558.186900@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <76qfe3p98kpn36tul21e6826lgfv4nmn5g@4ax.com> <UiZFi.83590$CK1.10342@fe04.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Wed, 12 Sep 2007 21:59:16 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>And of course make certain that he knows that RW will PAY for the development 
>work to take "fully functional" mainframe files and convert them to XML. <G>

Files are not "fully functional" when recipients have to struggle to read them, as we've
seen here dozens of times. 

In the Old Days, when people said they'd received a Cobol file, I responded there is no
such thing. Cobol uses regular (flat) files that can be read by any programming language. 

Now I've changed my opinion. A file containing packed decimal really IS a 'Cobol file',
because it cannot be read with common tools such as spreadsheets, database loaders, ODBC
drivers nor by programs written in Java, C#, C++, scripting languages, etc. The recipient
has to find a specialized tool, write a Cobol program or hunt down a specialized class
library. Microsoft Host Integration Server, one such specialized tool, refers to packed as
a "COBOL data type". We know it's not, but the rest of the world thinks it is.
http://msdn2.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms964696.aspx
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error with COMP-3

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-13T12:50:40+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5krfr2F54tbsU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1189519119.951558.186900@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <76qfe3p98kpn36tul21e6826lgfv4nmn5g@4ax.com> <UiZFi.83590$CK1.10342@fe04.news.easynews.com> <3ivge35p788nmi19n15jspdi8cm7q3o23c@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:3ivge35p788nmi19n15jspdi8cm7q3o23c@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 12 Sep 2007 21:59:16 GMT, "William M. Klein" 
> <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://msdn2.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms964696.aspx

Fair comment.

I've accessed packed fields from C# but, as you noted, I had to use a 
special library to do it.

I see this as being kind of analogous to the advent of Relational DB (which 
generally coincided with the advent of desktop computing). One of the 
reasons people in the early 80s started looking askance at COBOL was because 
the "file system" (ISAM/VSAM) was "closed". Need another report? Write a 
COBOL program. Suddenly they found if it was on an ACCESS or DB2 database, 
anything could get to it, and reports could be generated without any 
knowledge of COBOL.

15 years later we had Internet forums and people were still resisting 
vehemently. I pointed out at the time that opening up the corporate data 
resource by migrating it to RDB might be a good thing and got rubbished for 
it. (The usual, "overheads", "performance", "There's nothing wrong with what 
we've got", etc.) In fact, I remember some quite venomous posts saying that 
I was undermining COBOL by even suggesting it :-).

It is ironic that 25 years later I am being paid to write tools to assist 
the migration from flat files to RDB... (there are still some places that 
have resisted to the last.)

Packed Decimal will eventuall disappear because the reasons it was invented 
have no relevance today. (I remember it being an option on early System 
360s; Gene Amdahl never designed it into the system. It was implemented in 
firmware, and only if you paid for the "commercial instruction set". The 
idea was to make 360 a flexible system that could be sold to the commercial 
and scientific communities. When competing systems like the ICL 1900 began 
creaming IBM in benchmarks, they realised it was much too slow...)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The fate of Packed-Decimal (was: Error with COMP-3

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-13T14:22:41-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13ej03sga4od6b6@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1189519119.951558.186900@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <76qfe3p98kpn36tul21e6826lgfv4nmn5g@4ax.com> <UiZFi.83590$CK1.10342@fe04.news.easynews.com> <3ivge35p788nmi19n15jspdi8cm7q3o23c@4ax.com> <5krfr2F54tbsU1@mid.individual.net> <dD2Gi.58809$gR1.4966@fe03.news.easynews.com> <46E90A64.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```

"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message
news:46E90A64.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com...
[snip]
> I've long wondered, what do non-mainframe systems use for their currency
> data types?  Do they use floating point, with it's inherent decimal
> inaccuracies?

It is not mainframe vesus non-mainframe; it is languages
that have decimal data types versus those that do not.
What might a mainframe FORTRAN programmer use
for a currency data type?

Floating-point may be used, as an integer, to hold a
currency data type. Such use requires attention to scaling,
rounding, and normalization.

I suspect most who use currency data types extensively
would build or buy a library for support.

> I know that the .NET framework has a "Decimal" data type, though since x86
> does not (as far as I know) have support for decimal arithmetic I wonder
> what type of actual machine code instructions are used to support it.
> Anyone know?  Is integer arithmetic used, along with the knowledge of
where
> the decimal point exists?

There are x86 instructions for decimal arithmetic. These
instructions operate on both ASCII characters and
packed decimal, one byte at a time. There are also
floating-point instructions for loading and storing packed
decimal numbers, 8-byte (or 16 digit) positive integers.
(Source: Micosoft Macro Assembler 5.1 Reference)
```

> I've long wondered, what do non-mainframe systems use for their currency
> data types?  Do they use floating point, with it's inherent decimal
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

For Java your options are BigDecimal, long, int, double or float.

Here are some of my notes from  Effective Java by Joshua Bloch:

item 31: avoid float/double if exact answers required 
149
float/double designed primarily scientific/engineering calculations      149
binary floating-point arithmetic 
149
accurate approximations over broad range magnitudes                     149
do not provide exact results 
149
float/double particularly ill suited monetary calculations 
149
impossible represent 0.1 or any other negative power of 10 exactly  149
example: float/double monetary calculation problem 
149
use BigDecimal, int, or long for monetary calculations 
150
example: use BigDecimal monetary calculation 
150
BigDecimal less convenient than primitive arithmetic/slower              150
int/long alternative to BigDecimal, depends on amounts involved      150
example: use int for monetary cslculation 
150
BigDecimal give full control over rounding 
151
BigDecimal lets you select from eight rounding modes                     151
can use int if quantities not exceed 9 decimal digits 
151
can use long if quantities not exceed 18 digits 
151

Numbers on right side are page numbers.
```

<all snipped>

as a follow-up on "decimal" vs other data types and operations ....

For those interested in "decimal arithmetic", the latest draft of the IEEE 
Decimal Floating Point Standard is expected to be available for comment soon. 
(I will be posting information on the invitation to participate in the review, 
when I get it).

So far, IBM has already introduced hardware support (in z/OS 1.9 related OS & 
hardware) and both Sun (for Java) and XML (W3C - if I recall correctly) have 
indicated INTENTIONS to include support for the new data types and operations. 
It is also included in the current draft of the NEXT COBOL Standard.
```

<a id="t-1dc7758729"></a>
## Thread: COPY vesus TYPEDEF — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### COPY vesus TYPEDEF

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-12T22:33:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13eh8ionfpv1of9@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:MqZFi.109178$M%1.62167@fe10.news.easynews.com...
> As MF and the current (and recent) Standards allow nested COPY statements,
you
> could certainly have a single COPY member with all the COPY statements for
the
> individual structures (as long as you didn't become recursive, this could
go
> down multiple levels).
>
> Also, STRONG types in the '02 Standard do provide protection *IF* the
programmer
> uses them.  If they decide to avoid those rules, then (like REDEFINES and
a
> variety of COBOL features) the results are unpredictable.
>
> Certainly doesn't sound to me as if there are many (if any) reason for a
shop
> with competent COBOL programmers (even if they do also use other
languages) to
> change from standard COPY members to the MF non-Standard TYPEDEF
extension.  (If
> a shop DID already use COBOL TYPEDEFs, then I wouldn't see much advantage
to
> changing to COPY statements either.  This is simply a two equivalent
styles to
> do the same thing.)

Copy and typedef are not equivalent styles, merely similar
within the context of a single program. They are a world
apart (at least in my solar system <g>) when used in the
context of an application consisting of many programs.

Application- or system-wide typedefs may be copied into
every program and, because they are only definitions, not
use one character of storage in the data division (2002,
TYPEDEF clause, GR(2)). The same can not be said of
an approach using nested COPY statements.

Typedefs defining group items are aligned the same as
record descriptions (2002, TYPE clause, GR(2d)). The
same can not be said of COPY statements, because the
COPY statement does not require alignment (2002,
COPY statement, GR(6)).

Because typedefs are not copy statements, there is an
opportunity to think differently about programming. One
can treat a typedef as something similar to an intrinsic type
by defining programs and functions to access and modify
the elements of the typedef. This promotes reuse of code;
this is not an idea that leaps into one's head with copy
statements, perhaps because copy statements, particularly
for files, sometimes contain several items that could be
typedefs.
```

#### ↳ Re: COPY vesus TYPEDEF

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-13T03:58:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0A2Gi.91130$qQ1.41755@fe05.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<13eh8ionfpv1of9@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message 
news:13eh8ionfpv1of9@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
> news:MqZFi.109178$M%1.62167@fe10.news.easynews.com...
<snip>
> Because typedefs are not copy statements, there is an
> opportunity to think differently about programming. One
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I certainly can accept that there are differences and different uses available 
for TYPEDEFs and COPY statements.  I don't see them as "inherent" in the uses 
that started this thread.

I also am one of the people that think that COPY statements (PARTICULARLY in the 
Procedure Division - which many in this group don't use) definitely DO lead to 
thinking about "reuse" (within a single program or across an application - or in 
the case of error handling often across an entire shop).

I guess, my "mainframe mentality" is showing when I do *not* think that the 
storage used by Working-Storage or Local-Storage COPY statements is a "big deal" 
as compared to NOT allocating storage for TYPEDEFs.  I certainly can imagine 
some applications where this WOULD be a significant factor in deciding which to 
use (if both were available).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COPY vesus TYPEDEF

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-13T01:16:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13ehi5f9nkio424@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<13eh8ionfpv1of9@corp.supernews.com> <0A2Gi.91130$qQ1.41755@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:0A2Gi.91130$qQ1.41755@fe05.news.easynews.com...
>
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> I certainly can accept that there are differences and different uses
available
> for TYPEDEFs and COPY statements.  I don't see them as "inherent" in the
uses
> that started this thread.

While the thread started with a narrow scope, it expanded
to include typedef versus copy generally. I have no opinion
on the narrow scope; but feel compelled to defend the
general use of typedef.
```

<a id="t-723920df03"></a>
## Thread: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL" — 44 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-13T05:14:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```
I just thought that as a follow-on to another current (and other well past) 
threads, I would start one not on what IS good COBOL, but how one can/should 
judge if a COBOL program contains "good COBOL".  The following are just some of 
what I would use (and not necessarily in order of importance):

1) Does the code actually accomplish what it was designed to do?

2) Does the compiled code run within the performance requirements set out for 
it.  (Better MAY be nice - but not always if other things suffer for performance 
reasons)?

3) Is the code "easily" maintainable and extendable?  (I know that IT *may* be 
moving to components that are replaced not enhanced, but that is not the USUAL 
environment for COBOL programs)

4) If there is any REASONABLE chance that the code will soon (or medium-soon) be 
ported to other environments (OS or compilers) does the code use "standard" 
techniques that are generally available (whether they are in a current standard 
or not)?

5) If those "behind" the program assure the programmer that it will NOT be 
ported, then does the program take appropriate advantage of those features that 
are available but specific to the environment where it is intended to run?

6) (Personal preference, but probably NOT universally expected)  Is there 
adequate documentation of what and how the program does what it does?  Are there 
adequate unit and regression tests available to insure that what works today 
STAYS working when changes are made to it.

7) Does the program use facilities of the language that are either commonly used 
or easily picked up by those who may have to maintain or enhance it?

8) Is the program internally consistent on "style"?  (e.g. No MIXTURES of 
PERFORMs and PERFORM THRUs; either all paragraphs numbered or none, etc)

9) If intended for a "changing part" of the business, it the program/application 
written in a manner that is likely to allow for easy change and enhancement?

10) If there ARE "shop standards" that are expected to be followed (whether they 
actively enforced or not), does the code follow them?  (If there are standards 
that are regularly "ignored" are they at least ignored so that "better" 
approaches can be used?)

11) If those requesting the program say (claim?) that it is a "one time" program 
needed immediately, is it done as quickly and simply as possible and NOT 
complicated for non-essential "beauty" or "style" issues.  (Personally, I never 
believed a manager who claimed that a program would be a "one-time" program, but 
I also didn't spend TOO much time making it so that it would be able to last 
forever)
```

#### ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-09-13T13:18:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcbdao$kd5$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```
In article <lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>I just thought that as a follow-on to another current (and other well past) 
>threads, I would start one not on what IS good COBOL, but how one can/should 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>1) Does the code actually accomplish what it was designed to do?

IF PROGRAM-RUNS
    PERFORM NEXT-ASSIGNMENT
ELSE
    PERFORM CODE-LIKE-HELL UNTIL DAMNED-THING-WORKS
END-IF

>
>2) Does the compiled code run within the performance requirements set out for 
>it.  (Better MAY be nice - but not always if other things suffer for
>performance 
>reasons)?

IF RUNS-WITHIN-WINDOW
    PERFORM ALL-IS-WELL
ELSE
    PERFORM HOLD-OFF-IRATE-USERS UNTIL DAMNED-THING-FINISHED
END-IF

>
>3) Is the code "easily" maintainable and extendable?  (I know that IT *may* be 
>moving to components that are replaced not enhanced, but that is not the USUAL 
>environment for COBOL programs)

IF SUFFICIENTLY-MODULAR
    PERFORM PERMIT-SCOPE-CREEP
ELSE
    PERFORM HOLD-MANY-MEETINGS UNTIL USERS-DROP-REQUESTS
END-IF

>
>4) If there is any REASONABLE chance that the code will soon (or
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>or not)?

IF CROSING-PLATFORMS-EXPECTED
    PERFORM STICK-TO-STANDARD
...

>
>5) If those "behind" the program assure the programmer that it will NOT be 
>ported, then does the program take appropriate advantage of those features that 
>are available but specific to the environment where it is intended to run?

...
ELSE
    PERFORM USE-UNIQUE FEATURES
END-IF

>
>6) (Personal preference, but probably NOT universally expected)  Is there 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>STAYS working when changes are made to it.

IF FILE-CABINETS-UNLOCKED
    PERFORM READ-OUTDATED-DOCUMENTS
ELSE
    PERFORM SLOG-THRU-CODE UNTIL SENSE-MADE
END-IF

>
>7) Does the program use facilities of the language that are either
>commonly used 
>or easily picked up by those who may have to maintain or enhance it?

IF MAINTAINABLE-BY-2YR-CODER
    PERFORM NEXT-ASSIGNMENT
ELSE
    PERFORM REMOVE-SUBTLE-FEATURES UNTIL ALL-IS-OBVIOUS
END-IF

>
>8) Is the program internally consistent on "style"?  (e.g. No MIXTURES of 
>PERFORMs and PERFORM THRUs; either all paragraphs numbered or none, etc)

IF CODE-LOOKS-MOREORLESS-THE-SAME
    PERFORM NEXT-ASSIGNMENT
ELSE
    PERFORM FIND-BETTER-SKELETON UNTIL INDISTINGUISHABLE-FROM-OTHERS
END-IF

>
>9) If intended for a "changing part" of the business, it the
>program/application 
>written in a manner that is likely to allow for easy change and enhancement?

IF SUFFICIENTLY-MODULAR
    PERFORM MAKE-TODAYS-CHANGES
ELSE
    PERFORM IMPLEMENT-YESTERDAYS-CHANGES UNTIL NEW-REQUIREMENTS-MET
END-IF

>
>10) If there ARE "shop standards" that are expected to be followed
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>approaches can be used?)

IF GEEZER-STILL-THERE
    PERFORM CODE-AS-ALWAYS
ELSE
    PERFORM IMPLEMENT-NEW-FEATURES UNTIL LIFE-IS-EASIER
END-IF

>
>11) If those requesting the program say (claim?) that it is a "one time"
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>forever)

EVALUATE NEEDED-RIGHT-NOW ALSO Y-IS-CONSONANT
    WHEN TRUE ALSO TRUE
        PERFORM LV-T-VWLS UNTIL TTRLY-LLGBL
    WHEN TRUE ALSO FALSE
        PERFORM LV-T-VWLS UNTIL TTRL-LLGBL
    WHEN FALSE ALSO TRUE
    WHEN FALSE ALSO FALSE
        PERFORM GENERATE-MAINTAINABLE-CODE
END-EVALUATE

DD
```

See : re point 6

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com...
> I just thought that as a follow-on to another current (and other well
past)
> threads, I would start one not on what IS good COBOL, but how one
can/should
> judge if a COBOL program contains "good COBOL".  The following are just
some of
> what I would use (and not necessarily in order of importance):
>
> 6) (Personal preference, but probably NOT universally expected)  Is there
> adequate documentation of what and how the program does what it does?  Are
there
> adequate unit and regression tests available to insure that what works
today
> STAYS working when changes are made to it.
>


All bread-and-butter perfectly sensible requirements.  But that being said,
I bet there are as many excuses as there are programmers for not doing
documentation.  PD has a realistic take on it and as long as he sticks to
his rules he will be able to answer YES.  I am reasonably certain of my own
virtue in this respect as I can cite users who've taken my documentation and
used it to solve problems I hadn't anticipated.

It OUGHT to be universally REQUIRED.

Do the CDP and similar certificates have anything to say about this?

PL
```

#### ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-13T16:56:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13ejcf4apu5gr35@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> I just thought that as a follow-on to another current (and other well
> past) threads, I would start one not on what IS good COBOL, but how
> one can/should judge if a COBOL program contains "good COBOL".  The
> following are just some of what I would use (and not necessarily in
> order of importance):

COBOL, like barbeque and Christians is, intrinsically, good.

There's no such thing as bad barbeque, a bad Christian, or, well, bad COBOL. 
It's all in the eye of the beholder, the tongue of the taster, or the heart 
of the pentinent.

I've seen programs that didn't work, never worked, and couldn't be made to 
work, held up as exemplars of "good" programming practices.
```

What do you mean by "good?"... does it mean "pretty?" does it mean
"efficient?" does it mean "so cryptic that no one but you can
understand its logic and therefore they can't get rid of you?"... or
does it mean structured, readable, easy to follow, easy to modify,
easy to debug?
```

>>> On 9/14/2007 at 8:12 AM, in message
<1189779168.930606.292620@50g2000hsm.googlegroups.com>,
bfwd<bforward@texarkanacollege.edu> wrote:
> What do you mean by "good?"... does it mean "pretty?" does it mean
> "efficient?" does it mean "so cryptic that no one but you can
> understand its logic and therefore they can't get rid of you?"... or
> does it mean structured, readable, easy to follow, easy to modify,
> easy to debug?


Good COBOL is
- structured
- readable
- easy to follow
- easy to modify
- easy to debut
- pretty (as pretty as COBOL can be, which isn't that pretty...)
- 'efficient enough'

Actually, it seems to me those are describe traits in pretty much any
programming language.

Frank

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-14T09:35:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba9le39e3b0430os650ebh65sa89b0662v@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com> <13ejcf4apu5gr35@news.supernews.com>`

```
On Thu, 13 Sep 2007 16:56:48 -0500, "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
wrote:

>COBOL, like barbeque and Christians is, intrinsically, good.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>work, held up as exemplars of "good" programming practices. 

A recent study found brain differences between people who were upset
by changes to their routine and those who were not so upset - for
instance when there was a detour on the way home from work.     They
expanded on this and found, understandably, that those who were upset
were far more likely to be politically conservative than those who
weren't.     Looking at my young grandchildren, I see which ones
really, really like to have rules and which ones prefer more
randomness.    This even goes to such things as having a 4-year old
putting better than his 7 year old, more coordinated brother.   The
4-year old likes knowing how to putt properly - his brother just wants
to hit the ball.

As we get older, it is generally more difficult to adjust.   Few of us
enjoy arts that are younger than our formative years (music is the
obvious example).   And my work skills are most developed for tools
that I have been using for close to 4 decades.    Change threatens my
superiority - and even my ability to compete - what good is it to be
the best CoBOL programmer in a shop that is dropping CoBOL?   It is
understandable that we get more conservative as we age.   It's hard to
compete when the "game has passed me by", as they describe great
coaches of the past.

It's also understandable that we value our skills, abilities, and
decisions, even when other abilities are being better valued
elsewhere.    Bosses value management highly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-15T11:44:17+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5l0kmiF5s788U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com> <13ejcf4apu5gr35@news.supernews.com> <ba9le39e3b0430os650ebh65sa89b0662v@4ax.com>`

```


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:ba9le39e3b0430os650ebh65sa89b0662v@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 13 Sep 2007 16:56:48 -0500, "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> to hit the ball.

As long as both of them have fun, there's no harm in this. :-) For those of 
us to want to be good at what we do, it is difficult to refrain from 
enforcing this on others, especially children, but we must. The best you can 
do is offer instruction if they reach for it, and simply enjoy their company 
and the amazing conversational insights they show, if they don't.

>
> As we get older, it is generally more difficult to adjust.   Few of us
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> coaches of the past.

I was quite touched by this, Howard. I'm sure there are quite a few who feel 
like the game has passed them by. For myself, I combat this by staying 
interested in the bigger picture as well as the smaller one, and ensuring 
that my skill set is adequate to meet the needs of the future as well as 
today.

I think a background in COBOL can be of great benefit when moving to other 
things, but it can also be a hindrance.

Yesterday I spent a couple of hours writing some quite complex routines in 
C#  (calculating offsets and lengths, padding to needed lengths, and 
converting parts of strings to integers etc.) to handle some fixed length 
data from COBOL. When I came to integrate it all, I realised I needn't have 
bothered. It was because I was thinking like a COBOL programmer 
(manipulating fields of fixed length) that I had caused myself a lot of 
aggravation. I replaced the code I had written with a couple of lines of 
much smarter code and chalked it up to experience.

My father, for whom I had the utmost love and respect (he died when I was 13 
and my mother and I were both devastated), used to say that there is no such 
thing as useless information, and that all knowledge is valuable. He saw the 
acquisition of knowledge as the key to wisdom. When I challenged him on 
filling one's head with apparently useless nonsense, he smiled and said: 
"There is no useless information; there may be information you haven't found 
a use for yet. And your head has much more capacity than you give it credit 
for, my son..."

A grounding in COBOL can be useful, but if the next steps are not taken, 
then being passed by by the game is inevitable.

COBOL WAS the game, but it isn't any more. The new game is every bit as 
exciting and interesting as the old one.

Fortunately, for many here, retirement is approaching and there is therefore 
no economic driver to upgrade the skillset. If they do so, it is because 
they simply love computer programming and want to keep up with it.

Finally, I'm quite sure you personally don't feel like the game has passed 
you by. If you do, you need to re-evaluate :-)  My opinion is that if you 
had never written a line of COBOL in your life, you would be no less 
valuable than you are. COBOL or no COBOL you have shown from your posts here 
that your life has been a success.

None of us should be defined by our jobs; what we do, is NOT who we are :-)

And what we are is much more important than what we do.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-17T09:01:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c03te3pdooloda692ptvnujj7gl6bg3a7j@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com> <13ejcf4apu5gr35@news.supernews.com> <ba9le39e3b0430os650ebh65sa89b0662v@4ax.com> <5l0kmiF5s788U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Sat, 15 Sep 2007 11:44:17 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> It's hard to
>> compete when the "game has passed me by", as they describe great
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>today.

I got a MBA in OO in my 50's with this kind of thinking.    But it is
interesting to note that those coaches that my above quote applies to
are those who were successful enough to be among the very elite.
Surely they studied the new game and worked hard at staying relevant.

I think it is Isaac Asimov has a famous quote comparing old scientists
with new scientists - which I could not find with a quick Google. But
it is interesting to see how many great scientists and artists slow
down in their innovative work as they age.    It's hard to be
innovative when we already know effective ways of thinking.

People like you who work hard at not being left behind are in the game
- but rarely are in forefront, the way a youthful version of yourself
would have been.

And in fields that we don't care so much about, the gap is bigger. The
common example used is the next generation's music.

But being "in the game" probably is good enough.   As long as I can
continue learning and contributing, I can have a good life.   It's not
a contest.

>My father, for whom I had the utmost love and respect (he died when I was 13 
>and my mother and I were both devastated), used to say that there is no such 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>for, my son..."

When we stop growing, we start dying.   Your joy in learning is your
joy in living.

>None of us should be defined by our jobs; what we do, is NOT who we are :-)

Pretty much.   It is interesting that Larry Niven didn't need to work,
but when he went to parties and such, people asked him "what are
you?", and he would have to say "I'm nothing".   So he became a
writer.   Even when he's not writing he's having fun at SF conventions
and being part of an active, thinking community.

So part of who we are is what we do.    This isn't limited to our
jobs, I am a grandfather - if I do grandfathering.    My goal is to be
a lusty programmer, golfer, grandfather, and husband.    And I want to
have a lust for life at whatever I'm doing.   If I no longer have the
energy that I did when I was young - I can still do what I can.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-17T11:02:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mAxHi.305057$5y.75938@newsfe18.lga>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com> <13ejcf4apu5gr35@news.supernews.com> <ba9le39e3b0430os650ebh65sa89b0662v@4ax.com> <5l0kmiF5s788U1@mid.individual.net> <c03te3pdooloda692ptvnujj7gl6bg3a7j@4ax.com>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:c03te3pdooloda692ptvnujj7gl6bg3a7j@4ax.com...
>
> I think it is Isaac Asimov has a famous quote comparing old scientists
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

More likely Clarke, I think: he said something like "if an elderly and
respected scientist says that something's possible, he's probably right; but
if he says it's not possinbe, he's almost certainly wrong".  Asimov had a
towering ego and would never have admitted that his current work was not up
to his earlier accomplishment.  Still, his latest novels didn't suffer in
comparison: check out Heinlein and Fred Hoyle for awful examples.

PL
```

#### ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-15T01:25:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pisme3l4sms77aq1seaqokepn2bogd65br@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Thu, 13 Sep 2007 05:14:57 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I just thought that as a follow-on to another current (and other well past) 
>threads, I would start one not on what IS good COBOL, but how one can/should 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>1) Does the code actually accomplish what it was designed to do?

Testers answer that question, not code reviewers.

>2) Does the compiled code run within the performance requirements set out for 
>it.  (Better MAY be nice - but not always if other things suffer for performance 
>reasons)?

In Waterfall Methodology, performance tuning is done near the end of the process, long
after code review. 

Timings on the test box are meaningless. We want to know how fast it runs on a production
box. That's often a function of database access, not program code. Besides, we're not
allowed to test on a production box. 

>3) Is the code "easily" maintainable and extendable?  (I know that IT *may* be 
>moving to components that are replaced not enhanced, but that is not the USUAL 
>environment for COBOL programs)

Translation: do I like it? More importantly, would the old geezer in the corner office
like it? 

Since there is no objective test for easily maintainable, it means whatever the reviewer
wants it to mean. If you don't like a section of code, don't say so in those words, you'll
seem subjective and arbitrary. Say it is not easily maintainable. No one can prove that
statement wrong.

>4) If there is any REASONABLE chance that the code will soon (or medium-soon) be 
>ported to other environments (OS or compilers) does the code use "standard" 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>are available but specific to the environment where it is intended to run?

Sure, like management knows what will happen next year. 

>6) (Personal preference, but probably NOT universally expected)  Is there 
>adequate documentation of what and how the program does what it does?  Are there 
>adequate unit and regression tests available to insure that what works today 
>STAYS working when changes are made to it.

Test plans are not available at code review time.

>7) Does the program use facilities of the language that are either commonly used 
>or easily picked up by those who may have to maintain or enhance it?

Translation: can the old geezer understand it?

>8) Is the program internally consistent on "style"?  (e.g. No MIXTURES of 
>PERFORMs and PERFORM THRUs; either all paragraphs numbered or none, etc)
>
>9) If intended for a "changing part" of the business, it the program/application 
>written in a manner that is likely to allow for easy change and enhancement?

In other words, is it table driven? No code change is easy, because it has to go through a
six month cycle of testing and approval. 

>10) If there ARE "shop standards" that are expected to be followed (whether they 
>actively enforced or not), does the code follow them?  (If there are standards 
>that are regularly "ignored" are they at least ignored so that "better" 
>approaches can be used?)

Translation: do I like it? Since shop standards are not written (just make it look like
the others) or were written 20 years ago and nobody can find them, you can't be proven
wrong. Any language feature introduced after the standard was written (or the old geezer
wrote his last program) is automatically forbidden until "they" revise the standard. Since
"they" retired years ago, that's not gonna happen.

>11) If those requesting the program say (claim?) that it is a "one time" program 
>needed immediately, is it done as quickly and simply as possible and NOT 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>forever)

"There is nothing so permanent as a temporary fix."

A good way to insure it won't be used forever is to fill it with wildly nonstandard code.
One-time programs are not code reviewed.  If it's still running in production a year
later, you can use it as a precedent. 'What do you mean, not allowed? Program X does the
same thing and it's been running in production for a year.' To really score a win, find it
in a production program written by the reviewer.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-15T11:30:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13eo236duggguf2@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com> <pisme3l4sms77aq1seaqokepn2bogd65br@4ax.com>`

```
Robert wrote:
>
> Timings on the test box are meaningless. We want to know how fast it
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Heh. My shop (a non-COBOL one) once had three separate routines MOVEW(ord), 
MOVEH(alf)W(ord), MOVEB(yte) to move words, half-words, and bytes. THE MOVEW 
routine did a little fiddling when it started to get the counter to a 
word-boundary, then did Load/Store operations on 4-byte hunks until it got 
to the end, then did a little fiddling with the remainder bytes.

Same with half-words.

MOVEB(yte) moved a byte at a time.

Somebody got the bright idea to combine these three subprograms. The entry 
MOVEW(ord) took the value to move, multiplied by 4, and branched to the 
MOVEB(yte) code.

One hour jobs now took six. The six shifts over the weekend turned out one 
shift's worth of work.

Come Monday morning...
```

> Robert <no@e.mail> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >> operations, everything was load/store registers. Moreover, the
routine
> was
> > >> written by the "Systems" section - supposedly the best programmers in
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> characters.  That's storage-to-storage.  That was my point.

< http://www-03.ibm.com/ibm/history/exhibits/mainframe/mainframe_PP2044.html
>
-----
Normally, the Model 44 uses an abridged set of System/360
instructions -- those associated with scientific operations. It can,
however, with the addition of an Extended Instruction Set
capability run programs interchangeably with Models 30, 40, 50,
65 and 75 if system configurations (memory capacity and
peripheral devices) are comparable and the problem programs
not time dependent.
-----
< http://www.thegalleryofoldiron.com/2044.HTM >
-----
Months later, the 44 OS was made available, which was basically
360 DOS, rewritten for the 44 instruction set (which lacked the
commercially oriented "SS" instructions).
-----
< http://www.beagle-ears.com/lars/engineer/comphist/model360.htm >
-----
360/44 - the oddest model. It could be described as a 40 with a
hardware floating point processor and faster memory. ...
It had only 1/2 word and 1 word instructions and could therefore
use a one word memory width without any speed penalty. Due to
the odd instruction set, it had its own operating system, PS/44.
-----
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-16T06:26:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<104Hi.834$SP6.771@fe04.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com> <pisme3l4sms77aq1seaqokepn2bogd65br@4ax.com>`

```
Robert,
  Bunches of your comments seem to imply that the ONLY (main?) time that 
"judging" whether COBOL code is "GOOD COBOL" is done at "code review time".  I 
certainly made no such restriction in my original post (or certainly didn't mean 
to imply one).  My note was simply asking what ARE the criteria that individuals 
would use in making such a judgment (and posted some of mine).

Certainly, both the
    "old geezer in the corner office"
        and
   the second year programmer doing maintenance or emergency fixes

are two issues that I might take into consideration - depending on the situation 
when I was making such a judgement.

        ***
Whether or not I said it explicitly in my original note, I question whether 
there are any universal and objective ways to determine if "COBOL source code" 
is "GOOD COBOL source code".  I might believe that doing what is expected of the 
program (with code delivered on time and that performs adequately) are universal 
and objective ways to evaluate code, but I don't think that "good" is the normal 
adjective for such code.

IMHO, (almost?) all else is subjective style decisions - and it was exactly 
those SUBJECTIVE criteria that I was interested in (especially from those who 
know their opinions are subjective).
```

#### ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-16T10:25:40-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13eqf5immqef7bb@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
news:lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com...
>I just thought that as a follow-on to another current (and other well past) 
>threads, I would start one not on what IS good COBOL, but how one 
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Maybe it somewhat easier to say what is bad COBOL.  I still remember the 
first COBOL program I was asked to modify wriiten by Lt. JG McClure.  He 
coded statements up to the right hand margin and then continued where he 
left off on the next line just as if he was writing a novel. He did know 
about PERFORM because I remember "PERFORM DOMAN THRU LINKER".  Unfortunately 
about 100 pages separted these paragraph names.  The program read about 7 
different record types and there were many, many GOTO statements. It was 
true spaghetti code. Imagine following nest IF statement without any 
indentation to indicate structure. I started thinking maybe I was wrong to 
ant to be a programmer.

Another bad example was a program that did an internal sort which took about 
one-half hour on an IBM 360/30.  Then it read in two control cards, edited 
them and did a STOP RUN if they failed the edit. The sort time was wasted.

One guy was a user programmer who wanted to retire and come back as a 
contract programmer.  He changed some of the switch fiield names to make 
them meaningless and he added meaningless IFs withn these names to make it 
so no one but him could read the code.

The first database I used was System 2000.  The data the programs processed 
was highly classified, TOP SECRET CODEWORD only. In order for the programs 
to be at only at a SECRET level we coulf not use meaninful field names. The 
fields were named FIELD1, FIELD2, ... etc. We all had a sheet of paper that 
gave us FIELDx matched with its real and meaningful name. Also report 
headings had to be stored in and retrieved from the database.

So lots of poor data names, a monolithic structure (lack of modularity) and 
GOTO spaghetti code seem to indicate bad code. But how much is too much? 
That again is in the eye of the beholder. Picking good data names is harder 
for beginners and structured programming techniques may not be obvious, 
especially as back when I started the programming taught in college classes 
did not even mention that.

For Robert I suspect any mainframe COBOL program is a bad one. I don't know 
if his mother was frightened by a mainframe when she was pregnant or if he 
just had a bad work experience with one and generalized that to all 
mainframes. It is so easy to look at a program and nitpick it, especially if 
it has had a lot of maintainance. Programs have a history, often one 
dictated by someone other than the programmer. I suspect if Robert did not 
write it then he will find a way to proclaim it no good.  I seem to remember 
he was highly critical of academics and Donald Knuth in particular and 
claimed combsort was superior to quicksort. If Knuth does not measure up to 
Robert's criteria then what hope do the rest of us have?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-16T13:00:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uapqe3pcb77tufs71gf6a52ih1umh7t9vg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com> <13eqf5immqef7bb@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sun, 16 Sep 2007 10:25:40 -0400, "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:

>The first database I used was System 2000.  The data the programs processed 
>was highly classified, TOP SECRET CODEWORD only. In order for the programs 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>headings had to be stored in and retrieved from the database.

I worked on a large program that caused the compiler to run out of room for storing
literals. They moved all literals into three tables -- numbers, strings and one-byte
characters -- and referred to them by subscript. It was full of statements like:

IF (ACCOUNT-STATUS = B-LIT (27) OR B-LIT (123) OR B-LIT (17)) 
    AND ACCOUNT-BALANCE > N-LIT (5)

Compilation took one hour, which was scheduled once a day. If the compilation failed,
there wasn't time for a second try, you had to write patches to the executable. 

>For Robert I suspect any mainframe COBOL program is a bad one. 

Teams I managed wrote thousands of good programs. I have seen good code written by others.

At one shop we rewrote all programs, which numbered 800-1000. Production emergencies went
from 50 per month down to 1 per month. Time to make simple changes went from months down
to days. Number of programmers and IT budget was 1/10 of the industry norm.

I mentioned those stats to show that good programming isn't merely artistic, it has
business value.

>I don't know 
>if his mother was frightened by a mainframe when she was pregnant or if he 
>just had a bad work experience with one and generalized that to all 
>mainframes. 

I've seen code from many mainframe shops, especially when working for computer
manufturers.

It is so easy to look at a program and nitpick it, especially if 
>it has had a lot of maintainance. Programs have a history, often one 
>dictated by someone other than the programmer. I suspect if Robert did not 
>write it then he will find a way to proclaim it no good.  I seem to remember 
>he was highly critical of academics and Donald Knuth in particular and 
>claimed combsort was superior to quicksort.

It was radix sort. The Cobol I posted was 6.3 times the speed of qsort() from a commercial
C library. 

> If Knuth does not measure up to 
>Robert's criteria then what hope do the rest of us have? 

Computer science professors don't use Knuth's textbooks. In the '90s, they were using
superior texts by Sedgewick.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-16T22:20:02-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13erp0vkt1m7f65@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com> <13eqf5immqef7bb@corp.supernews.com> <uapqe3pcb77tufs71gf6a52ih1umh7t9vg@4ax.com>`

```
<snip>

"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:uapqe3pcb77tufs71gf6a52ih1umh7t9vg@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 16 Sep 2007 10:25:40 -0400, "Charles Hottel" 
> <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Wow what a selective memory you have.  I seriously doubt that claim.  As I 
recall I compared your sort to a COBOL quicksort that I got off the web and 
quicksort was much faster. I posted both programs as well as a mainframe 
timer that I used. At the time IIRC you lamely said the quicksort must have 
beeen optimized.  However I don't believe it was heavily optimized based on 
the way the code was structured.  I have coded quicksort in mainframe 
assembler base on Knuth's book including optimizations from the exercises 
and I am certain it would be much faster than the COBOL one I posted.

Even assuming that your statement is true it does not follow that quicksort 
is slower, it could be that the version you tested against was poorly coded. 
Also the results could be data dependent. Quicksort is very slow on sorting 
data that is already in order.  There are also some other special cases 
where quicksort is very slow.  I have posted references to articles IIRC by 
Jon Bently from a Unix Review article which discussed these other cases. In 
that case the quicksort was from Unix and Bently presented an optimized 
heapsort that was almost as fast as quicksort but avoided these problems. 
Heapsort is the only sort algorithm that I know of that is O(N Log N) 
although I admit may not be knowledgeable about the current state of the art 
in sorting.

Please notice that instead of trying to make the strongest possible case 
that I can against you, I have qualified my statements to try and make it as 
clear as possible the exact circumstances under which I am making my 
staements.  I object to your sweeping generalizations which attempt to win 
an argument while ignoring other important details which might weaken your 
argument or even make it invalid. You seem mostly interested in creating an 
uproar (to get attention?) and a Win-Lose situation instead of a Win-Win one 
where both sides learn something from the other.

>> If Knuth does not measure up to
>>Robert's criteria then what hope do the rest of us have?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Knuth's books can be challenging, especially some of the mathematics, but I 
have found them worth the effort. I personally think the hypothetical MIX 
was a mistake but I can understand his reasons for creating/using it. I 
would like to see and have sometimes thought of writing a mainframe 
assembler companion, as well as versions in C and in COBOL. I have seen 
articles by him from Scientific Amaerican that were much more readable but 
they were targeted at a more general audience. His Art of Computer 
Programming books are targeted at graduate level computer science people. 
Many of the colleges around here are still using his books.  So just another 
useless sweeping generalization to say that they are not used.

IIRC Sedgewick did his Phd work with Knuth as his advisor. His books are 
more readable but they do not deleve into the same depth as Knuth's books.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-17T14:12:01+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5l663jF6ih70U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com> <13eqf5immqef7bb@corp.supernews.com>`

```


"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:13eqf5immqef7bb@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
…[106 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Charlie, why would you want to measure up to ANYONE's criteria, Robert, 
Donald Knuth, or anyone else?

The only important yardstick, is, (for contract people, as Doc frequently 
states) the criteria set by the person signing the timesheet, and for 
non-contract people, the criteria you set yourself to aspire to.

For most people, as they gather experience, they realise they could do 
things better and they want to improve. There are a small minority who 
don't, but they are usually the unhappy ones who work because they need to 
and not because they enjoy it or value the workplace. Some of them have had 
any enjoyment or enthusiasm or aspiration knocked out of them (usually by 
bad management), and some of them are just in the wrong career.

In programming, as in life, you shouldn't NEED approval (although it is 
always nice to have); you should know what you need to do to continue the 
growth process and simply do it.

Robert is presenting opinions and arguments; he is not requiring you to 
"measure up". You don't work for Robert (sighs of relief all round :-))

Only each one of us can decide how we're doing.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>

<snip>

> Charlie, why would you want to measure up to ANYONE's criteria, Robert, 
> Donald Knuth, or anyone else?
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…

It is not about measuring up or seeking approval, it is about improving, 
learning, and being the best that you can under the circumstances, including 
your heredity and environment.  My point is that Robert presents his 
opinions in an overly general manner that seems more designed to provoke 
attention than to be attempts at accurate statements.  Why should I value 
opinions that seem to exclude all or most COBOL programmers here from the 
set of COBOL programmers capable of producing good COBOL code.  Except for 
newbies I am quite certaint hat most of the long time members posting here 
are quite capable of writing good COBOL and whether they do it on a 
mainframe or a PC is pretty much irrelevant. Why should I value opinions 
that devalue the contibutions of one of the foremost computer scientists of 
all time.  I have studied some but not all of Knuth's works and I personally 
feel that it stretched and strengthed my mind and knowlege considerably.

#### ↳ Re: How to JUDGE what is "Good COBOL"

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-17T08:11:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gLCdneOpXO_HFnPbnZ2dnUVZ_umlnZ2d@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<lH3Gi.92012$qQ1.21828@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> I just thought that as a follow-on to another current (and other well past) 
> threads, I would start one not on what IS good COBOL, but how one can/should 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> reasons)?

In going through the Air Force training for programmers, there was a 
particular question that was asked many times - the key term was 
"programs should be as efficient as required", not as possible.  It's a 
cost/benefit thing - if really obtuse code is a fraction faster, the 
readability tradeoff is greater than the performance gains.

> 3) Is the code "easily" maintainable and extendable?  (I know that IT *may* be 
> moving to components that are replaced not enhanced, but that is not the USUAL 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> are available but specific to the environment where it is intended to run?

These last two make sense.  :)  Of course, depending on the environment, 
you may have to use non-portable features.  (I know that all my MCB 
calls that are required to run in a TIP environment on a Unisys 
mainframe will likely not port anywhere else successfully.)

> 6) (Personal preference, but probably NOT universally expected)  Is there 
> adequate documentation of what and how the program does what it does?  Are there 
> adequate unit and regression tests available to insure that what works today 
> STAYS working when changes are made to it.

This one *should* be universally expected.  Documentation is key, and 
having an automated way to test code (a unit test, like we're doing now 
in our Java shop) can find errors before the users do.  :)

> 7) Does the program use facilities of the language that are either commonly used 
> or easily picked up by those who may have to maintain or enhance it?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> written in a manner that is likely to allow for easy change and enhancement?

Another big one - bonus points if the rules are configurable externally 
to the program.  That way, a business analyst or other subject matter 
expert can define the rules, and the program picks them up and does what 
it should.  :)  (Not that that's easy, but it's getting easier...)

> 10) If there ARE "shop standards" that are expected to be followed (whether they 
> actively enforced or not), does the code follow them?  (If there are standards 
> that are regularly "ignored" are they at least ignored so that "better" 
> approaches can be used?)

Standards should be reevaluated as the shop matures, and as new 
functionality is added to the language.  If the standards have examples 
of structures and what they do, and if "new guys" are expected to be 
knowledgeable of the standards, they won't be surprised when they 
encounter things in the code.

And, although Robert's post has invigorated this newsgroup again, his 
approach is one that should be taken.  "We don't use (x) because (y) is 
much more efficient."  Is it?  Maybe it was, and maybe that's something 
that the vendor has addressed in an updated version of the compiler and 
run-time.

I know that resistance to change is high, and most employers would say 
"I hired you to code, not change these standards."  But I really believe 
that revisiting these from time to time not only keeps them fresh, but 
it keeps everyone up on the standards, and can expand their knowledge. 
("What the heck is WS-VAR (4:3)?  WS-VAR isn't a *table*!!")

> 11) If those requesting the program say (claim?) that it is a "one time" program 
> needed immediately, is it done as quickly and simply as possible and NOT 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> forever)

I wish I had a nickel for every "one-time" program that got put into 
production.  I'd be able to by some coffee at Starbucks' for sure!  :) 
I also have one "hey, look what we can do with this" program that's been 
used as a system performance diagnostic tool.

Because of this, even my "one time" programs are well-commented, 
indented properly (like other programs in the shop), etc.  But, I agree 
that you shouldn't spend *too* much time on frills until the "one time" 
moniker is dropped.

These are good, Bill...  :)
```

---

b.	Simply displaying the index variable itself

d.	By using another S9(4) COMP-3 variable

#### ↳ Re: About index variable in cobol

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-14T06:15:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TFpGi.94228$ax1.39222@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1189678067.888298.32070@y42g2000hsy.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1189678067.888298.32070@y42g2000hsy.googlegroups.com>`

```
dhushetty@gmail.com wrote:
> Hi i have the following question about index variable in cobol?
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Well, on IBM COBOL for z/OS (forgot the exact level), options a, c, d, 
  and e are TRUE, and option b is false.  You have to SET an 
elementary numeric item TO the Index.

z/OS COBOL automatically converts numeric elementary items to display 
usage for a DISPLAY statement.  IBM OS/VS COBOL (obsolete for many 
years) did not have this capability, but I still sometimes miss the 
IBM extension of EXHIBIT NAMED <elementary item>.

Cheers,
```

- **Date:** 2007-09-14T04:55:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1189770917.373735.100340@19g2000hsx.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Sep 13, 9:59 am, dcan...@ix.netcom.com wrote:
> I downloaded from MicroFocus testmail.zip.  This zip file is an
> example of how to call outlook from within NetExpress.  After
> unzipping the file I was missing winflags.cpy and p2cevent.cpy.  Does
> anyone know where I can get these files?

If you install the GUI/Dialog System of Microfocus NetExp... then it
should be present under the ..\BASE\SOURCE\GUICL folder. These files
are windows event copy files under NetExpress GUI (Dialog System)
module.

Rene
```
