```
On 27 Sep, 02:41, "Pete Dashwood" <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> of them came to a sticky end, also. Probably a club best avoided...)

It is my understanding that St. John The Baptist was aware of Essene
teachings but, because of his respect for his parents, was not a whole-
hearted follower of the Essene creed. JC was a follower of SJTB with
some adjustments to that creed. There is an interesting documentary
about this (I think it may have been by the Naled Archaeologist but
I'm not 100% on that) that turns up to UK TV from time to time.

>
> Or is it the teaching of Jesus as modified and interpreted by the
> Organization set up after His death?
>

Despite the Spirit of God speaking through the followers, I have no
doubt that the creed as preached by JC has been adulterated by man
ever since:

1.  Mary Magdalene (in the Gnostic Gospel bearing her name) recounts a
dream which she uses to explain what JC would want (I don't believe my
dreams so why should she believe hers?);

2.  The previous statement about Paul being a nasty piece of work. It
is known that he changed the interpretation of JC teachings to his own
end (don't ask me for citations).

> Unless we were actually at the Mount and heard the sermon, who knows what
> was actually said? As the technology of the time did not admit to actual
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> said and how much has been "improved and gilded" by the Scribes.

Check out the four official Gospels for contradictory witness
statements of the crucifixion.

>
> (I love the scene in "Life of Brian" where the people at the back of the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> also very real.)

And a truly brilliant piece of work. How can one take the Bible
seriously (or any other work from any religion) having watched that
film?

>
> The best we can do is take what is useful and inspiring, whether it came
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Or just Hope?
```

```
On Thu, 27 Sep 2007 14:12:34 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>That's a great idea! Transcribe it onto some old goatskin and leave it 
>hidden in a cave somewhere in the Middle East. Before you know it, we'll 
>have the Cult of Ditdahdit supplanting the rest, as the One True Word of 
>God. :-)

I read a short story, where some guy brings a bottle containing old
manuscripts to some Christian scholars in Israel.   They start looking
at it and come to a line "taken down from the cross more dead than
alive".

The final scene shows the man in a cave, magically creating a bunch of
such clay bottles.   He points to them, and a couple thousand years of
radiation heat the cave.   Then his old man visage disappears and his
Satanic Majesty laughs as he leaves the cave.
```

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
> "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> written in Greek.

Mostly in Greek. "Eli, Eli, lama sabachthani?" (Matthew 27:46) is Aramaic.

Jesus also spoke Hebrew, of course; all Jews were taught it then, as well
as all religious Jews are now in Israel, often elsewhere as well. My Jewish
friends were taught it. This is so they could read the Tanakh (Jewish Bible,
what Christians call the Old Testament).
```

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-26T16:12:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%YzKi.91761$Pv4.81071@newsfe19.lga>`
- **References:** `<fd5mc6$1b6$1@reader1.panix.com> <SbadnSDaL696DmvbnZ2dnUVZ_qiinZ2d@comcast.com> <13fd73ccb00a22c@corp.supernews.com> <ZgxJi.118847$pu2.56037@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <2vyJi.74400$TK1.9691@fe04.news.easynews.com> <9wBJi.55413$7e6.54210@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <2iRJi.109249$lZ7.74409@newsfe20.lga> <7VVJi.111313$tu2.30117@fe09.news.easynews.com> <jaXJi.409$P21.95@newssvr19.news.prodigy.net> <1biif31ireqcjft2mi3npf7gdan652nvvk@4ax.com> <mYdKi.496$P21.58@newssvr19.news.prodigy.net> <i8tif35d3q7keenm76gt4fp0ibseiu5c8v@4ax.com> <5lu5i3F9vjubU1@mid.individual.net>`

```

Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message > >
> > I really don't understand how anybody could stay sane who truly
> > believed strangers, acquaintances, friends, and family will be
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> be strong and punishments were harsh. Simple people figured the harsher
the
> punishment, the greater the leader.
>

It's also a possibility that they didn't have the mathematics to really
understand what "eternity" (an infinite number of years) implied.  I believe
that the Greeks (the most mathematically advanced group in the ancient
world) had "myriad" (10,000?) as the biggest named number in common use; and
while Archimedes, in the "Sand Reckoner", got up into truly gigantic
numbers, even by today's standards, his method didn't affect general usage.
If your biggest number is "myriad" and your biggest expression is "myriads
of myriads of myriads ...", even if you go on for half an hour, you have no
idea what number you're really talking about.  It would probably have been
beyond comprehension that any number you could construct is zero as compared
to infinity: therefore it wouldn't have troubled them because they wouldn't
have understood what they were saying.

PL
```

```
In article <1190832843.004788.174190@50g2000hsm.googlegroups.com>, Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>On 24 Sep, 23:49, spamb...@milmac.com (Doug Miller) wrote:
>> In article <7VVJi.111313$tu2.30...@fe09.news.easynews.com>, "William M.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>discuss her religion.

Wouldn't have anything to do with the Church of England being the officially 
established religion there, would it? I'd expect Catholics in the U.K. to be a 
bit more reluctant to discuss their faith publicly than Catholics in the U.S.

And, of course, some people just aren't comfortable discussing their religious 
beliefs at all; common reasons include insecurity, uncertainty, or a desire 
for privacy. Many folks don't really know why they belong to the church that 
they do -- often, they attend a particular church simply because it's the one 
their parents attended.

I know a number of Catholics who are quite willing to discuss their beliefs, 
and very articulate in explaining why they believe as they do. Your experience 
here, while common, is far from universal, and should not be generalized to 
encompass all of us.
```

```
In article <1190916363.779875.220190@w3g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>, Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>On 26 Sep, 21:58, spamb...@milmac.com (Doug Miller) wrote:
>> In article <1190832843.004788.174...@50g2000hsm.googlegroups.com>, Alistair
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>atheist and argumentative.

I know several Catholics who would regard that as a challenge, rather than 
being intimidated by it. I'm one of them. :-)

> BTW, unrelated to her religion, she was the
>best programmer I have ever supervised.

I've not noticed much, if any, correlation between programming ability and 
religious affiliation: the best programmers I know are a couple of Catholics, 
a Lutheran, an atheist, a Hindu, two evangelical Christians, and one guy whose 
religion I never knew. The worst ones are a couple of Catholics, two 
evangelicals, an atheist, and a reincarnationist.

OTOH, IME there *does* seem to be a *strong* positive correlation between 
programming ability and affinity for classical music, especially Handel and 
J.S.Bach.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-24T15:15:52+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5loof8F9806oU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd4jia$ntb$1@reader1.panix.com> <zNGdnb-Q8paiZGjbnZ2dnUVZ_uKpnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd5mc6$1b6$1@reader1.panix.com> <SbadnSDaL696DmvbnZ2dnUVZ_qiinZ2d@comcast.com> <13fd73ccb00a22c@corp.supernews.com> <ZgxJi.118847$pu2.56037@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:ZgxJi.118847$pu2.56037@bignews1.bellsouth.net...
> "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
>>
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
> of it.

Well then Judson, with all due respect, maybe it is time to start 
throwing...

Or at least, questioning.

The parts of the Pentateuch you quote above were (probably... there is some 
dispute about who wrote what and when... just one credible example: 
http://www.awitness.org/contrabib/torah/latedate.html) written by Moses, 
based on the folk lore and legend of his time, and in an effort to unite and 
inspire a people suffering unendurable hardship. I have always thought it is 
incredible (as a work), but I don't believe it was inspired by God, any more 
than any idea anybody has is "inspired by God". (As I don't believe in God, 
it is easy for me to say that :-))

Moses had the benefits of an education at Pharoah's court; the Hebrew slaves 
did not. It was natural that he would emerge as a leader, and a leader 
(especially in those times) had to be strong.

But do the mores and rules of an ancient culture  (capital punishment, "eye 
for an eye" as "justice"...) have relevance today?

I think we can do better than that. If we can't, then we have learned 
nothing in 3500 years and we aren't going anywhere, either.

Maybe it is exactly beliefs such as this, that are holding us back.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2007-09-24T12:22:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GZNJi.294$P21.186@newssvr19.news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd4jia$ntb$1@reader1.panix.com> <zNGdnb-Q8paiZGjbnZ2dnUVZ_uKpnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd5mc6$1b6$1@reader1.panix.com> <SbadnSDaL696DmvbnZ2dnUVZ_qiinZ2d@comcast.com> <13fd73ccb00a22c@corp.supernews.com> <ZgxJi.118847$pu2.56037@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <5loof8F9806oU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5loof8F9806oU1@mid.individual.net>, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>But do the mores and rules of an ancient culture  (capital punishment, "eye 
>for an eye" as "justice"...) have relevance today?

"An eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth" is not a commandment to seek exact, 
identical revenge; rather, it is a *limitation*: you shall not do more than 
was done to you. In a time and place in which the norm was a *life* for an 
eye, this idea was nothing short of revolutionary.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-25T02:04:51+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5lpug6F9emsoU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd4jia$ntb$1@reader1.panix.com> <zNGdnb-Q8paiZGjbnZ2dnUVZ_uKpnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd5mc6$1b6$1@reader1.panix.com> <SbadnSDaL696DmvbnZ2dnUVZ_qiinZ2d@comcast.com> <13fd73ccb00a22c@corp.supernews.com> <ZgxJi.118847$pu2.56037@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <5loof8F9806oU1@mid.individual.net> <GZNJi.294$P21.186@newssvr19.news.prodigy.net>`

```


"Doug Miller" <spambait@milmac.com> wrote in message 
news:GZNJi.294$P21.186@newssvr19.news.prodigy.net...
> In article <5loof8F9806oU1@mid.individual.net>, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
A good response, Doug. I admit I hadn't thought about it like that.

Even so, revenge (like punishment) is really pointless. Punishment does not 
deter crime; changing people's minds about crime is mch more effective. 
Nowadays, we can sentence them to community service or working for the 
victim until the grievance is worked out. Both of these approaches have 
shown some promise here, particularly making miscreants confront their 
victims and see the damage they do, not just to one person but to families 
and communities. Even people who come from backgrounds where they had 
neither of these things have been known to change their minds, especially if 
they are young and have a chance at another better choice.

I have an elderly lady friend whose house was burglarized and she was pretty 
distraught. The kid who did it was made to sit down with her and received 30 
hours of service at her house, mowing lawns, doing chores and generally 
helping with odd jobs. She told me she was pretty uncomfortable with it but 
agreed to the experiment. That was a few years ago. The kid is now married, 
has a little girl, holds down a job at a local garage and calls to see the 
old lady about once a month. They are firm friends and he thanks her for 
giving him a chance. It can work. (Not in all cases, of course, but it's 
still better than plucking out eyes and teeth...)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-23T15:50:38-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mKWdnSXWlP25fWvbnZ2dnUVZ_oGjnZ2d@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<13fd73ccb00a22c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd4jia$ntb$1@reader1.panix.com> <zNGdnb-Q8paiZGjbnZ2dnUVZ_uKpnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd5mc6$1b6$1@reader1.panix.com> <SbadnSDaL696DmvbnZ2dnUVZ_qiinZ2d@comcast.com> <13fd73ccb00a22c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> I am tired of wars. I am tired of our leaders coming to us and saying "here 
> is why we have to go to war, and here is why it  is righteous, and will be 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> at fighting wars?

It takes 2 (or more) to make that work.  We can be as diplomatic as we'd 
like, but if the other side genuinely wants to kill as many of us as 
possible, no negotiation is possible.  War is then borne out of survival.

> Maybe we have to fight for things like freedom.

That is the crux of my argument in the thread you joined, that seems to 
be misunderstood by other participants.

> Thou shall not kill. Some say that is a mistranslation and should be thou 
> shall not murder. I wouldn't like to bet my soul on it.

KJV - "Thou shalt not kill."
http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Exodus%2020:13;&version=9;

NASB - "You shall not murder."
http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Exodus%2020:13;&version=49;

NIV - "You shall not murder."
http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Exodus%2020:13;&version=31;

NKJV - "You shall not murder."
http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Exodus%2020:13;&version=50;

CEV - "Do not murder."
http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Exodus%2020:13;&version=46;

HCSB - "Do not murder."
http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Exodus%2020:13;&version=77;

It's a pretty safe bet.  I've heard a couple of people who know Hebrew 
and Aramaic give that interpretation as well.

> Life is precious and we should not be so willing to 
> waste it.  Perhaps I am prejudiced because of recent events in my life. I 
> hope I can live my life without ever having to kill another person.

I agree.  I know this subject is one that has personally affected you in 
a strong way over the past year.
```

- **References:** `<s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd4jia$ntb$1@reader1.panix.com> <zNGdnb-Q8paiZGjbnZ2dnUVZ_uKpnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd5mc6$1b6$1@reader1.panix.com> <SbadnSDaL696DmvbnZ2dnUVZ_qiinZ2d@comcast.com> <13fd73ccb00a22c@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:13fd73ccb00a22c@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> young babies in church today.

Charlie, I think you have expressed what many people (all over the world) 
are feeling. And especially people who have to watch their children and 
grandchildren being put in harm's way for what can only be construed as 
"doubtful" politics.

We are all tired of it.

But what can you do?
Simply roll over and let the strong and ruthless trample the less strong? 
How long before the strong and ruthless are knocking on our door?

The powerful nations of the Earth do have a responsibility to the less 
powerful, and it is also in their interests to try and maintain some 
stability.

Living on a remote island in the South Pacific which has no capability to 
defend itself , should the need arise. I sometimes reflect on these things. 
I am aware that the fact I am sitting here typing this in English, rather 
than Japanese, is because a number of young Americans died at the Coral Sea.

Had the Japanese not been stopped they would have occupied Australia, and NZ 
would have been taken with a phone call. I know there would have been fierce 
resistance when occupation troops actually arrived, and some of my 
compatriots would have taken to the hills and would still be fighting to 
this day, but the majority of the population (those who are not fit fighting 
men) would have had to accept it. I know that most of the Japanese people 
alive today were not even born then, so I don't translate this into an 
anti-Japanese prejudice, but I can't help but wonder how different the world 
would be if the strong did NOT protect the weak occasionally.

I guess the point is that there are times when violence HAS to be an answer. 
(I don't like it, but I am not one to ignore reality...)

I mentioned my father here before. For a part of his life he was a 
professional boxer, and he gave me boxing gloves when I was six. He used to 
get on his knees and spar with me. By the time I was 10, he was on his feet 
and I used to enjoy our sessions very much. I'd hit him with my best shot 
and he would laugh and say: "Not like that, you couldn't knock the skin off 
a rice pudding..." Then he would show me how I COULD knock the skin off a 
rice pudding. Throughout my entire school career, I was never bullied and  I 
stopped several other kids being bullied as well. Yet I never looked for 
trouble. The Old Man's advice (and I have never forgotten it) was: "Don't 
ever fight if you can avoid it. Violence is not an answer. Walk away. BUT, 
if you absolutely have no choice, then don't stop while you can stand or 
see."

(In a professional career that spanned 5 years he was never knocked down, 
and held the NZ Middleweight title for 18 months. He never boxed under his 
family name because pugilism would have been demeaning to the Old Folks at 
Home.:-) I still have his pocket watch with the initials "FF" (his boxing 
name "Fred 'Kid' Fisher") engraved on it. My mother gave it to him in 1936.)

New Zealanders are currently deployed in Iraq and Afghanistan, and one of 
them was recently awarded the Victoria Cross  (equivalent to the CMH for 
Americans) for carrying his wounded mate several hundred yards to safety 
while under intense fire from Taliban. A quiet, unassuming Maori man who 
grew up in a rural community on the East Coast of the North Island. Others 
are rebuildng schools and providing security for the populace. Even out of 
war, some good can come, but the price is very terrible.

So what can we do about it?

I believe we are living in an ideological age. It is about ideas and 
ideologies. If someone can be so convinced of an idea that he will drive a 
truck loaded with explosives into a market place, destroying himself and 
killing and maiming innocent bystanders, then we are unlikely to change his 
mind with violence. (However, I do believe that a proper use of violence in 
this context is to prevent the truck reaching the market place...)

How do you go about changing people's minds?

When it comes to Religion, it isn't easy. The best you can hope for is to 
persuade them to tolerance of other viewpoints and that doesn't seem to be 
working very well. While I respect people's rights to their Religious views 
(as long as it doesn't harm others) I'd still like to see the world grow out 
of it... :-)  I believe it will, but it will take probably a thousand 
years...

I know (because I live in a society where it works) it IS possible for 
people to "get along", and even actually enjoy the differences in culture, 
Religion (or lack of it) and viewpoint. But it takes Education and time and 
the will to do so.

It also requires that everybody has enough to eat and a reasonable standard 
of living.

So, at the moment, the best we can do is assure the fanatics that they will 
NOT achieve their aims by violent means, and that means the sacrifices that 
we are all tired of seeing and making.

It worked in Northern Ireland. Eventually the IRA were persuaded that 
killing and maiming people was losing them more support than it gained and 
we now have a peaceful solution. It isn't perfect, but it is way better than 
it was.

If fundamental Islamics and Christians were not being indoctrinated in the 
politics of hatred, if the more moderate members of both communities could 
exercise some influence over what is being preached in mosque and church, 
that would be a start.

I don't want to see young people of any race or denomination being 
sacrificed in Iraq or Afghanistan, or anywhere else.

Like Charlie, I'm tired of it.

But until minds are changed, it will continue.

I see Education rather than weaponry as the hope for the future. When people 
can read, they can get new ideas.

Meantime, whether we like it or not, we need weaponry.

Pete.












>
```

```
DD
```

```
for whatever economic hocus-pocus they play on the employer.

often not the same.

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fdfu2o$al8$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <fMGdnX8gR4BxiGbbnZ2dnUVZ_uOmnZ2d@comcast.com>,
> LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>>> That is not 'moving the goalposts'  Our definitions are rather
different,
> >>>> it seems.
> >>> Not the definitions, the framing.  But yes, they are.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> what is being discussed.

Perhaps, Mr Dwarf, you might prefer Mr Summers choose
words and definitions such as these from
< www.dictionary.com >.
-----
welfarism: the set of attitudes and policies characterizing
    or tending toward the establishment of a welfare state.

welfare state: a state in which the welfare of the people
    in such matters as social security, health and education,
    housing, and working conditions is the responsibility
    of the government.
-----

Also given are these additional definitions from the
American Heritage New Dictionary of Cultural Literacy,
Third Edition.
-----
welfare state

A state or government that promotes public welfare through
programs of public health, pensions, unemployment
compensation, public housing, and the like. The expression
welfare state is often used by those hostile to government
intervention in these areas.

[Chapter:] World Politics

And

welfare state

An economic system that combines features of capitalism
and socialism by retaining private ownership while the
government enacts broad programs of social welfare,
such as pensions and public housing.

[Chapter:] Business and Economics
-----

These two additional definitions, particularly the ideas
"often used by those hostile to government intervention
in these areas" and "features of ... socialism", seem to
relate to statements made by Mr Summers.

In any case, for Mr Summers, please feel free to use
these for your own welfare (benefit); untaxed and not
mandated by government. <g>
```

```
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>< www.dictionary.com >.

Perhaps, Mr Smith, I would prefer to permit Mr Summers to respond with 
that which he feels might be appropriate.

[snip]

>These two additional definitions, particularly the ideas
>"often used by those hostile to government intervention
>in these areas" and "features of ... socialism", seem to
>relate to statements made by Mr Summers.

To be related to something else, Mr Smith, is not necessarily - last time 
I looked - the same as being that something else.  If Mr Summers is 
confusing welfarism with socialism then he may see it as such; if he is 
doing something else... then benefit might be garnered from that being 
clarified, as well.

DD
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5m1vncFb9grnU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<zNGdnb-Q8paiZGjbnZ2dnUVZ_uKpnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd5mc6$1b6$1@reader1.panix.com> <SbadnSDaL696DmvbnZ2dnUVZ_qiinZ2d@comcast.com> <fd71j7$5te$1@reader1.panix.com> <fMGdnX8gR4BxiGbbnZ2dnUVZ_uOmnZ2d@comcast.com>`

```


"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:fMGdnX8gR4BxiGbbnZ2dnUVZ_uOmnZ2d@comcast.com...
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
<snip>.
>
> Just because the government creates and distributes the "money" does not 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
On the contrary, Daniel, every time a Government has tried to tinker with 
the free market the said Government has been severely burned. You cannot 
buck the market.

The example I remember best was when Nigel Lawson, Chancellor of the 
Exchequer, tried to prop the UK pound up by buying pounds with British 
reserves. He lost 70 Billion (and his job) in an afternoon and George Sorus 
reputedly made 3 billion for himself...


<snip>>
> Yes - but when I contract an hour of my life, complete with whatever 
> physical and mental skills I possess, to someone else for an agreed-upon 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> whatever economic hocus-pocus they play on the employer.

Tell me about it :-)

However, that is the price we pay for living in a Democratic society and 
trying to ensure a reasonable standard of living for all. We can't be 
comfortable in cosy homes with food on the table, knowing there are beggars 
starving at the gates.

<anecdote follows... skip this if you don't like stories>

(I've been places where I've actually seen this... a palatial mansion with 
three Mercedes in the drive and security guards to keep the riff-raff out, 
with a young mother too weak to stand, propped up against the fence, while 
her pathetically undernourished two year old crawled towards a banana skin 
lying on the sidewalk. I've seen a man and his wife, living in a packing 
case shelter, with three kids, watch their child die from sickness and 
starvation and the anguish on their faces is etched in my memory. I've seen 
women and children beaten with a bamboo cane by a man on a truck, while they 
tried to pick up grains of rice, grain by grain, from the dust, where it had 
drizzled out of rice laden sacks on trucks taking it to a secure warehouse 
for sale on the black market. (The trucks had to wait while the armoured 
doors were rolled up. The women and kids knew there was rice to be had at 
this point and took the beating in order to get it.) These sacks were 
clearly marked as a present from the people of  the United States of America 
for distribution to the needy, but in a society without justice, graft and 
corruption rule. I've been in a crowded marketplace, felt someone tug on my 
sleeve and turned to see the face of Leprosy, the skin peeling off the 
bones, the body swathed in filthy bandages, the stumps of fingers as the 
remains of a hand are extended for charity, and looked into the eyes of 
what was once a person. A preventable and curable disease, when there are 
clinics financed from taxation.

What do you do?

I helped the mother and child with food and drink, but it was temporary. The 
parents in the packing case there was nothing I could do and I shall always 
feel sorry over that. The guy with the cane got a surprise. He suddenly 
found a 6 foot Kiwi with a demeanour that showed he was not happy, had 
joined him on the back of the truck, and was telling him calmly that if he 
didn't stop with the beating the cane would be inserted into one of his 
bodily orifices. Said cane was then broken into pieces and discarded. He 
stammered that they might mob the truck and my response was: "So what? The 
rice is supposed to be for them anyway." I made a promise to myself in that 
moment that I would never waste rice again, in memory of those people. To 
this day, if I have a rice dish in a Chinese restaurant and can't eat it 
all, I take it home and it is eaten. My friends think I'm "odd" but I have 
never told them the story. Besides, they think me odd in so many ways, it is 
just another... :-)

The leper I gave some money too, but I had no illusions it was pointless. 
The best I could do and have done was stop making jokes about lepers... 
(That doesn't mean YOU can't; you weren't there :-); it just means that I 
don't...) I still don't know if that was a man or a woman, but it was once 
Human and I shall always remember the eyes...

</end of anecdote>

The whole point of the above, Daniel, is that although we have far from 
perfect societies, I'd rather live in one where the Government wastes some 
of my tax than live in one where it is "every man for himself". At least I 
can make a noise and get others incensed about it and maybe even change the 
Government.  I guess that, as far as I'm concerned at least, the 
alternatives are far worse.

None of us are ever going to be happy about paying tax (unless of course, 
they brought in a fixed rate across the board and everybody paid the same, 
say 15%...), but we can rest assured that our doing so means people can be 
sheltered, fed, educated,and get basic medical care.

I realise that from your point of view you see your tax dollars being spent 
on things you don't approve of, and could not condone morally. That is 
particuarly hard. I don't approve of big desks and luxurious appointments 
for Civil Servants, but I'll swallow it as long as the schools and hospitals 
stay open :-)

>
> Your focus is on what's "legal" - mine is what's "right."  Those are often 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
There's nothing in any Democracy to stop us owning stuff. We just need to 
remember that not everyone is as "lucky" as we are. The able have a 
responsiblity to the less able, the strong have a responsibility to the 
weak. Sure, it gets screwed up by the lazy and the greedy, but by and large, 
it works. It is still better than the alternatives.

Pete.
```

```
and availability in large cities?  From these, it is clearly the 
consumers that are losing.

>> Yes - but when I contract an hour of my life, complete with whatever 
>> physical and mental skills I possess, to someone else for an agreed-upon 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

All right...  :)

[snip anecdote]
> 
> None of us are ever going to be happy about paying tax (unless of course, 
> they brought in a fixed rate across the board and everybody paid the same, 
> say 15%...),

But *why* won't they do that?  It's the ultimate in fairness - you make 
$100, you cough up $15; you make $1,000, you cough up $150.  But the 
demagogues would rather give a free ride to some, and tax others more, 
because they've figured out that there are more on the low end than the 
high end, and they will vote their wallets instead of their morals.

> There's nothing in any Democracy to stop us owning stuff. We just need to 
> remember that not everyone is as "lucky" as we are. The able have a 
> responsiblity to the less able, the strong have a responsibility to the 
> weak. Sure, it gets screwed up by the lazy and the greedy, but by and large,

Is it?  Who is to say that private charity could not do a better, more 
efficient job, with less waste and fewer free-loaders?

And I don't believe it's luck at all.  "The rich get richer because they 
keep doing what it is that makes them rich - ditto for the poor." - Neal 
Boortz (US radio talk show host)  I started not so well off - we had 
very little growing up.  I remember getting my first clerical temp job 
for $7.50/hr - that was the highest per-hour wage rate that anyone in my 
family had ever earned!  I've changed what I've done, from things that 
didn't work to things that did.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-28T08:14:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13fps4ekn12icb2@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fMGdnX8gR4BxiGbbnZ2dnUVZ_uOmnZ2d@comcast.com> <5m1vncFb9grnU1@mid.individual.net> <T_2dnQVAxKXy6mHbnZ2dnUVZ_tqtnZ2d@comcast.com> <fdii7d$gpc$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fdii7d$gpc$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <T_2dnQVAxKXy6mHbnZ2dnUVZ_tqtnZ2d@comcast.com>,
> LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> None of us are ever going to be happy about paying tax (unless of
course,
> >> they brought in a fixed rate across the board and everybody paid the
same,
> >> say 15%...),
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> who has 85% of ten times that income.

I notice, Mr Dwarf, a slight, perhaps unintentional change
from omitting the term "rate". I agree that a "flat tax" is
regressive because all pay the same amount without
regard to income, thus the effective tax rate decreases with
increased income. A pure "flat-rate tax" is neither regressive
nor progressive because the effective rate remains the same
with increased income.

Advocates of "flat-rate tax" call their plans progressive
because their proposals include an exemption before the
rate is applied, thus the effective tax rate increases with
increased income.

Since I am unfamiliar with any "entirely different situation"
that could alter these truths, I may reasonably conclude
that those who would call a "flat-rate tax" regressive are
simply opposed to all such plans and would use
name-calling, rather than facts, to support their opposition.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-09-28T12:58:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fditp5$hao$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<T_2dnQVAxKXy6mHbnZ2dnUVZ_tqtnZ2d@comcast.com> <fdii7d$gpc$1@reader1.panix.com> <13fps4ekn12icb2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <13fps4ekn12icb2@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fdii7d$gpc$1@reader1.panix.com...
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>with increased income.

Perhaps the terminology has changed since I last looked at such things.  
As I recall, a flat tax (one which is applied uniformly across a 
population, regardless of income or ability to pay) is always represented 
as a particular percentage of income (in the abovegiven example, 15%); 
this percentage is the tax's rate (http://www.m-w.com, rate, noun, def'n 
3b: 'a charge, payment, or price fixed according to a ratio, scale, or 
standard')

If that is the case then to speak of a flat tax is to speak of a flat tax 
at a given rate; I am not sure how the term flat-rate tax (which you refer 
to surrounded by quotation marks, indicating... something) differs from 
flat tax (which, as noted above, is always at a given rate).

>
>Advocates of "flat-rate tax" call their plans progressive
>because their proposals include an exemption before the
>rate is applied, thus the effective tax rate increases with
>increased income.

If there are variations in its application then the rate is not, by 
definition (http://www.m-w.com, flat, adj, def'n 6b(1): not varying: FIXED 
<a flat rate>), flat.

>Since I am unfamiliar with any "entirely different situation"
>that could alter these truths, I may reasonably conclude
>that those who would call a "flat-rate tax" regressive are
>simply opposed to all such plans and would use
>name-calling, rather than facts, to support their opposition.

It may be, Mr Smith, that some see the word 'flat' and assume, perhaps 
incorrectly, that it is being used in a manner which reflects a definition 
found in a commonly-accepted source... and that others see that calling 
something which has variations in it 'flat' as valid.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-28T14:45:19-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13fqj1reqtrpj5b@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<T_2dnQVAxKXy6mHbnZ2dnUVZ_tqtnZ2d@comcast.com> <fdii7d$gpc$1@reader1.panix.com> <13fps4ekn12icb2@corp.supernews.com> <fditp5$hao$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fditp5$hao$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <13fps4ekn12icb2@corp.supernews.com>,
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
> >
> ><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message
news:fdii7d$gpc$1@reader1.panix.com...
> >> In article <T_2dnQVAxKXy6mHbnZ2dnUVZ_tqtnZ2d@comcast.com>,
> >> LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> None of us are ever going to be happy about paying tax (unless of
course,
> >> >> they brought in a fixed rate across the board and everybody paid the
same,
> >> >> say 15%...),
> >> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> familiar with the arguments that have been made for this?  Someone who
has
> >> 85% of one income is in an entirely different situation than someone
else
> >> who has 85% of ten times that income.
> >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> flat tax (which, as noted above, is always at a given rate).

Perhaps it differs in degree of precision--"flat-rate tax",
as a specific reference to a concept, being less ambiguous.
Since I am a stickler for precision, I dug into my closet
and pulled out the book that introduced me to the concept;
"Low Tax, Simple Tax, Flat Tax" by Robert E. Hall and
Alvin Rabushka, 1983. A quick thumbing-through of the
book showed that "flat tax", "flat-tax", and "flat-rate tax"
were regarded as synonymous, within the context of a tax
on income assessed at a flat rate. (All three variations
found on the same page! And there are other reasons to
beleive the book was rushed to press!)

> >
> >Advocates of "flat-rate tax" call their plans progressive
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> <a flat rate>), flat.

Page 21 of the book, last paragraph, "The flat tax can ....
Also, a flat-rate tax is progressive when a personal
allowance for adults and dependents is granted, ...."

While the statutory rate is flat (not varying, fixed); the
effective rate does vary by increasing with increased
income.

> >Since I am unfamiliar with any "entirely different situation"
> >that could alter these truths, I may reasonably conclude
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> something which has variations in it 'flat' as valid.

Or, that some may see or use the word "flat" and fail to
recognize when its use is ambiguous and clarity depends on
context. For the driver, a tax for driver's license renewal
may be flat, but for the state, a flat rate--the same rate or
tax for every driver in the state. For the taxpayer, property
taxes may be flat for non-ad valorem and flat rate for ad
valorem assessments; but, for the tax assessor, flat rate
for both.

In the context of the U.S. income tax, the tax is a flat rate
tax within ranges; but it is not a "flat-rate tax". The latter
term seems to remove all doubt.


Given the immediate context of "The flat tax has been
called a regressive tax" and my knowing that a "flat-rate
tax" can not be a regressive tax, my mind appears to have
jumped to examples of "flat tax" which are regressive.
And, with that jump, could not determine your intended
use of "flat tax".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-09-28T22:05:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fdjtrj$3j7$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<13fps4ekn12icb2@corp.supernews.com> <fditp5$hao$1@reader1.panix.com> <13fqj1reqtrpj5b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <13fqj1reqtrpj5b@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fditp5$hao$1@reader1.panix.com...
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Perhaps the terminology has changed since I last looked at such things.

[snip]

>Perhaps it differs in degree of precision--"flat-rate tax",
>as a specific reference to a concept, being less ambiguous.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>beleive the book was rushed to press!)

Well... how curious that I echo your source so precisely, even unto the 
synonymous use.

>
>> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>allowance for adults and dependents is granted, ...."

My apologies, Mr Smith... but I have trouble taking this seriously, 
perhaps it is the geometry I studied.  

Mr Summers original conditions, quoted above, were 'It's the ultimate in 
fairness - you make $100, you cough up $15; you make $1,000, you cough up 
$150.'; no allowances, deductions, protected investments or deferred 
interest... a rate of taxation that is 'not varying, FIXED' as per the 
definition I cited.

What Hall and Rabushka seem to be proposing is not that; they are 
proposing that certain income(s) are exempt and others will be taxed at a 
rate not to exceed (n)%.

>
>While the statutory rate is flat (not varying, fixed); the
>effective rate does vary by increasing with increased
>income.

Exactly.  This reminds of me 'cash is fact, profit is opinion'... because 
once the barn door is opened to income exemptions one may, rather easily, 
wind up with a tax system which approaches the current one.

[snip]

>> It may be, Mr Smith, that some see the word 'flat' and assume, perhaps
>> incorrectly, that it is being used in a manner which reflects a definition
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>context.

My situation appeared to be the reverse... I saw the word 'flat' and 
realised that the use being made of it seemed to be outside of the 
accepted definitions; this is sometimes called 'idiosyncratic use'.  
There's nothing wrong with such use, of course... but it helps to cut down 
confusion when it is made clear, up front, that the definitions found in 
commonly-accepted references no longer apply.

[snip]

>Given the immediate context of "The flat tax has been
>called a regressive tax" and my knowing that a "flat-rate
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>use of "flat tax".

Glad I was able to help clear that up by - completely unintentionally, 
mind you - sticking with the definitions (the ones equating flat tax and 
flat-rate tax') used in a book with which you were familiar.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-30T20:07:05-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oomdnWabZJ7Wy53anZ2dnUVZ_t2inZ2d@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<fdii7d$gpc$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<fMGdnX8gR4BxiGbbnZ2dnUVZ_uOmnZ2d@comcast.com> <5m1vncFb9grnU1@mid.individual.net> <T_2dnQVAxKXy6mHbnZ2dnUVZ_tqtnZ2d@comcast.com> <fdii7d$gpc$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <T_2dnQVAxKXy6mHbnZ2dnUVZ_tqtnZ2d@comcast.com>,
> LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> electorate... or maybe it is something else.

Like them campaigning saying they would do it, then forgetting that part 
once they're elected?  Say it ain't so!  :)

>> It's the ultimate in fairness - you make 
>> $100, you cough up $15; you make $1,000, you cough up $150.
> 
> The flat tax has been called a regressive tax, Mr Summers... are you 
> familiar with the arguments that have been made for this?

I am - and it's not regressive; it's neither regressive nor progressive. 
  It's fair - and being fair to those who have achieved more in this 
life is something that most politicians can't stomach.

I also have never seen an argument that wasn't based on emotion - with 
facts and numbers, the flat tax rate is the way to go.

> Someone who has 
> 85% of one income is in an entirely different situation than someone else 
> who has 85% of ten times that income.

True - but isn't nearly everyone in a different situation?  What is 
unfair about a level playing field?  I will know that if I make (x), 
I'll take home (x - 15%); if I make (y), I'll take home (y - 15%).

And, while we're on the subject of taxes, why tax earnings?  Wouldn't it 
be smarter to tax expenditures?  Not from the politician's perspective. 
   Tax it when you earn it, tax it when you spend it, tax it when you 
invest it, then tax it before it goes to your kids.  It's ridiculous! 
And why is *that* much money needed?  Because the government is doing 
things it has absolutely no business doing.

Withholding tax is probably the worst thing that has happened to our tax 
system, as far as transparency - it masks exactly how much is being 
taken out.  Ask the average person how much they pay in taxes, and 
they'll say "Oh, I don't pay - I get some back!"  ...just because they 
had too much taken from their checks to begin with.  Change the system 
and make people write out tax checks every quarter, and it would take 
about 3.1 months for the revolution to begin.  :)  If people actually 
see their earnings leaving their checking account and go to the 
government, they would understand exactly what they're paying.

>> But the 
>> demagogues would rather give a free ride to some, and tax others more, 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> equitable way to redistribute property?

An argument to authority from you?  :)  I'm not familiar with geometric 
taxes, but if it redistributes property from the haves to the have nots, 
it doesn't sound right to me...
```

- **Message-ID:** `<8e5qf31ba926bqdbfhfb1sgndfo2qomrpo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<zNGdnb-Q8paiZGjbnZ2dnUVZ_uKpnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd5mc6$1b6$1@reader1.panix.com> <SbadnSDaL696DmvbnZ2dnUVZ_qiinZ2d@comcast.com> <fd71j7$5te$1@reader1.panix.com> <fMGdnX8gR4BxiGbbnZ2dnUVZ_uOmnZ2d@comcast.com> <5m1vncFb9grnU1@mid.individual.net> <T_2dnQVAxKXy6mHbnZ2dnUVZ_tqtnZ2d@comcast.com>`

```
On Thu, 27 Sep 2007 21:39:25 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>But *why* won't they do that?  It's the ultimate in fairness - you make 
>$100, you cough up $15; you make $1,000, you cough up $150.  But the 
>demagogues would rather give a free ride to some, and tax others more, 
>because they've figured out that there are more on the low end than the 
>high end, and they will vote their wallets instead of their morals.

Taxing has always been about getting money.  Taking money from us at
gun-point isn't about morals, it is about getting what the state can
get away with.

As Willie Sutton said about why he robbed banks "That's where the
money is".

Sometimes they screw up - famously, the tax on yachts meant that the
world's rich people stopped buying American made yachts.    But as
long as the tax isn't high enough to move significant amounts of
America's rich away, it is working.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Thu, 27 Sep 2007 21:39:25 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> get away with.

Doesn't "getting away with" something imply immorality?  If it's right, 
why would one "get away with" it?

> Sometimes they screw up - famously, the tax on yachts meant that the
> world's rich people stopped buying American made yachts.    But as
> long as the tax isn't high enough to move significant amounts of
> America's rich away, it is working.

Businesses make choices based on their bottom line.  America takes too 
much of it, America loses all of it.  But, if American takes more than 
it should, the business does not succeed as it should; its' citizens do 
not receive the maximum benefit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-29T16:21:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5m626iFbr0lgU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<zNGdnb-Q8paiZGjbnZ2dnUVZ_uKpnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd5mc6$1b6$1@reader1.panix.com> <SbadnSDaL696DmvbnZ2dnUVZ_qiinZ2d@comcast.com> <fd71j7$5te$1@reader1.panix.com> <fMGdnX8gR4BxiGbbnZ2dnUVZ_uOmnZ2d@comcast.com> <5m1vncFb9grnU1@mid.individual.net> <T_2dnQVAxKXy6mHbnZ2dnUVZ_tqtnZ2d@comcast.com> <13fq72mgq86ee06@corp.supernews.com>`

```


"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:13fq72mgq86ee06@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't think so.

Many companies (and individuals) pay high fees to specialists to avoid 
paying tax. If the rate was reasonable (say, 15%) there would be no need to 
avoid tax, and it wouldn't be possible anyway. (No exemptions, expenses, 
thresholds, etc.)

If you are right, and there actually was a shortfall, it could be made up by 
raising the percentage on invisibles and corporate profit by a couple of 
points. For example, companies under the new regime might pay 20% on profit 
instead of the flat 15%. It is still way better than the 30% they currently 
pay (or actually DON'T pay, because they use eleaborate schemes to avoid 
showing profit.)

There are other, less tangible benefits from such a system also. For 
example, everyone has equal "value" in terms of their contribution to 
society. You might therefore be less likely to hear pompous asses 
complaining about how their personal taxes were supporting a small hospital 
and they therefore are of much more importance than everybody else, and such 
nonsense.

Even if the revenue raised WAS less than currently, bureaucrats would have 
to learn to adapt to the available funds; most of them would benefit from 
"living on a budget" as many mere mortals have to.

It would go hand-in-hand with a drive to reduce corruption and sandbagging; 
no more $200 toilet seats for the military, and such..., much more openness 
and accontability in local and national government, and so on...

I don't think it will happen, but it would if I ruled the World :-)

(Be thankful I don't... :-))

Pete.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:5m626iFbr0lgU1@mid.individual.net...
>
>
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Don't get me wrong.  I like the idea of a flat tax of some kind or a 
variation thereof. I think a creative way could be found to make it work, 
but I haven't thought about the details very much.  That being said, 
however, I just did a rough calculation of your above suggested 15% and I 
believe that at 15% I would end up paying more taxes.  Right now I have 
approximately 14% to 15% of my gross adjusted income deducted in taxes even 
though my supposed tax bracket is I think 33%. This is because I claim extra 
exemptions.  Even with that because I can benefit from itemizing deductions, 
I usually receieve some (<$1000) refund. So I am paying less than 15% now. 
People with incomes that place them in lower tax brackets may be renting 
because they cannot afford to buy a house and therefore they may not benefit 
from itemizing deductions. I believe they will end up paying more also. The 
analysis that I have read (a long time ago) indicated that 15% would not be 
high enough.

Hres is a link to current tax brackets in the USA: 
http://www.moneychimp.com/features/tax_brackets.htm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-09-29T13:19:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fdljcm$kh5$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<T_2dnQVAxKXy6mHbnZ2dnUVZ_tqtnZ2d@comcast.com> <13fq72mgq86ee06@corp.supernews.com> <5m626iFbr0lgU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5m626iFbr0lgU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>It would go hand-in-hand with a drive to reduce corruption and sandbagging; 
>no more $200 toilet seats for the military, and such..., much more openness 
>and accontability in local and national government, and so on...

Mr Dashwood, this one is difficult.  Those who profit from the armed 
forces and military conflicts have existed for... well, about as long as 
armed forces and military conflicts have.  (a comic-strip character in the 
United States of America, Little Orphan Annie, had a benefactor who made 
his pile supplying the military... 'Daddy' Warbucks)

Given that there is, in the words of Country Joe Mcdonald, 'plenty good 
money to be made supplying the Army with the tools of its trade' there's 
plenty good room for fraud, as well... and while a fraudulently-made pair 
of pliers can be a nuisance around the house the same flaw, when 
encountered in combat during some ad-hoc equipment repairs, can be a bit 
more lethal for a few more people.

More specialised equipment, more specifications for equipment, more 
inspectors to insure that equipment is up to specs, more managers to 
co-ordinate the inspectors... these are all costs which need to be figured 
into the price of the product.

And, of course, as with any system... humans make it and humans can 
game/break it.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-30T20:58:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jr6dnT7C2o7e_53anZ2dnUVZ_hmtnZ2d@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5m626iFbr0lgU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<zNGdnb-Q8paiZGjbnZ2dnUVZ_uKpnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd5mc6$1b6$1@reader1.panix.com> <SbadnSDaL696DmvbnZ2dnUVZ_qiinZ2d@comcast.com> <fd71j7$5te$1@reader1.panix.com> <fMGdnX8gR4BxiGbbnZ2dnUVZ_uOmnZ2d@comcast.com> <5m1vncFb9grnU1@mid.individual.net> <T_2dnQVAxKXy6mHbnZ2dnUVZ_tqtnZ2d@comcast.com> <13fq72mgq86ee06@corp.supernews.com> <5m626iFbr0lgU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> It would go hand-in-hand with a drive to reduce corruption and sandbagging; 
> no more $200 toilet seats for the military, and such..., much more openness 
> and accontability in local and national government, and so on...

You know, that brings up an interesting point about the way budgeting 
works in the military.  If an organization does not spend its entire 
budget one year, it loses that amount in the next years' budget 
automatically.  The fiscal year ends 30 Sep, so we just went through 
that.  It's a juggling act - if you go a year without upgrading much 
infrastructure, you can be screwed the next year by not having the funds 
to do so.  This has led to an end-of-the-year buying spree, and $200 
toilet seats run through that quickly.

I'm not saying it's right, I'm just explaining the culture in which that 
could happen without too many red flags going up.

(We benefited this year from not only our money - the development team 
is getting new computers and wide-screen monitors, which will really 
help productivity - but other folks' money as well.  Our budgeting 
people had contracts ready to execute, and when other orgs couldn't 
spend their money, they transferred it to us.  Good times at the Safety 
Center!)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-29T06:27:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13fsdj9d8td00a6@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<zNGdnb-Q8paiZGjbnZ2dnUVZ_uKpnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd5mc6$1b6$1@reader1.panix.com> <SbadnSDaL696DmvbnZ2dnUVZ_qiinZ2d@comcast.com> <fd71j7$5te$1@reader1.panix.com> <fMGdnX8gR4BxiGbbnZ2dnUVZ_uOmnZ2d@comcast.com> <5m1vncFb9grnU1@mid.individual.net> <T_2dnQVAxKXy6mHbnZ2dnUVZ_tqtnZ2d@comcast.com> <13fq72mgq86ee06@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
>>
>> But *why* won't they do that?  It's the ultimate in fairness - you
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> accept.

That's the flat tax. I propose the flat-flat tax. Take the budget (say $1 
trillion) divide by the number of people (say 250 million). That means that 
every person has to kick in $4,000. There are problems with this plan.

Consider a poor person who doesn't HAVE $4k. They could pay in kind - say 
one unit of blood platlets each month (at $400) and, in ten months, have 
their tax bill for the year paid.

But, but, but, what about the mother of four children all under the age of 
five! She can't give FIVE units of blood platlets a month and we certainly 
are not going to take blood from children!

She could give a kidney.

An artificial kidney machine costs, oh, say $45,000. One kidney would equate 
to about two years worth of taxes for her and her brood. Well enough, you 
may say, but what happens after two years? We're certainly not going to take 
the OTHER kidney!

She could donate a cornea.

Another two years. Before long her oldest child will be responsible for her 
own taxes (say at age 12) and mom can look forward to an early retirement.

Oh, sure, there are problems to be worked out, values assigned, "donation" 
clinics built, but nothing insurmountable and certainly nothing as 
complicated as the current tax scheme.

The basic objection to the flat-flat tax scheme, it seems to me, is that the 
burden falls most heavily on the lower rungs of society. Well, they're the 
ones who use the most government services! Sure, even the rich ride on 
roads, get mail, and benefit from military defense, so they should pay 
something. But you'll have to admit that the poor make use of the public 
schools, hospitals, and jails more than the affluent.

Seems fair to me that people should pay for what they get.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-09-30T03:10:56+13:00

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-24T09:10:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kgkff3977i9faffo39r1b0682u0u48pas3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<VLydnRiEfowsIWzbnZ2dnUVZ_saknZ2d@comcast.com> <ik55f3tfr5h3i6ui4rtb2atc3rtghpqsio@4ax.com> <uJ-dnaJYC-eXqm7bnZ2dnUVZ_uevnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd0cpt$s7c$1@reader1.panix.com> <s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com>`

```
On Fri, 21 Sep 2007 23:11:33 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Choices are being made for Americans every day, with the money taken 
>from us, and being used for who knows what.  Do I want to use my money 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>don't care about public schools!

The state pays for public schools because powers want lots of workers
earning them money.   Educated workers make the country strong and
wealthy - which gives them more power.

>The same thing applies to all the issues I mentioned above, and then 
>some.  When the government takes *my* money to give it to someone else 
>(not the aforementioned "common" things), *that's* socialism.

When the government takes your money to do anything at all with it, it
is still taking your money and spending it the way it wants.   Some
people think some expenditures are worth while, and others think other
expenditures are worth while.

And there really isn't a significant difference when your employer
takes some of the money it costs to hire you and subsidizes insurance
- and all of the other people using that insurance pay for your
medical care.   And the hospitals charge enough to pay for the poor
and for the paperwork.    It still is society paying.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2ZSdnWoW6pdAimbbnZ2dnUVZ_qGknZ2d@comcast.com>`

- **References:** `<VLydnRiEfowsIWzbnZ2dnUVZ_saknZ2d@comcast.com> <ik55f3tfr5h3i6ui4rtb2atc3rtghpqsio@4ax.com> <uJ-dnaJYC-eXqm7bnZ2dnUVZ_uevnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd0cpt$s7c$1@reader1.panix.com> <s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com> <kgkff3977i9faffo39r1b0682u0u48pas3@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Fri, 21 Sep 2007 23:11:33 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> wealthy - which gives them more power.

But the idea that the "government is the solution" in the first place is 
what boggles my mind.  What has the government done well?  There have 
been several studies that show private schools doing a lot more for 
their students with a lot less money per student.  Doesn't that merit at 
least a passing glance?  Nope - government is the answer.  Let's pour 
our money down a drain that isn't doing what it's supposed to do, and 
whose employees and their union grumble loudly whenever any legislation 
is passed to encourage them to do it!

>> The same thing applies to all the issues I mentioned above, and then 
>> some.  When the government takes *my* money to give it to someone else 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> expenditures are worth while.

I'm all for charity.  I give my money to charity, I volunteer, my Cub 
Scout pack volunteers too.  I just resent the government taking the 
money and using it for *their* preferred charity instead of me having 
that money to put to *my* preferred charity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-27T06:38:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zAMKi.132$z7.35@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<VLydnRiEfowsIWzbnZ2dnUVZ_saknZ2d@comcast.com> <ik55f3tfr5h3i6ui4rtb2atc3rtghpqsio@4ax.com> <uJ-dnaJYC-eXqm7bnZ2dnUVZ_uevnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd0cpt$s7c$1@reader1.panix.com> <s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com> <kgkff3977i9faffo39r1b0682u0u48pas3@4ax.com> <2ZSdnWoW6pdAimbbnZ2dnUVZ_qGknZ2d@comcast.com>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>
> But the idea that the "government is the solution" in the first place is what boggles my mind.  What has the government done well? 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> them to do it!

You nailed it there. Not only has government always been lousy at
doing things, but even when it is currently in progress demonstrating
how lousy it is, people *still* want to throw more money at it. The
U.S. spends several times as much money per student as any other
country, but has the worst secondary educational system of any
industrialized nation, worse than some third world countries. And
the answer is to give them more money? Insanity!

I know what would correct the U.S. educational system, and believe
it would do it in three years. First calculate the amount of money the
government spends per student, then offer *every* parent the option
of sending their child to public school as now, or getting a voucher
for 1/2 the amount the government spends per student to be used at
*any* private school the parent chooses. I sent my kids to private
schools, and any private school I know of would be ecstatic to get
1/2 what the government pays per student. The flight from public
schools would become a stampede, private schools would bloom.
Teachers would be leaving the public schools in droves, being
snapped up by private schools. For a couple of years this would be
somewhat chaotic, but I believe it would settle out in about three
years. Eventually deadwood teachers would be fired by the much
more efficient private sector, leaving only the better ones. Every child,
even poor ones, would benefit from a private school education, and
the taxpayers would be saved 1/2 the cost. Students would no longer
be indoctrinated in the current PC doctrine, but be educated in the
way the parents wanted it done. Win-Win-Win. The only losers
would be those vested in a failed public education system. They
betrayed our confidence, wasted our money and trashed our
children's educations to serve their own agenda. They rightfully
deserve prison sentences for malfeasance. Losing their jobs is far
less penalty than they deserve, and mild compared to damaging the
educational opportunities for millions of children.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-09-27T08:45:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13fnctr9mcf080c@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<VLydnRiEfowsIWzbnZ2dnUVZ_saknZ2d@comcast.com> <ik55f3tfr5h3i6ui4rtb2atc3rtghpqsio@4ax.com> <uJ-dnaJYC-eXqm7bnZ2dnUVZ_uevnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd0cpt$s7c$1@reader1.panix.com> <s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com> <kgkff3977i9faffo39r1b0682u0u48pas3@4ax.com> <2ZSdnWoW6pdAimbbnZ2dnUVZ_qGknZ2d@comcast.com> <zAMKi.132$z7.35@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

>  The only losers
> would be those vested in a failed public education system. They
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> educational opportunities for millions of children.

Truely, the inmates are in charge of the asylum. Do you realize that a 
doctorate in education is the only terminal degree (aside from specialties 
like medicine or law) that does not require knowledge of a foreign language?

Several years ago, I did a bit of research. In my state - and probably 
yours - the following people are not qualified by law to teach in the pubic 
schools:

No living Nobel Laureate
No living Pulitzer Prize winner
No winner of Edgar, Newberry, or other prestigious literary prize
No sitting member of the federal judiciary
No living winner of the Fields Medal
No sitting member of the United States Senate
No living president or ex-president
No member of the Board of Directors of the Federal Reserve
etc.

They all lack the requisite "education" courses.

Does anyone doubt a retired nurse could teach high-school biology off the 
top of her head? Could a retired architect or civil engineer teach geometry 
or algebra or high-school physics? How hard would it be for a PhD in 
Chemistry to instruct in high-school?

As for the required "education" courses, remember a PhD in any science has 
twenty-years of classroom experience, plus, in most cases, one or two years 
of teaching undergraduate courses. How much MORE experience is required?

Bah!
```

```
On Thu, 27 Sep 2007 14:32:55 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>You have misrepresented what I was trying to say. No system is perfect.
>I'm saying that market forces would compel private education to be much
>better than public education. It already does so today, why would you
>think it wouldn't do so in the scenario I described above?

Such market forces exist for Universities.    But I see some private
colleges designed for people with money who are looking for an easy
degree.   Some Continuing Education programs seem designed to make
sure everybody graduates.

The question is, whether those in power want the same thing as the
people being educated.   Those in power want the best wealth producing
workers.   The students tend to want a degree and the best paying job.
These aren't the same thing.
```

```
>> The question is, whether those in power want the same thing as the
>> people being educated.   Those in power want the best wealth producing

If the state is paying the voucher, the result should be what the
state wants.    This is what produces wealth for the state.

parent wants.   This is what produces success for the family.

These aren't identical goals.
```

```
In the U.S. at least, that money doesn't belong the state, it belongs to
the citizens, from whom they took it. The government is supposed to
be using the people's money on the people's behalf. Government "for
the people," remember?

The only way my suggestion can work properly is if the parent has
complete discretion about which school receives the voucher. The whole
point is to get government out of controlling education.
```

```
> If my money is being spent on your children's schools, why shouldn't I

place, they wouldn't need to "give it back" with vouchers.  Wouldn't you

with vouchers too - at least that's a little more fair...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ (OT) Private vs. Public education - was Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-27T23:21:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Il%Ki.57$pX5.46@newsfe14.lga>`
- **References:** `<VLydnRiEfowsIWzbnZ2dnUVZ_saknZ2d@comcast.com> <ik55f3tfr5h3i6ui4rtb2atc3rtghpqsio@4ax.com> <uJ-dnaJYC-eXqm7bnZ2dnUVZ_uevnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd0cpt$s7c$1@reader1.panix.com> <s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com> <kgkff3977i9faffo39r1b0682u0u48pas3@4ax.com> <2ZSdnWoW6pdAimbbnZ2dnUVZ_qGknZ2d@comcast.com> <zAMKi.132$z7.35@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <K3RKi.63304$hP1.26210@newsfe13.lga> <vzTKi.80824$Lu.54221@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:vzTKi.80824$Lu.54221@bignews8.bellsouth.net...
>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What >>I<< was trying to point out is that private enterprise is just as
ridden with dishonesty and incompetence as is the public system.

Nor do I have any faith in market forces.  "Market forces" are actually
individual people making individual choices, not some sentient and potent
entity (I call this the fallacy of thinking with collective nouns).
Individual people as a rule don't act effectively as players in unrestricted
free enterprise circumstances: they have miniscule clout as buyers compared
with the collective power of the sellers. Unrestricted free enterprise
ALWAYS leads to monopolies (or a small group of sellers acting together as a
monopoly).  The first case turns up where different products are available:
think computers, operating systems; where they're all selling the same thing
you get a group - insurance, banks, oil companies.  I'm not aware of any
product or service for which the number of competitors in actually
increasing.

And that's the ultimate failing point of your argument.  A monopoly or
cartel providing education will set the standards and the costs leaving the
consumer no choice.  Better to have a public monopoly: then at least we know
the head office address in Washington DC (or Ottawa, Ontario) instead of the
Bahamas, or Lichtenstein, etc.

PL
```

- **References:** `<VLydnRiEfowsIWzbnZ2dnUVZ_saknZ2d@comcast.com> <ik55f3tfr5h3i6ui4rtb2atc3rtghpqsio@4ax.com> <uJ-dnaJYC-eXqm7bnZ2dnUVZ_uevnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd0cpt$s7c$1@reader1.panix.com> <s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com> <kgkff3977i9faffo39r1b0682u0u48pas3@4ax.com> <2ZSdnWoW6pdAimbbnZ2dnUVZ_qGknZ2d@comcast.com> <13fnc66smd6a0f8@corp.supernews.com> <bsSdnR8zmIah72HbnZ2dnUVZ_r7inZ2d@comcast.com>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:bsSdnR8zmIah72HbnZ2dnUVZ_r7inZ2d@comcast.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> > "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
> > news:2ZSdnWoW6pdAimbbnZ2dnUVZ_qGknZ2d@comcast.com...
> > [snip]
> >> But the idea that the "government is the solution" in the first place
is
> >> what boggles my mind.
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Well, sure, take the sentence out of context...  :)

Actually, I was adding an omitted, fundamental context,
that of rights. That "to secure rights" "government is the
solution" is fundamental to any discussion on welfarism,
in the U.S. Or, generally, if X is a right, then government
is the solution to secure that right.

>  Compare
> expenditures per student with average test scores, and even a Democrat
> should be able to see that private education is a much better investment
> than public education.

This is irrelevant to the fundamental question: Is there
a right to education? If the answer is no, then government
should have no direct involvement in education, though
it may be indirectly involved through its authority to
regulate commerce, such as, to prevent fraud. If the
answer is yes, then government involvement in education
is the solution to securing a right to education and
expenditures and test scores are merely complaints
about the particular choices made by some governments.

> >>  What has the government done well?
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Apart from law enforcement and defense, can you think of anything else?

Again, from my experience, most government regulated
or supplied public services that relate to infrastructure:
power, phone, water and sewer, trash collection, roads,
mail, emergency services, etc., seem to function well.

The part that extends into welfarism is where most of my
complaints arise, though I have no specific complaints
regarding public education, perhaps because I had no
children and, as a result of Florida's homestead exemption,
I do not pay any of the ad valorem taxes that are used to
fund public schools, locally. <g>
```

- **References:** `<VLydnRiEfowsIWzbnZ2dnUVZ_saknZ2d@comcast.com> <ik55f3tfr5h3i6ui4rtb2atc3rtghpqsio@4ax.com> <uJ-dnaJYC-eXqm7bnZ2dnUVZ_uevnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd0cpt$s7c$1@reader1.panix.com> <s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com> <kgkff3977i9faffo39r1b0682u0u48pas3@4ax.com> <2ZSdnWoW6pdAimbbnZ2dnUVZ_qGknZ2d@comcast.com> <13fnc66smd6a0f8@corp.supernews.com> <bsSdnR8zmIah72HbnZ2dnUVZ_r7inZ2d@comcast.com> <13fp168ehiorn47@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
> news:bsSdnR8zmIah72HbnZ2dnUVZ_r7inZ2d@comcast.com...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> is the solution to secure that right.

True - but, the definition of "right" has been greatly expanded.  Just 
because something is nice and improves one's quality of life does not 
make it a right.  Is there a "right" to health care, to education, to 
Internet access?  There are politicians in this country who feel that 
there is.  There are others of us that feel that they are full of crap.  :)

(Sorry for missing your point above...)

>>  Compare
>> expenditures per student with average test scores, and even a Democrat
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> about the particular choices made by some governments.

Well, I'd fall squarely in the "it's not a right" camp.  It's good to 
have, and it certainly makes life better, but it is *not* a right. 
Inherent human rights exist without taking away the rights of others.  I 
can be allowed to live without taking away the lives of others; I can 
hold my property without depriving others of holding theirs; I can 
decide where I go without restricting the mobility or freedom of others. 
  Government provides law enforcement to preserve these rights, and 
defense to ensure that another sovereign nation does not come and take 
these rights away.

However, I cannot get free health care, though, without someone else 
giving up the funding for it.  I cannot get free education without 
someone else giving up the funding for it.  Internet access is not free 
either - it takes funding to set up the infrastructure and provide the 
service to it.

In my opinion (which seems to be the same as the framers of our 
Constitution), this is the difference between a *right* and "nice-to-have."

>>>>  What has the government done well?
>>> Off-hand, prevention of anarchy comes to mind; but that
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> mail, emergency services, etc., seem to function well.

True - but all these services have improved with deregulation and 
competition.

> The part that extends into welfarism is where most of my
> complaints arise, though I have no specific complaints
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> fund public schools, locally. <g>

Well, good for you.  :)  I brought up education as an example, but there 
are plenty others where government is mucking up what they shouldn't 
have gotten into in the first place.  Why do we fund "the arts" - isn't 
the "starving" part of the "starving artist" where they get their 
inspiration?

(heh heh...  I'm hear about it now...)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-09-28T19:09:39-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<qn0rf3tmnsf1u3jeh7cqmo0ndgi9vkm8e8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<VLydnRiEfowsIWzbnZ2dnUVZ_saknZ2d@comcast.com> <ik55f3tfr5h3i6ui4rtb2atc3rtghpqsio@4ax.com> <uJ-dnaJYC-eXqm7bnZ2dnUVZ_uevnZ2d@comcast.com> <fd0cpt$s7c$1@reader1.panix.com> <s8GdnRfyUvDoOWnbnZ2dnUVZ_g-dnZ2d@comcast.com> <kgkff3977i9faffo39r1b0682u0u48pas3@4ax.com> <2ZSdnWoW6pdAimbbnZ2dnUVZ_qGknZ2d@comcast.com> <13fnc66smd6a0f8@corp.supernews.com> <bsSdnR8zmIah72HbnZ2dnUVZ_r7inZ2d@comcast.com>`

```
On Thu, 27 Sep 2007 21:17:15 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Rick Smith wrote:
>> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>than public education.

Tell that to Milwaukee taxpayers, who pay MORE per student for the 25% attending private
schools under voucher than they pay for the 75% in the public system.

Total per student expenditures for public schools are about twice that of private schools.
Public schools spend 50% of their budget on administration whereas private schools spend
only 25%. Taxpayers should be clamoring for MORE administrators in both systems, because
it would save them money. Here's why --

When public schools account for administrators, they use something called Fund Based
Accounting, which makes each Federal program a little profit center.  If the program
doesn't bring in more money than it costs, the program and its administrator get the axe.
It's little different from a private business, except the revenue source is Federal
government rather than consumers or local taxpayers. 

The Feds are paying more than half of Milwaukee's public school budget. Private schools
have much fewer opportunities to feed at the Federal trough. If public schools fired half
its administrators, putting it on par with the private system, Federal subsidies would
disappear, causing property taxes to go up about 50%. 

Don't blame the Milwaukee public school system for the seeming inefficiency, blame
Washington. Is it inefficient? Students in both systems get about the same dollar amount
in instruction (teachers' salaries). Test scores are about the same for both. There's no
evidence that private schools are cheaper or better than public. 

There is healthy competition between public and private at the university level. Neither
is a clear winner over the other. Both public and private spend  the same $20,000 per
student (vs. $8,000 for high school).  Contrary to popular belief, universities' greatest
source of funds is not the state (for public) nor tuition (for private), nor endowments.
Most of the money comes from research grants and patent royalties. For instance, Uiniv. of
Virginia, a public school, receives only 8% of its revenues from the state and 15% from
tuition and fees. It it funded 43% by patents and 16% by research.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Iraq

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-09-27T09:04:35-06:00

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>

- **In-Reply-To:** `<13f7j25t22snj10@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<wzjGi.64367$gR1.27555@fe03.news.easynews.com> <8rCdnb1u4u_fpGzbnZ2dnUVZ_oKhnZ2d@golden.net> <3jj2f3dr881q662kpb3pphn0d7uqmt0mcr@4ax.com> <X9-dnYZshvgMLGzbnZ2dnUVZ_remnZ2d@golden.net> <fcseoj$124$1@reader1.panix.com> <LJidnU64f4e7-2_bnZ2dnUVZ_hqdnZ2d@golden.net> <13f67o342ohj9d7@corp.supernews.com> <13f7j25t22snj10@news.supernews.com>`

```
HeyBub wrote:
> Probably I/O is, today, more efficient than table look-ups. The O/S has 
> buffered the last zillion reads and can probably find the desired record 
> faster than a program could in a table. With less chance of a mistake. 

I had to chuckle when I read this.  :)  Today, we sat through a 
preliminary report from a capacity planning company.  Basically, if your 
"stretch factor" is 1, there is no queuing in your system.  If it's a 2, 
there is as much queuing as their is work being done.

Our reports system has been running slow, so that's why these folks came 
out.  For 5 concurrent reports, our stretch factor is 12.  For ten, it 
was up over 100.  The bottleneck?  Two disk partitions - 98%+ of the 
queuing was on those two partitions.

I/O is not necessarily faster, depending on how it's used.  :)
```
